statement:
You have joined a new project team and are moving to an Agile way of
work. Part of this is co-locating the team to gain the benefit of
osmotic communication. Your project sponsor asks what this means. What
do you tell them?
option1: The team is able to meet more frequently when co-located.
option2: It is easier to hand off work to someone if it meets their strengths.
option3:
It is a written process made available to everyone in the team for
transparency.
option4:
The team will overhear conversations and information in the common area
that will help them solve future problems.
explanation:
Osmotic communication is the accidental overhearing of background
information that may later end up being important -- part of the benefit
to co-locating a team in Agile.

statement:
You are working with an Agile team who are not co-located. They are in
various locations around the country. As the Agile lead, how will you
get the benefit of Agile without a co-located team?
option1:
Utilise group messaging software, daily teleconference stand-ups, an
electronic Kanban and backlog.
option2:
Ask the organisation to re-locate the team to ensure everyone is in the
same place.
option3:
Hire or find new team members from within the organisation who are in
the same place so you can co-locate.
option4:
Remove all meetings so people can focus on their work without
distraction.
explanation:
It may not be feasible to move entire teams, or hire new ones. Utilising
the many electronic options so your team can still get the benefit of
frequent contact and communication is the most cost-effective approach.

statement:
You are leading an Agile team that has members in various locations
around the country. You have arranged daily stand-ups via phone, with
electronic Kanban and backlogs, but the team is not able to meet face to
face. What else will you do to increase the team working together and
getting to know each other?
option1: Have a team board with their backgrounds and pictures.
option2:
Swap roles throughout the team so each member gets a feel for what
others do.
option3:
Pair up team members to work on tasks, and rotate the pairs for each
feature.
option4:
Have a virtual lunch and learn where team members share something about
themselves.
explanation:
Pair programming is a common technique in Agile and can be used for most
tasks. Pairing up team members on a task or iteration gives them the
opportunity to talk and work with each other one-on-one

statement:
You are showing the project sponsor around the team area for your Agile
project and see the burndown chart next to the team Kanban board. The
project sponsor asks what the chart tells you. What will you say?
option1: It shows how many team members are moving on and off the project.
option2:
It shows the remaining tasks versus what was planned for a given
iteration.
option3: It shows the automated tests run as part of continuous integration.
option4: It shows the return on investment for the delivered features.
explanation:
A burndown chart is a line chart showing the iteration tasks completed
versus what was planned over the iteration. The same idea can be used
for features over the life of a project, or even risks with the project
itself, and can be measured in any way you choose -- by hours, tasks,
cards or stories completed and more.

statement:
You are working through the feature backlog with the functional manager
of the organisation, who is the product owner for your Agile team. They
ask about the velocity chart that is hanging in the team area. What do
you tell them it is?
option1:
The number of points, tasks or features completed by the team in each
given iteration.
option2:
The number of team members working on the Agile project over the life of
the project.
option3: How fast each team member completes their work each day.
option4: The speed with which the project budget is being used up.
explanation:
Team velocity is the amount of work a team can complete in a given
iteration, whether it's measured by points, hours, story cards, tasks,
or features themselves. A velocity chart therefore shows the number of
whatever unit of measure you choose per iteration.

statement:
In 2001, a group of individuals, representing the most widely used
lightweight software development methodologies, agreed on a common set
of values and principles which became known as the Agile Manifesto.
Which of the following is one of the Agile Manifesto\'s core values?
option1: Developer negotiation over feature collaboration.
option2: Product owner interaction over Scrum Master delegation.
option3: Customer collaboration over contract negotiation.
option4: Getting things right over getting the right things.
explanation:
The Agile manifesto is made up of four core values, one of which values
Customer collaboration over contract negotiation.

statement:
You are working with your project team in a way that ensures you Analyse
the requirements, then Design, Build and Test for those requirements,
repeating them until the product is correct, and adjusting your product
or approach as necessary before releasing in one large feature. Which
project lifecycle are you using?
option1: A Predictive project lifecycle approach.
option2: An Agile project lifecycle approach
option3: An Incremental project lifecycle approach
option4: An Iterative project lifecycle approach
explanation:
The iterative project lifecycle Analyses, Designs, Builds, Tests, and
repeats these activities until it is correct, then delivers in one
release. Incremental delivers small increments of value (i.e. features),
Agile is a combination of Iterative and Incremental, and Predictive does
one release only with little or no iterative feedback.

statement:
You are working on a project with clear procedures based on past
projects. The product is clear and the risks are fairly well known as
the organisation has done this before. Which is true from the below?
option1: It is definable work, you can choose a predictive project lifecycle.
option2: It is definable work, you can choose an Agile project lifecycle.
option3:
It is high-uncertainty work, you can choose an incremental project
lifecycle.
option4:
It is high-uncertainty work, you can choose an iterative project
lifecycle.
explanation:
When the situation is fairly definable and well known, and the risks are
also fairly well known, you can choose a predictive project lifecycle
that plans most of the project up-front and delivers in one release.
Agile, Incremental and Iterative lifecycles are used for high
uncertainty work.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager leading a project team who is used to a
Waterfall way of work. The project sponsor wants to plan the entire
project up front so they can see when the project will be delivered. You
tell them that Agile uses adaptive planning. Why might this be a better
alternative?
option1:
Adaptive planning allows the project sponsor to adapt to the project
plan over time.
option2:
Adaptive planning allows the team to deliver usable features while
continually reprioritising next pieces based on business value.
option3:
Adaptive planning ensures that the users can adapt to the product
delivered by the project team.
option4: Adaptive planning is stable after the prototype is complete.
explanation:
Adaptive planning allows the project sponsor to receive incremental
value delivered as features, and for those features to be re-prioritised
by the product owner throughout the life of the project. As the saying
goes, many waterfall projects start out as predictive planned, and end
up as adaptive planned (when schedule and cost projections are not met
and they need to adjust).

statement:
You are the Scrum Master and about to begin a new iteration in your
Agile project. New features have been added to the backlog, and the team
are unsure of which of those features to work on next. How will you
guide the product owner and the team when analysing next priority?
option1:
Analyse the value of each feature with the product owner, versus the
effort and risk of each item with the Agile team.
option2: Focus on the shortest tasks first so you can deliver value quickly.
option3:
Choose to deliver the entire project at the end so the project sponsor
can see it all together.
option4: By asking the customers what they want delivered first.
explanation:
While value is typically determined by features that customers want, the
priority is ultimately decided by the product owner, who represents the
customer or the business and constantly refines and prioritises the
backlog of features. Just as important is developing high risk items,
and considering the development effort versus the benefit.

statement:
You are working through the backlog with the product owner and
estimating the story cards with the development team. The team are
unsure if their estimates are accurate enough. Some of the team have
worked on similar projects in the past. What will you recommend they
use?
option1:
Analogous estimating, using actual effort from a similar feature on a
different project.
option2:
T-shirt sizing, using broad Small, Medium or Large sizes to accommodate
variation.
option3:
Three point estimating, where the team gives the average of an
optimistic, pessimistic and most likely estimate.
option4:
The product owner should estimate the story cards as they are getting
the benefit of the delivered features.
explanation:
While most estimation methods will do, the best one in this case is
Analogous estimating, using a similar example from a previous project.
Agile estimating is most often done by breaking down the features into
small tasks, then estimating on those tasks (bottom-up). Where that
doesn't work, Analogous estimating will fill in the blanks.

statement:
While on an Agile project team, you prioritise the backlog of features
with the product owner, and agree what you will work on first. Your
project sponsor wants to know how long it will take to deliver some
value. How will you estimate how long each of these features will take
to complete?
option1:
Break down the feature into tasks or user stories, then estimate how
long those tasks will take to complete with the dev team.
option2:
Take the total number of people in the team, multiplied by the hours
worked, divided by the number of features in the backlog.
option3:
Ask the sponsor how many iterations the project is funded for, then plan
to complete the features by the end of that time.
option4:
Find a similar feature delivered by another part of the organisation,
and use that as your estimate.
explanation:
Agile favours a bottom-up approach to estimating, where the work is
broken down to its lowest level, typically a user story, and estimated
by the development team. This will give you an estimate of the effort
and time required to complete the feature.

statement:
You are working with your Agile team on your first iteration. The
feature has been chosen by the product owner and the team breaks it down
into user stories to work on. What is a user story?
option1:
A piece of functionality small enough to be completed in one to three
days.
option2: A description of a group of features from the viewpoint of the customer.
option3:
A story about the end-user so the team understands the feature
completely.
option4: A prioritised backlog that the team can choose features from.
explanation:
A user story is the smallest unit of work in an Agile team -- a task
that can be completed in no more than three days. If a task will take
longer, it is common to try and break it into two or more pieces to
accommodate the user story rule. Each single User Story is ideally
written from the Customer\'s viewpoint, often in the form of \"As
a\...\" \"I want\...\" \"So I can\...\".

statement:
You have begun a new project in a new organisation, and the project team
mentions you are working in an iterative project environment. How do you
know you are working within an iterative project life cycle?
option1: The team iterates around one idea to make sure that idea is right.
option2:
You are improving the product or result through successive prototypes or
proofs of concept.
option3: The project sponsor says that the project lifecycle is iterative.
option4: You are using Agile sprints or iterations to manage your work.
explanation:
An Iterative project lifecycle uses successive prototypes or proofs of
concept to gather feedback, then release the product in one go.

statement:
In 2001, a group of individuals, representing the most widely used
lightweight software development methodologies, agreed on a common set
of values and principles which became known as the Agile Manifesto.
Which of the following is one of the Agile Manifesto\'s core values?
option1: Developing as much as possible to see what gets the best result.
option2: Finding a product owner over finding a development team.
option3: Cross functional team members over executive buy-in.
option4: Responding to change over following a plan.
explanation:
The Agile manifesto is made up of four core values, one of which values
"Responding to change over following a plan".

statement:
You are working on a project where the organisation wants to move
towards an Agile way of work. Which two project lifecycles combine to
create an Agile environment?
option1: Iterative and Predictive lifecycles.
option2: Incremental and Predictive lifecycles.
option3: Iterative and Incremental lifecycles.
option4: Predictive and Agile lifecycles.
explanation:
An Agile project environment is created by combining Incremental project
approaches, where smaller features are released more regularly, and
iterative project approaches, where the product improves through
iterations and is released after the feedback.

statement:
You have come on board a new project team and have been advised that the
project works with a predictive style project lifecycle. How will you
know you are working in a \"Predictive\" or Waterfall style approach?
option1:
The bulk of planning is done up front, then executed in a single,
sequential process.
option2:
The bulk of the planning is done up front, then prototypes are created
to gather feedback before releasing in one go.
option3:
A product roadmap is created and smaller features are designed and
released as part of the whole.
option4:
The project uses feature sprints, Kanban boards and retrospectives to
manage their work.
explanation:
A predictive or waterfall project lifecycle is defined by doing all the
planning up front, and executing in a sequential process. It is ideal
for definable work or products where the risk is well known.

statement:
You are working as a project manager in an Agile team. Which of the
below are NOT examples of execution practices you will see in Agile?
option1:
Test at all levels, where the team applies end-to-end testing and unit
testing (of an individual story or feature). Agile teams prefer
automated tests.
option2:
Test-Driven Development, where the team writes automated tests before
writing the product code to help mistake-proof the product.
option3:
Spikes, which are time-boxed research or experiments and useful for
estimation, acceptance criteria definition, or learning some critical
technical or functional element.
option4:
Project management plan, where the project is planned is detail up front
to avoid misunderstanding by project stakeholders.
explanation:
A project management plan is a key artefact in a predictive or waterfall
project approach. Test at all levels, Test driven development and Spikes
are all Agile practices you will see.

statement:
The product owner has worked with the development team to list all work
to be done, write up user stories for each feature and place them in the
product backlog. What will you do next?
option1: Refine the backlog to ensure only important items are being worked on.
option2:
Create the schedule management plan to ensure you know when each item
can be delivered.
option3:
Sort the items by business priority, place them in an iteration,
complete the items then remove them from the backlog.
option4:
Move the items into the requirements traceability matrix so you can
report on their progress.
explanation:
Managing the project requirements via the product backlog is a
fundamental Agile practice. The product backlog is the source of truth,
and is continuously re-prioritised as the work gets done to ensure
maximum value is delivered first.

statement:
The business partner or product owner continuously refines, or grooms,
the product backlog. This includes adding new stories, reprioritizing
stories, and removing stories when needed. What role does the
development team take during this process?
option1: Developing the cards as soon as they are added to ensure maximum value.
option2: Removing cards from the backlog that do not make sense to complete.
option3:
Estimating the work needed for each card so the product owner can
prioritise based on value and effort.
option4:
Coming to daily stand-ups to report on what was done yesterday and what
will be done today.
explanation:
During backlog grooming the development team is responsible for sizing
or estimating the work. This allows the product owner, business
representative or customer to prioritise effectively, based on value and
time to delivery.

statement:
You are working with your Agile project team to estimate each task and
how long they might take. The team is finding it difficult to give an
exact figure, and worries that it might impact project delivery. What
can you show them, to estimate more accurately?
option1:
Ask them to estimate by whole iteration to give a little room for
variation.
option2: Estimate the cards in full days instead of effort per card.
option3:
Use comparative estimates or relative sizing such as T-shirt sizing or
story points.
option4: Prioritise the backlog based on customer value.
explanation:
A common Agile practice is to rely on relative sizing such as story
points or "T-Shirt sizing." Team velocity is then measured using those
same methods as the team completes a certain amount of points per
iteration. This is easier than trying to predict an exact time period.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager and talking through the iteration with
the product owner. They have created the list of items in the product
backlog, but there is confusion in the team about who should perform the
process of story card sizing. What will you tell them?
option1: The Product Owner works through the backlog to size the story cards.
option2:
The Agile team work together to size the story cards, relying mostly on
information from the developers and testers.
option3:
The Agile team leader or scrum master works to size the story cards for
the team.
option4: The customer sizes the story cards to ensure maximum value.
explanation:
The story point sizing process is performed by the Agile team, primarily
estimated by developers and testers who will be doing the work.

statement:
You have been estimating story cards with your Agile project team, but
the effort captured has not been matching the time it ends up taking to
complete. What is the most likely reason for this occurring?
option1: The team have not included sick days in the estimate.
option2: The team have not included testing or refactoring in the estimate.
option3:
The team have not included time to merge the code for continuous
integration.
option4: The team have not included the time it takes for the daily stand-up.
explanation:
Estimates for story cards should include all known activities required
to complete that card, including testing and refactoring, complexity and
risk.

statement:
You are an Agile team leader and talking through the process for your
first retrospective. The team is curious as to what they should do. What
is the way a team retrospective would normally work?
option1:
Discuss what was successful, what could be improved, and incorporate
those into future iterations.
option2: Discuss what the team liked about working with each other.
option3: Discuss what went wrong, and make sure the team knows who is to blame.
option4: Discuss the validity of the features being developed.
explanation:
A team retrospective enables the team to answer questions that improve
their team process and way of work, such as "What went well," "What
could be improved," and "What still puzzles me."

statement:
The 12th principle of Agile is "At regular intervals, the team reflects
on how to become more effective, then tunes and adjusts its behaviour
accordingly." In practice, this is a Retrospective. But what is the
practical reason for including Retrospectives in an Agile team?
option1:
It reduces the communication time between the project sponsor and the
developers.
option2: It ensures features are released on schedule and on budget.
option3: It helps the team learn about and improve their process over time.
option4:
It keeps the team focused on the main feature to deliver the most
business value.
explanation:
Agile retrospectives help the team learn about their process and improve
it over time, through regular and structured feedback given and used as
a team.

statement:
Retrospectives are typically held at the end of an iteration, but an
Agile team can also hold retrospectives at any other time. What is NOT a
time that an Agile team might hold a retrospective?
option1: When the team completes or ships something new.
option2: When more than a few weeks have passed since the previous retrospective.
option3: When the team appears to be stuck and completed work is not flowing.
option4: When the feature backlog has not been created and work is yet to begin.
explanation:
A team retrospective is there to gather feedback and improve on the team
process once that process is in place. It iterates towards improvement.
It is typically not held before the work or process has begun, as there
is nothing to gather feedback on yet.

statement:
You are in a new project team and working through which project
lifecycle or way of work to use in order to deliver the products to your
customer, and how far to modify it. What are some of the project factors
that will influence your tailoring decisions to use Agile or other
methods?
option1: Which project lifecycle the customer wants to use.
option2:
What the latest lifecycle is -- the latest one must be the best and will
be most interesting to upper management.
option3:
Whether the flow of work is often interrupted by various delays or
impediments, and is easily definable or highly uncertain.
option4:
Project approaches should never be tailored - we should follow one
approach to the letter so we make no mistakes.
explanation:
Project lifecycles and how much you tailor them depend on many factors.
Having definable work or high uncertainty work are two common factors
that move you toward more Predictive or Incremental workflows, as is the
team's existing experience.

statement:
You are in the forming stages of a new project team, and deciding on the
way of work to use when operating as a team. What are some of the
factors that will influence how you tailor the project lifecycle?
option1: The demand pattern for value or delivery: is it steady or sporadic?
option2: The rate of process improvement required by the team.
option3: Is more than one team is needed to build the product?
option4: All of the above.
explanation:
Project lifecycles or ways of working can be influenced by many factors
-- in fact almost everything will impact this decision. The way the
organisation works, whether the work is certain or uncertain, whether
the features are subject to change, what has worked before and what the
team is comfortable with all play a part.

statement:
You are working through the backlog with the product owner and
estimating the story cards with the development team. Some of the team
have worked on similar features in the past. The team are unsure if
their estimates are accurate enough, as many of the team are new to the
project. What will you recommend they use?
option1:
Analogous estimating, using actual effort from a similar feature on a
different project.
option2:
T-shirt sizing, using broad Small, Medium or Large sizes to accommodate
variation.
option3:
Three point estimating, where the team gives the average of an
optimistic, pessimistic and most likely estimate.
option4:
The product owner should estimate the story cards as they are getting
the benefit of the delivered features.
explanation:
While most estimation methods will do, the best one in this case is
Analogous estimating, using a similar example from a previous project.
Agile estimating is most often done by breaking down the features into
small tasks, then estimating on those tasks (bottom-up). Where that
doesn't work, Analogous estimating will fill in the blanks.

statement:
You are delivering features through your Agile project and the project
sponsor and product owner are happy with the progress. Your project
sponsor raises a concern about future items and what that development
looks like. What will you show them?
option1:
The Project Management Plan, that has outlined the project delivery in
full.
option2:
The product backlog, that has the list of features the team will
deliver.
option3:
The product roadmap, that shows planned future features and their
approximate dates, similarly to a Gantt Chart.
option4:
The team velocity chart, that shows how much the team completes each
iteration.
explanation:
A product roadmap is a visual display of the product releases, similar
to a Gantt Chart or project schedule but based around Agile feature
releases. It is a high level view and revised as necessary.

statement:
Your Agile team is estimating story cards using planning poker. For one
of the larger features, some of the team has estimated three points,
while the others have estimated eight points. How will you guide the
team to come to an estimate?
option1: Ask them to average out the points and assign that value to the card.
option2: Change the method of estimating to accommodate the team decision.
option3: Change the feature to make it easier to estimate on.
option4:
Ask the high and low estimates to discuss their reasons, and the team to
estimate again until a consensus is reached or more information is
needed.
explanation:
In Planning Poker, if estimates are different, the high and low
estimators share their reasons. Once shared, the team estimates again.
The poker planning process is repeated until consensus is achieved or
until the team decides to gather more information.

statement:
The Agile team is working through a particularly complex feature that is
difficult for the team find a solution for. As the Agile lead, you
recommend a spike. What is a spike?
option1: A shifting of the feature into another iteration.
option2: A time-boxed task to explore or investigate an issue.
option3:
A short meeting at the beginning of the day to discuss tasks in
progress.
option4: A demonstration of the feature to the product owner.
explanation:
A spike is a time-boxed task to explore or investigate an issue. It is
separate from development work and only used if something needs to be
figured out or investigated. It can be done at any time during a
project.

statement:
The Agile manifesto has twelve clarifying principles added to it to help
projects and teams understand the Agile values. Which of the below is a
correct clarifying principle?
option1: Tailor the project approach to suit the project and the team.
option2: Ensure executive buy-in by reporting frequently to your managers.
option3:
Deliver working software frequently, from a couple of weeks to a couple
of months, with a preference to the shorter timescale.
option4:
Ensure complete planning for your product and release once tested
thoroughly.
explanation:
"Deliver working software frequently, from a couple of weeks to a couple
of months, with a preference to the shorter timescale," is one of the
twelve Agile clarifying principles.

statement:
The Agile manifesto has twelve clarifying principles added to it to help
projects and teams understand the Agile values. Which of the below is a
correct clarifying principle?
option1:
Business people should dictate to the development team what to do and
how to do it.
option2:
Developers can code and release products at a schedule that suits them,
for complete autonomy.
option3:
Business people and developers mustn\'t work together, but they can chat
about their work over lunch.
option4:
Business people and developers must work together daily throughout the
project.
explanation:
As you will know form the various Agile approaches, "Business people and
developers must work together daily throughout the project" is one of
the twelve Agile clarifying principles.

statement:
The Agile manifesto has twelve clarifying principles added to it to help
projects and teams understand the Agile values. Which of the below is a
correct clarifying principle?
option1:
Build projects around a core idea, then brainstorm frequently to get the
job done.
option2:
Find the right executive support and ensure they tell you the product
requirements.
option3:
Deliver frequently whether an item has been tested or not, as quality is
everyone's responsibility.
option4:
Build projects around motivated individuals. Give them the environment
and support they need and trust them to get the job done.
explanation:
Agile is about building projects around motivated individuals -- the
cross functional and "T-shaped" team members, giving them the support
needed by removing blockers and ensuring a flow of work.

statement:
The twelve Agile clarifying principles also match up with the best
advice for projects, leadership and communication. Which of the below is
a correct clarifying principle when it comes to project communication?
option1:
The most efficient and effective method of conveying information to and
within a development team is face to face conversation.
option2:
Communication is always done in writing to ensure you have ongoing proof
the communication occurred.
option3:
Agile teams ensure downward communication only so the executive in
charge directs the product features and work.
option4:
When communicating ensure to use various forms such as telephone,
writing, face-to-face, and ensure variety.
explanation:
An Agile team knows that communicating close in person, place and time
is the most efficient and effective way -- often in a co-located team
area so other members can pick up information by osmosis.

statement:
The Agile manifesto has twelve clarifying principles added to it to help
projects and teams understand the Agile values. Which of the below is a
correct clarifying principle?
option1: Union attendance is the best measure of team happiness.
option2: Working software is the primary measure of progress.
option3: Finding risks is the only way to avoid failure.
option4: Learning new things keeps a team engaged.
explanation: In an Agile team, working software is the primary measure of progress.

statement:
You are new to your Agile project, and the team are working through the
forming stages. How do you help the team improve as the project
progresses?
option1:
Ensure the team holds a project post-mortem and captures the lessons
learned at the end of the project.
option2:
Hold a retrospective with the whole team at the end of each iteration
and build those lessons learned into each new iteration.
option3:
Ask the product owner to show the team where they can improve to deliver
the features they want.
option4: Rotate tasks and roles amongst the team during each new iteration.
explanation:
Agile uses retrospectives at the end of each iteration with the whole
team, to ask "What went well?" "What did not go well?" What did I
learn?" and "What still puzzles me?" The lessons from this session are
actioned and built back into future iterations to improve quickly.

statement:
You are the Agile project manager working through the product
requirements for the product owner and executive project sponsor. The
project sponsor raises concerns that the development team do not fully
understand the requirements. What will you do next?
option1:
Show them the project management plan, which will have the scope
statement listed in the scope baseline.
option2:
Ask the team to create a high-level prototype of the requirements, then
discuss and review with all stakeholders.
option3:
Show them the project schedule, outlining each feature and their
delivery dates.
option4: Hold a retrospective with the team to gather feedback and improve.
explanation:
Agile uses fast visual mock-ups or prototypes such as storyboards,
wireframes, or models, to get a quick understanding of the requirements
and consensus from all stakeholders.

statement:
The scrum master on your Agile project has asked you for the total lead
time of a particular feature. You add up the cycle time of all the cards
that contributed to that feature. What other two factors must you take
into consideration when calculating total lead time?
option1: Time for testing the code and time for merging the code.
option2: Meeting times and lunch times
option3: Value-added time and non-value added time
option4: Testing and documenting
explanation:
Lead time is a concept from Lean and the Toyota Production System.
Whenever you are calculating Cycle time (smaller items such as
processes, story cards, or an iteration in a larger project) or Lead
time (larger items such as features or the project itself) you must take
into consideration both value-added time (that directly contributes to
the customer outcome) and non-value-added time (that does not directly
contribute to the customer outcome).

statement:
When working in an Agile team it is common to use prototypes,
demonstrations and reviews, wireframes and storyboards to elicit
feedback from the customer or product owner. What is the benefit of
using these tools?
option1: So the development team don't have to create the real thing.
option2: So the team can get together and have another meeting.
option3:
It allows users to create new forms that will be required for the new
system.
option4:
It allows the product owner to discuss and agree on requirements with
their stakeholders.
explanation:
Prototypes, wireframes and storyboards are important ways to gather
feedback, and that feedback allows all stakeholders to discuss and agree
on the requirements and product features before money is spent on actual
development.

statement:
You are finishing up the first iteration on your Agile project and have
set a retrospective meeting for the whole team. One member of the team
asks why we have this meeting and if they really need to go. What will
you tell them?
option1: It helps the team prioritise product features for the next iteration.
option2: It allows the team to blame others when things go wrong.
option3:
It's an opportunity for the whole team to look at their way of work and
improve.
option4:
It showcases a part of the product so everyone can see what was
delivered.
explanation:
Retrospectives are a set meeting at the end of each sprint that actively
ask for feedback with certain questions: What went well, what didn't go
well, what still puzzles me? The actions from these questions are built
back into the team in future iterations.

statement:
You have been completing small, usable increments of value as part of
your Agile team. When reporting back to the project sponsor of this
project, the last iteration is referred to as a "fast failure." What
does this mean?
option1: The iteration did not complete all the story cards in the allotted time.
option2: The project team found a bug in the code before delivery.
option3:
The proof-of-concept for that iteration did not have the intended
outcome.
option4: The product backlog was not groomed in time to start the next iteration.
explanation:
Agile operates on the concept of delivering increments of value. Fast
failure means that particular feature or proof-of-concept effort was not
successful. By failing fast and in small increments, the project team is
able to learn and adjust quickly and at a much lower cost.

statement:
The organisation you are working in has decided to move towards an Agile
way of work and project lifecycle approach. Which of the below is how
you would describe an Agile project lifecycle?
option1:
Both iterative and incremental, to refine work items and deliver
frequently.
option2:
Both iterative and predictive, to refine work items and deliver in one
go.
option3: Both fast and furious, designed to deliver the most bang for your buck.
option4:
Both predictive and incremental, to plan carefully and deliver smaller
features.
explanation:
The Agile project lifecycle is a combination of Iterative (where we
gather feedback to improve) and Incremental project lifecycles (where we
deliver smaller usable features of value to our customer).

statement:
There are four main lifecycle approaches to managing a project, with
variations in between. What is one of the characteristics of an
\"Iterative\" lifecycle approach?
option1:
It does not need feedback for work in progress to reduce that work in
progress.
option2: It allows feedback for unfinished work to improve and modify that work.
option3: It focuses on delivery, not feedback.
option4: It plans the project up front and delivers in one go.
explanation:
An Iterative project lifecycle "iterates" towards success. It uses
prototypes and unreleased versions of the product to gather feedback and
improve before releasing it.

statement:
You are working through a sprint planning meeting with your team. The
team have sized up the cards and prioritised the backlog. What will you
do next to determine how much work can be done during this iteration?
option1:
Count up the user stories that the product owner wants completed by the
end of the iteration.
option2:
Check the team's velocity against the number of points for the story
cards you want to complete.
option3:
Ask the Scrum Master to allocate work to each team member in the fairest
manner possible.
option4:
Look at other teams and what they did in the last iteration to give the
best idea for your team.
explanation:
Velocity is the amount of work (measured by points, hours, cards or
anything you wish) a team completes on average in a given sprint. By
checking the total points for the cards you wish to complete against
what is possible via the team velocity, you will know what is possible
and can adjust as necessary.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager working on the next feature of your
product. The product owner has asked you for an estimate on how long the
remainder of the features may take to complete. You look to the team's
velocity chart. Why is this?
option1:
The team's average velocity across previous iterations is the best
predictor of future iterations.
option2: The velocity ensures the team will never work harder than they have to.
option3:
The scrum master has the final say over team velocity to ensure the work
gets done.
option4:
The velocity chart shows how quickly new items are picked up by the
team.
explanation:
The average velocity, or work completed during each iteration, is the
best predictor of future velocity, because previous iterations typically
include all tasks and pitfalls that may impact their work.

statement:
You are the Agile lead or Scrum Master for your Agile team and planning
for the next iteration is about to begin. What will you do first?
option1: Break down the features into user stories.
option2: Write the story card acceptance criteria with the team.
option3: Choose which stories the team will work on in the iteration.
option4:
Work with the product owner to prioritises the highest value ones to
work on.
explanation:
During iteration planning the team should have already broken down
features into story cards. Now is the time to reprioritise the cards
that can be done based on the business value and the team velocity (what
can be completed).

statement:
You are in the middle of a sprint with your Agile team, and moving the
project forward with daily stand-up meetings. What is a stand-up
meeting?
option1:
A project kick-off meeting where stakeholders must stand so everyone is
alert.
option2:
A project progress meeting where you see how proposed schedule stands up
against the actual schedule.
option3:
A very quick meeting time-boxed at 15 minutes or less, where the team
say what they have accomplished and report any blockers.
option4: A team training meeting focused on continuous improvement of the team.
explanation:
The daily stand-up is a short meeting, usually around the Kanban board,
where the team say what they accomplished since yesterday, what they
will do today, and anything that is blocking their progress.

statement:
You are the Agile project manager working on a software project, and the
development team have identified a major risk for the delivery of a
certain feature. That feature is not scheduled until very late in the
project. What will you do next?
option1:
Wait until later in the project, as the problem may be fixed by other
features going in.
option2: Ask the developers to find a way to proceed without that feature.
option3:
Plan a spike around the risk, identify a solution then prioritise that
feature earlier in development.
option4:
Remove all other story cards from the current sprint so the team can
swarm around the issue and fix it.
explanation:
Agile risks are prioritised early in development, to reduce and remove
those risks. Discovery work is typically done with a spike card, but
does not need to be done by the whole team. Allocate a certain amount of
time to it, then prioritise the risk early so it is taken care of and
you know what you are dealing with.

statement:
Your Agile project team is using Scrum to manage the work and
deliverables for your project. Which of the below is not one of the
Scrum events or artefacts?
option1:
Product Backlog, where the product owner manages a prioritized list of
planned product items which evolves from sprint to sprint.
option2:
Sprint Backlog, which are the items selected in Sprint Planning for the
upcoming sprint.
option3:
Increments, or Features, which are all the Product Backlog items
completed during a Sprint and a step toward the main vision.
option4:
Team Area, which is where the co-located team sits for fast
collaboration.
explanation:
Scrum works with a Product or Feature Backlog, a Sprint Backlog, and
delivers in increments or features. Agile teams are typically
recommended to work in a co-located space to ensure fast collaboration,
however this is not one of the events or artefacts or Scrum itself.

statement:
Your Agile team has been working with Scrum for some time now and have
become quite familiar with its events and ceremonies. You are asked to
explain this to other stakeholders within the organisation. What is NOT
an example of a Scrum event or ceremony?
option1:
Sprint Planning, where the Scrum team selects the highest priority
items.
option2:
Daily Scrum, where the team meets to walk through project tasks, often
on a Kanban board.
option3:
Sprint Review, where the development team gives a demo on the product to
the product owner for sign-off or change.
option4:
Change Control Board, where all proposed changes are reviewed before
adding to the backlog.
explanation:
The Change Control Board is a typical part of a predictive or Waterfall
project lifecycle, not an Agile or Scrum way of work. It has key
stakeholders review any changes to the pre-assigned scope, schedule or
cost, and approve or decline as the project works towards completion.

statement:
Scrum is a single-team framework for managing product development. There
are certain events, ceremonies and roles and responsibilities within a
Scrum team. Which of the below is NOT a role or responsibility in a
Scrum team?
option1:
Product Owner, representing the customer and responsible for maximising
the value of the product.
option2:
Development Team, developing and testing the product, cross functional,
self-organising with all the roles needed to deliver the product.
option3:
Scrum Master, ensuring the Scrum processes such as stand-ups and
retrospectives, coaching the team and removing blockers.
option4:
Risk Manager, capturing and reviewing risks and ensuring each one has an
assigned owner.
explanation:
Risk and Quality are the responsibility of the whole team in an Agile
way of work, and so typically a single person is not responsible for it.
The product owner ensures the right features are delivered, the
development team ensures the code is refactored regularly, the Scrum
Master ensures the team process is improved over time, and the Quality
tester ensures features are free of defects.

statement:
Servant leadership is one of the key foundations to an Agile team, where
leadership and team participation and openness are encouraged. Which of
the below is NOT one of the characteristics of a servant leader?
option1: Promoting self-awareness and helping people grow.
option2: Listening to others more than speaking yourself.
option3: Serving those on the team by removing blockers.
option4: Reporting on low-performers to ensure the team is streamlined.
explanation:
Agile and Servant Leadership work by building towards psychological
safety, openness, growth and ensuring work is free from blockers. It
starts with the Scrum Master or Agile coach/leader role, but expands to
the whole team.

statement:
Your team are about to proceed with the development of a new product for
the organisation you work in. Depending on the product, stakeholders,
risk appetite, need for value and more, the lifecycle or way of work
will change. Which of the below represents an \"Incremental\" lifecycle
approach?
option1:
It provides unfinished deliverables that the customer can review for
feedback.
option2:
It provides value in one release after thorough risk, quality, schedule
and scope planning.
option3:
It provides multiple finished deliverables that the customer can use
immediately.
option4:
It provides a way for the team to do less with more, by releasing only a
small feature.
explanation:
An Incremental project lifecycle or way of work delivers small
increments that a customer can use immediately, gain value from, and
provide feedback on.

statement:
You are working with your Agile team to size the story cards and place
them in upcoming iterations. You notice that the points allocated to
story cards are getting bigger and bigger with each release. Why might
this be happening?
option1: The product owner keeps adding scope to the project
option2: Technical debt is being built on from previous releases
option3: Team members are not working their full allotted hours
option4: Team members are working too hard and being burnt out
explanation:
Agile accommodates for changing scope, and should find a regular rhythm
of velocity, ruling out A and D. The best answer is technical debt,
where code may be rushed to production without being simplified or
refactored. With each subsequent release it creates more work to build
on and work around disorganised code.

statement:
The project sponsor of your Agile team is quite familiar with Lean and
Agile terms and methods. She has asked you for the average Cycle time
and average Lead time for the project so far. What will you give her?
option1:
The average time for team leave, and the average time for team
attendance.
option2:
The average time to complete stand-ups, and the average time to complete
retrospectives.
option3:
The average time to complete story cards, and the average time to
complete features.
option4: The average team downtime, and the average time to complete releases.
explanation:
Cycle time is the time for shorter tasks -- most often story cards in an
Agile environment. Those story cards make up a feature. Lead time
measures the time to complete a feature (multiple story cards) from
beginning to end.

statement:
You are going over the velocity of your Agile team and notice it has
increased over the last two iterations. What might be the reason for
this?
option1: The team removed some of the technical debt.
option2:
The team no longer tests each card because they already have a good
understanding of it.
option3: The team estimated the story cards more accurately.
option4: The product owner removed some features from the backlog of work.
explanation:
Velocity and throughput are similar concepts. Typically velocity is the
work completed by the entire team. Technical Debt is messy, hacky or
bloated solutions that might work initially but can often slow down
future progress. Cleaning some of this up by \"Refactoring\" the code
can improve the team\'s velocity. Better estimation might increase or
decrease Velocity, but will make it more stable.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager working with your Agile team and
talking through the defects raised in the project so far. To get a more
accurate view of effort per card, you ask the quality tester to begin
tracking the defect cycle time. What will they report back on?
option1: How many defects were discovered in that iteration.
option2:
How long it took to fix a defect from the time it was found to the time
it was fixed.
option3: How many cycles it takes to raise a defect for the developers.
option4: The number of features in the backlog that have experienced defects.
explanation:
Cycle time is the measure of time of a smaller increment, often a story
card, from start to finish. Therefore, defect cycle time is the
measurement of how long it took to fix a single defect once it was
discovered.

statement:
You are checking in with the quality tester in your Agile team, and they
advise you that there are a number of escaped defects in the most
recently completed feature. What are they referring to?
option1: A feature that was not completed by the end of the iteration.
option2: Code that needs to be refactored before the next iteration can begin.
option3: Defects that have made it through testing and quality assurance.
option4: Team members who did not attend the daily stand-up this iteration.
explanation:
Defects that make it through testing and into a release are called
escaped defects. The cost and risk increases for defects the closer they
are to a live environment, making these the most expensive type of
defect to fix.

statement:
Agile approaches emphasise Servant Leadership to empower teams. Servant
leadership is the practice of leading through service to the team,
understanding and addressing the needs and development of the team
members to enable the highest performance possible. Servant leaders
approach project work in this order:
option1: Purpose, People, Process.
option2: People, Systems, Technology.
option3: Information, Reporting, Process.
option4: Plant, People, Machine.
explanation:
The Servant Leader approaches work starting with the purpose or "why"
behind the work, then works with growing and developing people, and
finally ensures a solid process for making the work happen.

statement:
You have been part of an Agile team for some time, working on the
aspects of servant leadership to improve the results of your team. What
is NOT one of those characteristics of a servant leader?
option1: Coaching versus controlling.
option2: Promoting safety, respect, and trust.
option3: Promoting the energy and intelligence of others.
option4: Ensuring the team does what you say.
explanation:
The servant leader in Agile coaches the team, promoting psychological
safety and trust, and promoting the energy and intelligence of others.
The approach does not include directive leadership, or forcing others to
do what you say.

statement:
The Twelve Clarifying Principles in Agile were designed to give more
clarity to the Agile manifesto core values. Which of the following is
one of the twelve clarifying principles?
option1:
Our highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and
continuous delivery of valuable software.
option2:
Our highest priority is to ensure executive buy-in by delivering high
value items.
option3:
Our highest priority is to reduce the impact of change by keeping
information to a minimum.
option4: Our highest priority is to plan thoroughly and execute with discipline.
explanation:
Agile focuses on early and continuous delivery of value -- most often in
a software environment but also in any knowledge work, such as design.

statement:
You have built an experienced Agile team and have delivered constantly
for your organisation. The product owner who has been with you since the
beginning has taken another job, and a new team mate joins the team --
one with very little experience. Which of the below will you NOT tell
them regarding the Product Owner role?
option1:
The Product Owner represents the customer and generates, maintains, and
prioritizes the product backlog to ensure the highest business value
without creating waste.
option2:
The Product Owner must have previous experience in Agile to work in the
team.
option3:
The Product Owner may work with stakeholders, customers, and the teams
to define the product direction, and rank the work based on its business
value.
option4:
Product owners typically have a business background and bring deep
subject matter expertise to the decisions.
explanation:
The product owner role represents the customer or the area gaining the
benefit of the value delivered by the Agile team. Because of this, they
often have deep knowledge of that area and know what will bring the most
value, and are able to prioritise work items efficiently.

statement:
You have delivered half of the features in the backlog for your Agile
project. You've noticed there is quite a difference in the number of
defects over the last three iterations, and you ask the team to
investigate both common cause and special cause variation. What will
they investigate?
option1:
Issues that arose from normal operations, and issues that arose from a
unique or new condition.
option2:
Issues caused by the common area, and issues caused by the special
software.
option3: Issues found in the last release, and issues found in this release.
option4:
Issues caused by continuous integration, and issues caused by no
integration.
explanation:
Variation in Lean is separated into common cause, which is caused by
normal operations, and special cause, which is caused by special or new
factors.

statement:
You are working in a new Agile team and have reviewed the team velocity
over the past three iterations. The maximum number of points completed
was 35, and the fewest was 25. What will you do next?
option1:
Push the team to complete five more than the maximum, and roll over any
incomplete cards to the next iteration.
option2:
Set 35 as the Work in Progress (WIP) limit, and adjust it during future
iterations if the team is able to pull more work than assigned.
option3:
Add more people to the team to complete the work, because 35 is not
enough.
option4:
Reduce the number of features in the product backlog to suit the team
velocity.
explanation:
Agile works on continuous, sustainable delivery. Pushing the team beyond
their known limits is not a part of Agile practice. Once the team
Velocity is known it is good practice to set a WIP limit for future
iterations so as to not overburden the team. Team members can still pull
in new cards to work on if they finish existing work, and at that time
you can review the team velocity.

statement:
You are the product owner in an Agile team and have a list of features
in a backlog that need to be developed. Each feature is assigned a
dollar value, representing the return on investment. You would like it
to be a risk-adjusted backlog. What will you do next?
option1:
Note the risk impacts in dollars against the return in dollars, then
reprioritise the backlog.
option2: Adjust the backlog to focus on the largest features first.
option3:
Capture known risks for each feature, then place cards to control those
risks and prioritise in the backlog.
option4:
Create a risk register for the project, then have it signed off by the
risk owners.
explanation:
The risk-adjusted backlog is a sprint or release backlog containing risk
response tasks for actionable risks. These will be prioritised against
the normal features, also in the backlog, to adjust for risk.

statement:
You are the Agile project manager analysing the risk for each feature in
your backlog by assigning a probability and an impact score to each
risk, then multiplying them to calculate risk severity. How will the
Agile team handle items with the highest risk?
option1: Change the requirements to remove the risk.
option2:
Schedule them later in the project so you can deliver maximum value
first.
option3:
Schedule them earlier in the project to reduce the risk impact over
time.
option4:
Place all the high-risk items in a "risk iteration" where the focus is
on reducing risk.
explanation:
Risk is highest early in the project before items have been completed.
By completing the high-risk items first, you are significantly reducing
those risks and the threat of unknowns later on in the project.

statement:
The Scrum master tells you that Agile builds problem solving into its
process in a number of ways, using transparency tools and ceremonies.
Which of the following is NOT something that is designed to reveal and
solve problems?
option1: Retrospectives
option2: Writing user stories
option3: Iteration demonstrations and reviews
option4: Raising blockers in the daily-stand-up
explanation:
Retrospectives, Sprint reviews and daily stand-ups all serve to reveal
and solve problems that arise during product delivery. Writing user
stories is a useful task, but is not itself designed for revealing
problems.

statement:
The Whole Team Approach in Agile is an important part of creating the
right cross-functional team. The team facilitator is a key member within
the whole team. What is NOT correct regarding the Team Facilitator role?
option1:
The Team Facilitator, or servant leader, can be called a project
manager, scrum master, project team lead, team coach, or a portfolio
manager.
option2:
The Team Facilitator must have a degree in business to be sufficiently
respected.
option3: All Agile teams need servant leadership on the team.
option4:
Team members should build up their own servant leadership skills of
facilitation, coaching, and removing blockers.
explanation:
The Team facilitator role, or Scrum Master, servant leader, project
lead, helps grow team members into their own leadership skills and all
Agile teams need this on their team. It also can be performed by anyone
in the team if there is no specific role for it. You do not need a
degree in business, but you do need to remove blockers and facilitate
problem solving.

statement:
You are rearranging a current Agile team, and you know that Agile teams
are cross-functional. What sort of team members will you select as you
rearrange the team?
option1:
Generalising specialists, with one focus speciality and a wide range of
experience in other skills.
option2:
Team leaders, with a speciality in leadership so everyone can lead in
the Agile team.
option3: Risk specialists, because there is a higher risk in Agile team delivery.
option4:
Don't add team members -- you should shrink the team in accordance with
the XP principles.
explanation:
An Agile team is made up of generalising specialists, or "T-shaped"
people with one deep knowledge area and a wide range or experience in
other areas. The whole team is responsible for Risk in an Agile team.
Shrinking Teams is only applicable for mature teams who are performing
well and can reduce time and effort.

statement:
You are helping the organisation set up a new Agile team and making sure
they consider the Whole Team Approach. What is NOT correct regarding the
Whole Team Approach?
option1:
The whole-team approach means involving everyone with the knowledge and
skills necessary to ensure project success.
option2:
The team should be relatively small (successful teams have been observed
with as few as three people and as many as nine).
option3:
Teams are often 100% dedicated to the delivery, because when switching
or multitasking people make more mistakes and experience between 20% and
40% loss of productivity.
option4:
Teams should not be multi-skilled as this can distract from the clarity
of the role.
explanation:
The whole team approach ensures team members ARE multi-skilled, and have
at least one deep specialty.

statement:
You are having trouble selecting team members for your new Agile team in
a new organisation. What is NOT true about the cross functional team
member in the Whole Team Approach?
option1:
Cross functional teams consist of team members with all the skills
necessary to produce a working product.
option2:
Teams do not need to use visual management because they are already good
at what they do.
option3:
In software development, cross functional teams are comprised of
designers, developers, testers, and any other required roles.
option4:
Cross Functional team members are critical because they can deliver
finished work in the shortest possible time, with higher quality,
without external dependencies.
explanation:
Agile teams will use visual management no matter how good they are.
Methods like Kanban, Stan-ups, Retrospectives, Burn-down charts are used
to increase better team working, regardless of skill.

statement:
There are three main types of role in an Agile team. The Agile team
includes representatives for the Product Owner, cross functional team
members, and:
option1: the Team Creator
option2: the Team Facilitator
option3: the Team Runner
option4: the Team Enabler
explanation:
The Team Facilitator, also known as the Scrum Master, Team Coach,
Iteration Manager, Project Manager, Servant Leader and many other names.
Their role is to help raise and remove blockers for the team, ensure an
active flow of work, and problem solve with the team when needed.

statement:
You are working through the iteration with your Agile team, and are
having trouble completing one of the story cards in the sprint. It is
dependant on something else being done and the contact is away. What
will you do next?
option1:
Raise a blocker at the next daily stand-up (or before) so the team can
swarm around the problem and solve it quickly.
option2: Bring the piece of work in-house so you don't have to worry anymore.
option3:
Ask the product owner to remove that feature for now, you can revisit it
later.
option4: Add cost to the budget and find a third party to complete the work.
explanation:
Problem solving is done with the Agile team. Blockers are raised as soon
as possible -- at least during a daily stand-up, so the team can swarm
around the problem, get help from any experts needed, and move on.

statement:
The product owner representing the business on your Agile project is
used to a more dictator-style of leadership. You explain that servant
leadership is used in Agile to ensure a safe and open environment. Why
is this important to product development?
option1: So the Agile team lead knows who to blame when things go wrong.
option2: So the team can accurately count the defects and mistakes.
option3:
So the team feels free to ask for help, admit to problems or failures
and improve.
option4:
So the entire team can push work on to each other if they know they have
time.
explanation:
Psychological safety is one of the most important parts of a
high-functioning team, which means having the safety to be vulnerable,
admit problems, ask for help and improve. It starts with the leader, and
servant leadership contributes to this.

statement:
Feedback in many different forms is one of the most important parts of a
successful Agile product delivery. Which of the below is an Agile method
for gathering and using feedback to improve?
option1: Having a once-weekly working group meting with project stakeholders
option2:
Conducting sprint reviews or demonstrations that include all
stakeholders.
option3: Sending out project reports to project stakeholders to view.
option4: Asking stakeholders for feedback only development is blocked.
explanation:
Demonstrating the usable increment developed during an iteration to the
customer or product owner ensures feedback is gathered more quickly than
only at the end of a project. Retrospectives are another method for
feedback on the team process.

statement:
You are working on an Agile project where the product business
representative is pushing for more and more features to be delivered in
each iteration. You know what they are asking is not possible, based on
your team's current velocity. What will you do next?
option1:
Show them the team velocity chart and the points allotted to cards in
the backlog, and ask them to prioritise the features at the next sprint
planning.
option2: Add more members to the team so you can get the work they want done.
option3:
Ask for another business representative who understands technology
better.
option4: Remove some of the features yourself and see if anyone notices.
explanation:
An Agile team ultimately serves the customer, and in many cases the
customer is the business owner or representative. If they are not also
the product owner (or familiar with that way of work), you should show
them the team velocity versus how many points are currently allotted,
and invite them to prioritise the features they want next. This gives
them control over the value delivered while not overburdening the team.

statement:
You are working on a traditional waterfall project and the organisation
wants to move to an Agile way of work. The functional manager wants to
get the benefit of certain Agile tools they have heard about that are
used to uncover potential problems before they occur. What will you do
next?
option1:
Write the change into your project management plan and circulate it with
project stakeholders.
option2:
Set a weekly working group meeting with your project stakeholders to
discuss the project.
option3: Iteration planning
option4:
Make the product backlog, iteration Kanban board, and velocity chart all
visible in a co-located team area.
explanation:
There are many Agile tools to detect and prevent problems, and Visual
Management is one of them. By making the Kanban visible to all, along
with the backlog and velocity chart, everyone can clearly see what the
team is up to, if things are going off course, and if there are any
blockers. Co-location, Daily Stand-ups, team iteration planning,
retrospectives, demos and reviews, are all designed to discover problems
and solve them early.

statement:
You are in the beginning stages of a new project, where the organisation
would like to use an Agile approach to managing the project work. The
executive manager asks you how the team will make quality everyone's
responsibility, leverage the various skill sets within the team, and
remove reliance on external inputs. What Agile method is she describing?
option1: Servant Leadership.
option2: Sprint planning.
option3: The daily stand-up.
option4: The whole team approach.
explanation:
The whole team approach aims to bring all necessary people into the
project team, ideally in a co-located space. It involves
cross-functional team members who specialise in one skill and are fluent
enough in a wide range of other skills.

statement:
You have been working on an Agile project for some time and have been
blocked by the need for expertise in another team. You need to overcome
organisational silos. What will you do?
option1:
Work with the various managers of team members you need and have them
dedicate the necessary individuals to the cross-functional team.
option2:
Raise a change request for review by the project Change Control Board as
the work will be delayed.
option3:
Raise an issue in the issue log for review and management by the project
team.
option4:
Work with the risk team to raise a project risk, assign it to an owner
and create controls for those risks.
explanation:
This is describing the Whole Team Approach, where all the necessary team
members and their skills are brought into the project team.

statement:
The twelve Agile clarifying principles also match up with the best
advice for workflow and getting things done. Which of the below is a
correct clarifying principle when it comes to project workflow?
option1:
Agile does not use processes, as it prefers individuals and interactions
over processes and tools.
option2:
Agile processes engage and support the team by ensuring sponsors,
developers and users all get what they want.
option3:
Agile processes promote sustainable development. The sponsors,
developers, and users should be able to maintain a constant pace
indefinitely.
option4:
Agile processes help as much work get done as possible. When the team
thinks they can't go any further, Agile pushes them to go the extra
mile.
explanation:
Agile promotes sustainable development, where the team is not
overburdened and is able to work at a constant pace indefinitely. Be
wary of often switching tasks or pushing for too much work during each
iteration -- this will burn out individuals and ultimately slow down
your progress.

statement:
The twelve Agile clarifying principles also match up with the best
advice for quality in a project. Which of the below is a correct Agile
clarifying principle when it comes to project quality?
option1:
Continuous flow ensures work is done at a reasonable pace and defects
are caught.
option2: Testing is done at all levels, from unit testing to end to end testing.
option3:
Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design enhances
quality.
option4:
Quality is everyone's responsibility in an Agile team, meaning everyone
will test the product.
explanation:
While some of these answers match up with the Agile principles in
general, only "Continuous Attention to technical excellence and good
design enhances quality" is a clarifying principle from this list.

statement:
The twelve Agile clarifying principles also match up with the best
advice for keeping things simple in a project. Which of the below is a
correct Agile clarifying principle?
option1:
Simplicity is only possible when the team first understands how complex
the system is.
option2:
Simplicity is part of every project, everyone naturally simplifies the
work as part of their role.
option3:
Simplicity reduces cost in a project, ensuring executives are always
simplifying the process and the system.
option4:
Simplicity -- the art of maximising the amount of work not done -- is
essential.
explanation:
The correct answer here is "Simplicity -- the art of maximising the
amount of work not done -- is essential" being an Agile clarifying
principle.

statement:
You are going through a retrospective with your team at the end of the
sprint, where the team have raised an issue that is slowing down their
work. The scrum master guides the team to go through an exercise called
the Five Whys. What is the purpose of this?
option1: To ask who, what, where and when to find the cause of the problem.
option2:
To ask why the issue happened, then why for each reason after that,
until the cause is found.
option3:
To ask why the team is working on each feature, to give the team
purpose.
option4:
To allocate five whys to each story card to ensure the team knows the
reason for working on in.
explanation:
The Five Whys is a problem solving technique from Lean or the Toyota
Production System that asks "why" a problem occurred. For each answer,
the team asks "why" again, until they get to the real cause of the
issue.

statement:
The Scrum Master in the Agile team has asked the team to do a team
self-assessment. What is the focus of this of this assessment?
option1: How often the team meets during the week.
option2: How many story cards the team have completed during this iteration.
option3: How much yearly bonus the team should receive.
option4: How the team performs and delivers together.
explanation:
The team self-assessment is used to gauge the team members performance
together, with the results helping to identify what is working well and
where improvements can be made. The team then meets to problem solve
improvements and to celebrate what is working well.

statement:
You are working in an Agile team and the business representative would
like to get an idea of the risk associated with a particular feature.
What will you do next?
option1:
Show the risk management plan from your project management plan to the
business rep.
option2: Ask the development team if they like the feature.
option3:
Hold a pre-mortem, where each team member writes reasons they think the
task or feature might fail so these can be discussed.
option4:
Check your gut-feel for the feature, and if it doesn't feel right look
into it more deeply.
explanation:
A pre-mortem looks at reasons for failure before they happen, and is a
great way to brainstorm risks for a card or feature. Typically, team
members write their reasons down then discuss in a round-robin style, as
some people may come up with the same idea.

statement:
You are the Agile team leader and are working through a team
retrospective when an issue experienced by the team is raised. What will
you do next?
option1: Perform fishbone analysis
option2: Perform Kano analysis
option3: Perform regression analysis
option4: Perform multi-vari chart analysis
explanation:
When an issue is raised the best course of action is to get to the root
cause of the issue. Of the items presented, fishbone or Ishikawa
analysis is the best tool for root cause analysis.

statement:
You have been hired to lead a new project team as an Agile project
manager. As you come on board you learn that the organisation uses a
hybrid model. What does this mean?
option1:
The team is a hybrid of developers, designers and business users to get
the best return on investment.
option2:
The product you are developing will use both electricity and fuel for
power.
option3:
A combination of predictive (i.e. a project plan) and adaptive
(iterations, backlogs, Kanban) project methods will be used.
option4:
A hybrid team environment of physical and software reporting boards will
be used.
explanation:
A hybrid project management lifecycle means using both predictive and
adaptive methods. Predictive is your traditional waterfall approach,
with pre-planned project plans, scope plans, quality, schedule and risk.
Adaptive approaches will add a prioritised backlog of features to be
delivered, a Kanban board to manage the work, iterations and
retrospectives.

statement:
The Agile manifesto has twelve clarifying principles added to it to help
projects and teams understand the Agile values. They are also a best
practice on improving the team over time. Which of the below is a
correct clarifying principle?
option1: The team will meet daily to build a supportive network of workers.
option2:
At regular intervals, the team reflects on how to become more effective,
then tunes and adjusts its behaviour accordingly.
option3: Features are regularly improved through refactoring of the code.
option4:
The project sponsor regularly swaps team member roles to keep everyone
learning and growing.
explanation:
The correct Agile Clarifying Principle is: "At regular intervals, the
team reflects on how to become more effective, then tunes and adjusts
its behaviour accordingly," which becomes the team Retrospective, in
practice.

statement:
The Agile manifesto has twelve clarifying principles added to it to help
projects and teams understand the Agile values. Which of the below is
NOT an Agile clarifying principle?
option1:
Our highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and
continuous delivery of valuable software.
option2: Ensure delivery timelines are met at all costs.
option3:
Welcome changing requirements, even late in development. Agile processes
harness change for the customer's competitive advantage.
option4:
Deliver working software frequently, from a couple of weeks to a couple
of months, with a preference to the shorter timescale.
explanation:
Each of these are correct Agile clarifying principles, except answer B,
"Ensure delivery timeframes are met at all costs."

statement:
You are the product owner in an Agile team, working with the developers
to create Story Cards to place in the product backlog. In the three C's
of user story creation, what will you do first?
option1:
Create the card, which is the physical (or software driven) media
describing a user story.
option2: Confirm the acceptance criteria to go into the user story.
option3:
Have a Conversation with the team to flesh out the requirements and
criteria.
option4:
Catch defects before they are developed by working with Quality testers
before and after.
explanation:
The three C's of User Story Creation include: Creating the card,
Conversation with the Product owner and developers to explain how the
software or design will be used, and Confirming the acceptance criteria
and the definition of done.

statement:
You are working on the product backlog with your Agile project team, and
creating user stories to be prioritised. Which of the below is the best
answer for what should go into a user story card?
option1:
Key risks of the project and the feature being delivered, with their
owners and mitigations.
option2:
A wireframe of the feature to be developed that developers can work
with.
option3:
The requirement, its criticality, expected development and test
duration, and the acceptance criteria for that story.
option4:
The benefit of the feature so key stakeholders are aware of what they
will get.
explanation:
Agile story cards should have the requirement, its criticality, test
details and clear acceptance criteria.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager and have begun creating user stories
with your Agile project team to go into the product backlog. You have
created the card, added requirements, criticality and acceptance
criteria. What will you do next?
option1:
Place the card on the Kanban board so the team can clearly see the
future work.
option2:
Have a conversation with developers about how the software will be used,
confirm the acceptance criteria and definition of done.
option3:
Prioritise the card in the upcoming sprint backlog to ensure the work
gets done.
option4:
Advise the project sponsor and risk manager of the feature details to
ensure risks are captured.
explanation:
The three C's on user story creation are: Card, Conversation and
Confirmation. Once the user story card is created, have a conversation
with developers and testers to ensure understanding, check for any gaps,
and estimate the effort required. Finally, confirm the acceptance
criteria. You can then place it in the backlog for prioritisation in an
upcoming sprint.

statement:
You are working on a project as an Agile Project Manager. The executive
manager who will own the product you are delivering would like to focus
primarily on set processes and tools to manage to project. What will you
recommend to them instead?
option1: Focus on the definition of success as defined by the project manager
option2: Focus primarily on the individuals and interactions involved
option3: Focus primarily on the scope of the project
option4: Focus primarily on the people involved in the project
explanation:
The Agile manifesto recommends focusing on Individuals and Interactions
over processes and tools. Projects are run by people, products are
developed by people for people to use, and our interactions either make
or break a project.

statement:
The executive management in your organisation has assembled a new team,
and dictated that they will use Agile for their product development. As
the project progresses, there is confusion about the product scope and
what the definition of done looks like. As an Agile Project Manager,
what will you recommend to the executive team?
option1:
