Select a person from each part of the organisation to assist, to ensure
diversity.
option2:
Have the functional manager select their friends or favourite team
members.
option3:
Encourage a self-organizing team to form, including the people who asked
for the requirements and outcomes in the first place.
option4: Hire external consultants to run the project and create the product.
explanation:
Agile encourages self-organizing teams, formed by the people who created
the requirements and had the desired outcome in the first place. This
gives a higher level of ownership and product expertise, that often
cannot be bought.

statement:
You are running an Agile project and meet with the team for the next
sprint planning meeting. What will you do next?
option1: Negotiate the scope and costs of the project with the project sponsor.
option2:
Walk through three questions with the team---What did I do yesterday?
What will I do today? Is anything blocking me?
option3:
The development team demonstrates the working feature to the product
owner, who marks it as complete.
option4:
The product owner shares the updated backlog, and the team discusses it
to ensure a shared understanding.
explanation:
The product owner should share the updated items in the product backlog
with the team and ensure a shared understanding before moving forward.

statement:
You are a project manager working on an Agile project, and want your
team to maximise the value they bring to the customer. What can you
eliminate in order to maximise project value?
option1: Daily stand-ups
option2: Unnecessary features
option3: Sprint reviews
option4: Testing
explanation:
There are many ways to reduce \"wasteful\" steps in the process of an
agile team. We can stop the introduction of unnecessary features, reduce
work in progress, reduce delays and hand-offs, extra processes, task
switching, waiting, defects and more.

statement:
You are working on a project and the team have recently moved to an
Agile way of work. One of the team members is confused about Scrum and
what happens after the sprint review. What will you tell them happens
next?
option1: Hold a sprint retrospective with the team
option2: Perform product backlog refinement with the product owner
option3:
Perform story card elaboration with the three amigos (Business,
Development, Testing)
option4: Hold a Daily Scrum
explanation:
A sprint retrospective is held with the Whole Team, after a sprint
review but before the sprint planning meeting. We use it to gather
lessons learned and look for opportunities for improvement, and everyone
has a voice for feedback and to implement improvements before the next
sprint.

statement:
You are working in an Agile team and preparing the Agile team charter.
You work with the Agile team to ensure the team charter answers these
four questions: Why are we doing this project? Who benefits and how?
option1: When will the project end? What do we do with the product?
option2:
What does "done" mean for the project? How are we going to work
together?
option3: How much risk is on the project? What is the project scope?
option4: How many defects are allowed in the product? How many testers are there?
explanation:
An Agile team charter typically covers Why we are doing the project, who
benefits and how, what does done look like, and how are we going to work
together. The Agile Practice Guide (2017), Chapter Five, p49

statement:
You are working with a new Agile project manager and explain that Agile
favours value-based measurements instead of predictive measurements on a
project. What is an example of value based measurements?
option1: Earned value management and the schedule performance index
option2: Variance Analysis and the Estimate to Complete
option3: Feature burndown chart (features delivered) and customer satisfaction
option4: Variance at Completion and the cost performance index
explanation:
Agile works with value delivered as its primary measurement. All the
other options are predictive measurements from a traditional project
management methodology.

statement:
You are a leading a coaching session with your project team on the
additional Agile and Lean frameworks that have contributed to the Agile
way of work. What frameworks will you share with the team?
option1: Project Management Plan, Project Charter
option2: Scaled Agile Framework, Large Scale Scrum
option3: Working Group Meetings, Project Post Mortems
option4: Flow Charting, Affinity Diagrams
explanation:
Agile and lean frameworks include Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe), Large
Scale Scrum (LeSS), Scrum of Scrums, Dynamic Systems Development Method,
along with the more common Kanban, eXtreme programming and Scrum. The
Agile Practice Guide (2017), p110

statement:
You are working on a project in the role of Scrum Master, and have set a
meeting with the team and the product owner to refine the product
backlog. What should the team do in this meeting?
option1: Estimate and refine the work items
option2: Prioritize the work items
option3: Add features to the project backlog
option4: Identify fixes
explanation:
The Product Owner adjusts the priority of product backlog items. The
team is responsible for elaborating on and then estimating the effort
for their work items. Each time the team refines their estimates with a
higher level of detail.

statement:
You are working with a team who would like to become Agile. The
functional manager asks you what the difference between Kanban and Scrum
is. What will you tell them?
option1: Kanban teams plan their work in sprints or iterations.
option2: There are no WIP limits in Kanban.
option3: Kanban teams work on a project as a whole.
option4: Kanban teams employ a pull system.
explanation:
The main difference between Scrum and Kanban is that Kanban teams employ
a pull system. This means when an item of work is completed, it triggers
someone to "pull" the next item in the queue onto the board to work on.
Kanban teams work off a Kanban board that displays each task, and have a
limit to how much work in progress (WIP) can be in place.

statement:
You are working on a new project with an Agile product development team,
who have self-formed and chosen a Scrum Master called Jeremy. Jeremy
immediately sets a meeting with the team to explain and flesh out their
Team Charter. What has made Jeremy a good choice for Scrum Master?
option1: He likes to work within set processes
option2: He has been with the company for more than five years
option3: He shows servant leadership and engages the team in decisions
option4: He sticks to the plan and does not change
explanation:
Jeremy shows the traits of servant leadership by engaging the team in
team decisions, starting with the Team Charter and ground rules so
everyone knows where they stand. By communicating the reasons for his
decisions, he is a suitable Scrum Master and will be less focused on
command and control.

statement:
You are managing a project as an Agile project manager and a business
representative for the product you are delivering asks you how many
defects were found in the latest round of testing. You don't know the
answer. What should you do?
option1: Guess the number then change the subject
option2:
Tell the business partner you will send an e-mail to him with the
information
option3:
Take him into the team area, where the "information radiator" with
project information is displayed
option4:
Ask the business partner to call the Scrum Master to get a correct
answer
explanation:
A core component of Agile is Visual Management and transparency. Part of
this are the various parts of the project information radiator (often
physical, but can be tech-driven). This includes things such as the team
velocity, Kanban board, the product backlog, the burndown chart, the
latest test results and issues, and results of retrospectives.

statement:
You are working in a company that is moving from a Waterfall methodology
to Agile. You mention to the business unit manager that the team will be
better prepared to respond to change and meet the customer needs. Why is
this?
option1:
The Agile team works directly with a business owner or representative to
constantly prioritise delivered value
option2: The Agile team delivers the product all in one go
option3: The Agile team is for developers only, so development will improve
option4: The business representatives ask for feature recommendations
explanation:
Working with business partners or someone who represents the customer
almost daily is a key part of Agile. The team hears what the business
would like the results to be, and meeting face to-face gets faster
answers than reading documents, e-mails, or even having conference
calls.

statement:
For Agile life-cycles, two kinds of planning occur, release planning and
iteration planning. In release planning, business representatives
establish and prioritize the user stories for the release, in
collaboration with the team, refining larger user stories into a
collection of smaller stories. What is the INCORRECT description below
regarding iteration planning and backlog preparation?
option1:
The backlog is the ordered list of all the work, presented in "story"
form, for a team.
option2:
The project coach encourages the team to work alone, so no one bothers
them.
option3:
The facilitator encourages the team to work in triads of a developer,
tester, and product owner/business analyst.
option4:
The triad discuss, write, and then place enough stories into an
iteration, and enough features for a first release.
explanation:
Answers A, C and D are correct in describing Backlog Preparation for
Iteration Planning. The Agile Practice Guide (2017), p53, 5.2.3

statement:
Behaviour-driven development (BDD) allows a developer to focus on
testing the code based on the expected behaviour of the software and is
a method of writing user stories. What is the correct description
regarding BDD acceptance-based criteria?
option1: Make, Buy, Do
option2: Tell, Show, Repeat
option3: Find, Create, Perform
option4: Given, When, Then
explanation:
Given, When, Then is the way BDD is used, particularly when outlining
user Story Cards for your iteration. It highlights "Given" a certain
situation, "When" something happens, "Then" we want this to happen.

statement:
Every project has characteristics around requirements, delivery, change
and goals. Understanding the different types of life cycle, you can
choose the right one for the circumstances of your project. What is the
correct description for a \"Waterfall\" approach?
option1:
Predictive -- Fixed requirements, performed once for the entire project,
in a single delivery.
option2:
Predictive -- Fixed requirements, performed multiple times for the
entire project, in multiple deliveries.
option3:
Predictive -- Variable requirements, performed once for the entire
project in a single delivery.
option4:
Predictive -- Variable requirements, performed multiple times for the
entire project, in multiple deliveries.
explanation:
A Predictive or Waterfall project management lifecycle focuses on fixed
requirements, performed once for the project for a single delivery.

statement:
Every project has characteristics around requirements, delivery, change
and goals. Understanding the different types of life cycle will allow
you to choose the right one for the circumstances of your project. What
is the correct description for an \"Iterative\" project approach?
option1: Dynamic requirements, repeated until correct, in multiple deliveries.
option2: Dynamic requirements, repeated until correct, in a single delivery.
option3: Fixed requirements, repeated until incorrect, in a single delivery.
option4:
Fixed requirements, performed once for a given increment, in a single
delivery.
explanation:
Iterative methods have dynamic requirements, repeated with feedback
until they are correct, but delivered only once. The Agile Practice
Guide (2017), p18, Table 3-1.

statement:
A Team Charter is also a "social contract". Which of the below is NOT
usually a part of an Agile Team Charter?
option1: Team values, such as sustainable pace and core hours.
option2:
Working agreements, such as what "ready" means so the team can take in
work, and what "done" means so the team can judge completeness
consistently.
option3: Ground rules, such as one person talking in a meeting.
option4: Schedule performance index and cost performance index for the project.
explanation:
The Agile Team Charter focuses on the vision (the "Why") and the team
way of work. SPI and CPI are earned value management metrics from a
predictive project methodology, and not part of an Agile Team Charter.

statement:
You are working with a team that is new to Agile and are using Scrum to
manage the project. The business representative is confused about the
difference between a sprint review and a sprint retrospective. What will
you tell them?
option1:
Sprint reviews demonstrate the work completed in the sprint. Sprint
retrospectives look at all the work completed in the project.
option2:
Sprint reviews discuss what is worked on in the sprint. Sprint
retrospectives are done at the end of the project for a lessons learned
opportunity.
option3:
Sprint reviews review the lessons learned. Sprint retrospectives look at
the history of the product retrospectively.
option4:
Sprint reviews are for product demonstrations. Sprint retrospectives are
for lessons learned.

statement:
You are working on an Agile project as a team member, and begin
gathering the product requirements. What is the overall first question
that you should ask and use as a guide for your work?
option1: How long will the project last?
option2: What is the business value?
option3: How much is in the budget?
option4: How many team members will participate?
explanation:
Agile projects centre around delivering business value, and working
closely with the customers and business representatives to ensure that
the value is always correct. Business risk and the impact of not
undertaking the project must also be considered.

statement:
The Agile team for which you are the project manager has determined
several key features to deliver to the customer. How should the team
handle these feature ideas?
option1:
The features should be prioritized, developed, tested and delivered
incrementally
option2:
The features should be delivered quickly, whether or not they are
complete, to gather feedback
option3: The features should be delivered according to the project plan
option4: All features should be delivered at the end of the project
explanation:
Agile is a combination of Incremental delivery and Iterative
development. That means business value is continuously and iteratively
improved and prioritised, while also being delivered in increments that
the customer can see, feel and touch. The features should be complete,
tested, and quality is everyone's responsibility.

statement:
You are working on an Agile project and the project team have just
finished an iteration. All tasks for the iteration have been completed,
and you are ready to move to the next iteration, but are not sure what
the team should work on. What will you do next?
option1: Follow the project plan developed early in the project
option2:
Follow the PMO, who has already determined how the project should
progress
option3: Ask the product owner, who continuously prioritizes the backlog
option4: Ask the project coach or team leader to determine the next iteration
explanation:
The product owner represents the customer, who you are delivering
business value to. The team works through the list of items that have
identifiable value and have been prioritized by the customer.

statement:
While meeting with the customers or business owners, they introduce
several pieces of new functionality into the product backlog list. The
deadline for the product cannot be changed. What will you do next?
option1: Tell the team to work overtime in order to deliver the project
option2:
Stand by your team and tell the customers the project will not be
delivered on time
option3:
Be sure the customers understand that lower priority features may not be
completed at all
option4:
Place the new features in a \"parking lot\" that can be completed if
time allows
explanation:
An Agile project can accept changes even late in development. But while
the competing constraints of cost, quality and schedule are often fixed,
in Agile the scope can vary. This means some features may need to drop
off, as others become prioritised. A maintainable pace of work is
required too - project managers must not overwork the project team to
increase the pace of the project work.

statement:
Every project has characteristics around requirements, delivery, change
and goals. Understanding the different types of life cycle will allow
you to choose the right one for the circumstances of your project. What
is the correct description for an \"Incremental\" project management
approach?
option1: Fixed requirements, activities performed once, in multiple deliveries.
option2:
Dynamic requirements, activities repeated until correct, in one large
delivery.
option3:
Dynamic requirements, performed once for a given increment, in frequent
smaller deliveries.
option4:
Fixed requirements, performed once for a given increment, in frequent
smaller deliveries.
explanation:
Incremental project management approach includes dynamic requirements,
performed once for a given increment, with frequent smaller deliveries.
The Agile Practice Guide (2017), p18, Table 3-1.

statement:
There are certain core practices that are common amongst all the various
Agile frameworks, and used within any team that calls themselves Agile.
Which of the below is NOT a core Agile practice?
option1: Team Iteration Planning
option2:
Collaborative User Story Creation between the business, customer,
developers and testers
option3: Demonstrations and Reviews of the features developed
option4: Autocratic Leadership, to ensure the work gets done
explanation:
The preferred style of leadership in an Agile team is Servant
Leadership. Autocratic leadership typically is a one person affair,
while servant leadership builds psychological safety for the whole team
to engage and contribute. All other items listed are Agile core
practices.

statement:
Poor specifications are often a major reason for project failure. In
Agile development, user stories are written with the developers,
testers, and business representatives, with frequent reviews to ensure
they are right. Which of the following is NOT correct regarding
Collaborative User Story Creation?
option1: A user story addresses both functional and non-functional requirements.
option2: The project sponsor must sign off on all user stories in advance.
option3:
A user story includes acceptance criteria for the feature to be
developed.
option4:
Characteristics for acceptance criteria must be: Estimable, Small, and
Testable.
explanation:
While it is common for the developers, testers and business
representative to agree on the story card criteria, it is not signed off
by the project sponsor directly. The product owner, representing the
business and/or the customer, refines the feature backlog of work so
they always know that business value is being delivered.

statement:
In Continuous Integration: configuration management, compilation,
software build, deployment, and testing are wrapped into a single,
automated, repeatable process. Defects in code are:
option1:
not an issue during continuous integration, making it an ideal Agile
method.
option2:
detected more quickly because they constantly integrate their work,
build and test.
option3:
integrated into their work during this phase as part of Test Driven
Development.
option4:
collected and fixed in an iteration at the end of the project, to avoid
delay.
explanation:
Continuous Integration merges code to a test environment using an
automated process, including automated regression tests to ensure
nothing is broken. This allows the team to find and fix defects in the
code more quickly. Test Driven Development writes a test first (which
fails at first), then the code to ensure the test passes.

statement:
Agile has certain core practices that are used within any team working
in an Agile way. Which of the following is NOT a core practice of Agile?
option1:
The whole team approach, where any person needed is brought into the
project team.
option2:
Early and frequent feedback, to ensure a common understanding of the
product.
option3: Daily stand-ups, to report on progress and raise blockers.
option4: Reporting to executives, to ensure they are happy with your project.
explanation:
While reporting to executives has its place when running a project, in
Agile it would rather be done by including them in other Agile core
practices, such as demonstrations or reviews of the product, showing the
product burndown chart in the team area, and the backlog of work. All
other items are Agile core practices.

statement:
You are working on a project as an Agile project manager. You look at
the product backlog and ensure several features, or minimum viable
products (MVPs) can be scheduled for delivery throughout the duration of
the project. Why is this a good idea?
option1: To show executives that progress is being made by the development team
option2: To keep the project duration short
option3: To enable users to test the product for quality, and give feedback
option4: To enable the business to get value quickly, as features are delivered
explanation:
Minimum Viable Products are usable features in their own right that are
delivered incrementally. This allows for business value to be gained
more quickly, while gathering feedback on the use of these features.

statement:
You are working on a complex software project but see that the team uses
a whiteboard, and writes tasks on cards or sticky notes. The business
representative seems put off by the use of these low-tech tools on such
a high profile technology project. What will you tell them?
option1:
The team are using low-tech tools so low-tech people can understand
them.
option2: A whiteboard is actually a complex thing, they just don't know it yet.
option3:
The team is using the principle of visual management, and by using a
physical board it is transparent to anyone who needs the information.
option4:
The team will drop everything and develop a new software-based Kanban
board to avoid embarrassment.
explanation:
Visual Management is a Lean principle borrowed into Agile, where we make
key pieces of information as accessible as possible. Often found in the
team area and often physical, it means anyone can walk through the area
and see what they need at a glance. Kanban is an example of a low-tech /
high touch tool.

statement:
Agile teams practice frequent verification and validation during and at
the end of each iteration through demonstrations and reviews (such as
the Sprint Review). Which of the following is NOT a benefit of Sprint
Reviews?
option1: It enables the team to catch mistakes and missed requirements early
option2: It keeps everyone busy and gives them something to do
option3: It ensures an early feedback loop between the customer and developers
option4: It reduces cost overruns as mistakes are found and fixed earlier
explanation:
Busy-work does not add value to the project, and is not a benefit of
Agile.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager on a software project that needs to be
delivered quickly. You would like to shorten the time for identifying
and resolving defects as they arise. What will you implement with the
team?
option1: Continuous integration
option2: Daily build and smoke tests
option3: Usability testing
option4: Exploratory testing
explanation:
Continuous integration aims to merge code and have a working build once
a day, then executes automated tests quickly to see if anything is
broken. This frequent checking ensures less time passes before a problem
is identified.

statement:
You are working with your agile team, and they are confused about when
the best time is to determine the acceptance test criteria for the
feature or story card. What time will you tell them?
option1: When the first iteration is complete
option2:
During estimation and elaboration of the card, with the product owner,
business analysts and development team
option3: Before the product or feature is prioritised
option4: At the end of the project
explanation:
Acceptance tests and criteria are designed before the development of a
deliverable, and during elaboration, often in a "Given, When, then"
scenario or an "As a," "I want," "So I can," scenario.

statement:
Every project has characteristics around requirements, delivery, change
and goals. Understanding the different types of life cycle will allow
you to choose the right one for the circumstances of your project.
Choose the correct description for an \"Agile\" approach.
option1:
Agile -- Dynamic requirements, repeated until correct, in frequent small
deliveries.
option2:
Agile -- Fixed requirements, repeated until correct, in frequent small
deliveries.
option3:
Agile -- Dynamic requirements, repeated until correct, in a single large
delivery.
option4:
Agile -- Fixed requirements, performed once for the entire project, in
frequent small deliveries.
explanation:
The Agile project management approach includes dynamic requirements,
repeated until correct, with frequent smaller deliveries. The Agile
Practice Guide (2017), p18, Table 3-1.

statement:
Continuous Integration wraps software build, deployment, and testing
into a single, automated, repeatable process. This merges all changes
made to the software and:
option1: separates each feature into its own development pool
option2: ensures no code is changed, to avoid complication
option3: integrates all changed components regularly, at least once a day
option4: enables new features for the product backlog
explanation:
Continuous integration is designed to merge all changes made to the
software and test them automatically, discovering any defects or issues
on a daily basis.

statement:
Crystal is one of the original frameworks that contributed to Agile. It
is designed to scale and realises that each project may require a
slightly tailored set of practices based on size and complexity. Its
core values include:
option1: People, Interaction, and Community.
option2: Finding people, building people, leading people.
option3: Building products, buying products, selling products.
option4: Profit, Persistence, and Power.
explanation:
Crystal's core values include People, Interaction and Community - in the
same way as Agile is based around iterations over processes and tools -
and understanding the impact things have on way of work and getting
things done.

statement:
Crystal is designed to scale and realises that each project may require
a slightly tailored set of practices based on size and complexity. What
are some of the ways that Crystal scales?
option1:
Crystal blue, indigo and violet, based on number of features in the
backlog.
option2: Crystal clear, yellow or red, based on number of people involved.
option3:
Crystal metal, wood and water, based on the flow of story cards through
the Kanban board.
option4: Crystal High, Low, and Medium, based on dollars spent on the project.
explanation:
Crystal Clear, Yellow, Orange and Red are based on factors such as
people involved (0 to 8, all the way up to 100 or more), money involved
and the general comfort level of the project.

statement:
You are working through an Agile project with your team, meeting daily
in a daily stand-up. What is NOT one of the reasons Agile teams use
daily stand-ups?
option1: To micro commit to the team
option2: To uncover and remove blockers
option3: To ensure work flows freely through the team
option4: To report to the executive team
explanation:
Stand-ups are used to micro-commit to each other, uncover blockers, and
ensure work flows freely through the team. While everyone (including an
executive) is welcome to join the stand-ups, they are not specifically
for reporting to an executive team.

statement:
You are the project manager on an Agile project that is half way
through. The project sponsor is starting to raise concerns that the
project will not be completed in the allotted time. What will you do to
gather the information necessary to answer the project sponsor?
option1: Review the Kanban board and the current WIP
option2: Check the product backlog for remaining features
option3:
Review the team velocity, product burndown chart and other key
performance indicators
option4: Have a team meeting and log everyone's status
explanation:
Agile Key Performance Indicators include team velocity (rate of
progress) and remaining work. These can be shown visually as well, such
as on a burndown chart. Reviewing these KPIs will give you the most
reliable answer.

statement:
You are attending the kick-off meeting with the Agile team and the
business owners. The Scrum Master recommends using MoSCoW to help
prioritise the work with the product / business owners. How does MoSCoW
prioritise the product backlog?
option1: Using Priority 1, Priority 2, Priority 3, Priority 4
option2: Using Low, medium, high
option3: Using must have, should have, could have, will not have
option4: Using multiple dots that people can assign to the features they like
explanation:
The MoSCoW method of prioritization labels tasks as must have, should
have, could have, and will not have (or would like to have). Other
prioritisation techniques are multi-voting with dots, monopoly money,
Kano analysis, and fist of five.

statement:
Agile teams use different methods to manage work in progress (WIP). The
business sponsor believes that removing the limits to work in progress
will help the team take on more work and get their product delivered
faster. What will you tell them?
option1: You will remove the limits and push as much work through as possible
option2:
You will dictate overtime and no holidays for the team to get things
done
option3:
WIP Limits ensure the team focuses on one main thing at a time, and
avoids the time cost of switching tasks
option4:
WIP can be seen on the Kanban board, so it doesn't matter what the limit
is
explanation:
In Agile, work is taken on based on the team velocity or rate of work.
The Kanban board shows the WIP and who is working on what. Knowing the
velocity allows you to ensure enough work is taken on, but not too much.
Research has proven that multitasking or switching tasks actually
reduces effectiveness as the team gets up to speed again.

statement:
You working as an Agile project manager in a team with many stakeholders
who have not worked on an Agile project before. Some of these
stakeholders come to you with valid concerns about the Agile methods
being used. As a servant leader or project coach, what will you do to
make them more comfortable with the process?
option1: Educate the stakeholders, address their concerns, and keep them engaged
option2: Tell them is has worked in the past, it will work here too
option3:
Dictate to the stakeholders that this is how all future projects must be
executed
option4: Go back to a Waterfall style project management method
explanation:
A servant leader includes the team in decisions, and grows the team
through education and coaching.

statement:
You are working on an Agile project with stakeholders who are keen to
know: Why is it so important to incorporate multiple touchpoints with
them before, during, and after iterations?
option1: To raise and manage changes, and identify potential risks and issues
option2: To inform the stakeholders of the development team's direction
option3:
To ensure business executives hear about the team's value with multiple
meetings
option4: To have them invest more time in the project so they are committed
explanation:
Frequent touch points in Agile are there to ensure the features are
reviewed and meet stakeholders' expectations, and risks are addressed.
It allows changes to be raised early. Quality is everyone's
responsibility on an Agile team.

statement:
Teams use daily stand-ups to micro-commit to each other, uncover and
remove blockers, and ensure work flows smoothly through the team. What
is NOT true about stand-ups?
option1: They are often time-boxed to 15 minutes
option2: The team walks through the Kanban or task board
option3: The team facilitator or anyone in the team can facilitate
option4: Only people in the project team can attend
explanation:
Agile teams are open and transparent, and encourage stakeholders to
"pull" information or work when needed. Anyone can attend, to learn,
watch or contribute. Stand-ups are time-boxed to 15 minutes, the team
walks through the Kanban board, and anyone in the team can facilitate.

statement:
Features are completed in an Agile project, usually in the form of
multiple user stories. When a feature is complete, the team periodically
demonstrates the working product to the customer, business
representative and/or the product owner. These demonstrations or
reviews:
option1:
only occur at the end of a the project, or when stakeholders are
available.
option2:
often occur at the end of an iteration, or when enough features have
been completed into a set that is coherent.
option3:
often occur at the start of the project so everyone knows what they have
to deliver.
option4: often occur when the team wants to, as they are so busy working anyway.
explanation:
Agile "sprint reviews", or feature demonstrations, occur at the end of
an iteration or when enough cards or features have been completed into a
set that can be showcased.

statement:
You are working with the developers in an Agile team, who are using the
test-driven development approach. They ask you whether they should
operate using something called Red-Green-refactor. What are they
referring to?
option1:
Where no code is developed during a red status, some code is developed
during a green status, and new features are taken in during refactor
status.
option2: A traffic light approach to bringing in new features.
option3: Only releasing features when they are given the green light.
option4:
Where a test is written first that fails, then passes once the solution
is developed, and can then be refactored or streamlined.
explanation:
The process of writing a test that initially fails, adding code until
the test passes, then refactoring the code is part of test Driven
Development. (TDD). Red-Green-Refactor is the same as fail, pass, and
streamline the code.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager working with the Product Owner of the
Agile team. There are several key deliverables that have been identified
and listed in the product backlog. What will the Agile team do next?
option1: All deliverables should go live only at the end of the project.
option2:
The tasks should be delivered but not tested, to get value to the
customer as quickly as possible.
option3: The tasks should be prioritized, developed and tested incrementally.
option4:
The tasks should be planned carefully and delivered according to their
plan.
explanation:
Agile develops and delivers in features or increments. This means
prioritising for each increment, developing and testing for each
increment. Even though we want to deliver quickly, quality testing is
still important and quality is everyone's responsibility.

statement:
A team new to Agile has worked closely with the product owner and has
identified the highest value features of the project. What is one reason
to break those features down into increments and deliver them as soon as
possible?
option1: To get all the work out of the way so the team can relax.
option2: So the project lifecycle can be shortened.
option3:
So the product owner can report to her executive manager that work is
being done.
option4:
To reduce the risk of the feature no longer providing value for the
customer.
explanation:
The longer a feature takes to deliver, the higher the risk is that the
feature will no longer provide the value it was intended to provide.
Value driven delivery prioritises features by highest value and delivers
them quickly for maximum customer benefit.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager working closely with the Scrum Master
and Agile team. The Scrum Master mentions that Agile works differently
with risk compared to a traditional project. He sets a meeting with you
and the team to talk through Agile risks. What will you do next?
option1:
Ensure the team notes and schedules high-risk activities early in the
project so that they are mitigated quickly.
option2:
Ensure the team identifies risks, places them in a register and
mitigates them by the end of the project.
option3:
Ensure the team keeps the risk register to itself, so other stakeholders
don't think less of the project.
option4:
Ensure risk is only captured around people because an Agile team prefers
individuals and interactions over processes and tools.
explanation:
An Agile team delivers in increments and iterates toward improvement. By
prioritising high-risk features first, future risks for the project are
reduced.

statement:
You are a project manager on an Agile project that requires an external
third party to develop software for one of your features. You have
worked with the project team to gather the requirements and backlog of
product features. You begin working with your procurement department on
a contract for the vendor. What will you do next?
option1:
Request that the contract is one that accommodates changes to product
scope.
option2: Go back to the business and ask them to deliver the feature
option3:
Raise a risk to the delivery of your project based on the external
vendor
option4:
Find an existing "time and materials" contract from the organisation's
Organisational Process Assets.
explanation:
Agile works with changing scope and cost as the product is completed
incrementally, but primarily a fixed schedule and quality. As you are
working with the procurement department, you will need to ensure a
contract that accommodates changing requirements and scope.

statement:
Agile projects have short iterations enabling the project team to
receive early and continuous feedback on product quality throughout the
development lifecycle. By getting frequent customer feedback as the
project progresses, Agile teams can incorporate most new changes into
the product and the development process. What is NOT one of the benefits
of this approach?
option1:
Avoiding requirements misunderstandings, which may not have been
detected until later in the development cycle when they are more
expensive to fix.
option2:
Clarifying customer feature requests, making them available for customer
use early.
option3: Discovering, isolating, and resolving quality problems early.
option4:
Having one big release at the end of the project that everyone can
celebrate.
explanation:
Agile methods work with multiple features, releasing them in increments
so the customer can use them and provide feedback. Having a single
release is a waterfall approach.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager tasked with evolving your part of the
organisation into Agile. What is NOT something you would do, when making
this change?
option1:
Treat the change as an Agile project, with its own backlog of changes
that could be introduced by the team based on perceived value.
option2:
Treat each of the changes as an experiment, tested for a short period of
time to determine suitability or the need for further refinement.
option3:
Use Kanban boards to track progress, showing new approaches in use as
"done", and things being tried as "in progress".
option4:
Direct the teams to take on Agile in one go, moving ahead without
wavering.
explanation:
Agile is an incremental approach. Using a backlog of Agile methods the
teams would like to use, treating them as an experiment, and using a
Kanban board to track progress are all things you can do to use Agile
when implementing Agile.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager working with the project sponsor in the
beginning stages of an Agile project. The project sponsor mentions that
they would like help creating the Project Charter. You tell them you
will also do a Team Charter for your Agile team, that will include the
project sponsor. What does this mean?
option1:
You will capture the Team values, Communication guidelines and
Decision-making process separately to the project purpose, requirements
and summary schedule.
option2:
You will create a project plan with Scope, Schedule, Cost and Quality
outlines to follow.
option3:
You will take over from the project sponsor and direct the team on
future customer requirements.
option4:
You will defer to the team for all queries in the way of a servant
leader.
explanation:
An Agile project manager charters both the project and the team. A
project charter focuses on the business case, project purpose, high
level requirements, stakeholders and risks. A Team Charter focuses on
the agreements in how the team will operate, and is created as a team.

statement:
You are working with the team on the team charter and have outlined the
project vision, mission, agreed meeting times and accepted team
behaviour. What will you do next?
option1:
Ask the team to get to work straight away, because Agile teams move
swiftly.
option2:
Send the Team Charter to the project sponsor for their sign off, so the
project can begin.
option3:
Agree on and include the "definition of done" for team features and
tasks.
option4:
Set a meeting for two weeks time so you can continue to develop the
charter iteratively.
explanation:
The definition of "done" is an important part of the Team Charter, to
ensure the team knows when a task is completed, when they can pull the
next tasks, and so everyone agrees what success looks like. This could
involve a card being defined, elaborated, developed, tested, reviewed
and then released. The project sponsor may be included in the team
charter, but does not need to sign off on it -- it is an agreement
amongst the team.

statement:
You are working on an Agile project, gathering requirements from the
business stakeholders with the Product Owner. During the discussion,
different people seem to have different understandings of what the
feature will look like. What will you do next?
option1:
Ask the stakeholders to elaborate their ideas better, and set another
meeting in a week's time.
option2:
Park that feature for now and begin work on a feature that is elaborated
better.
option3:
Draw a storyboard prototype of the requirement so all stakeholders can
see it, adjust it and agree.
option4:
Give your understanding of the feature and ask the stakeholders to agree
or disagree.
explanation:
Gaining consensus regarding the content and flow of a piece of software
by developing a wireframe or storyboard will confirm everyone has a
complete understanding of what the output is.

statement:
You are working as a project manager with an Agile project team. A key
stakeholder for the requirements of your project sits on another floor,
and prefers to communicate by email only. What will you do next?
option1:
Ensure you communicate by email only with the key stakeholder so they
are comfortable.
option2:
Stop communicating with the key stakeholder until they at least come to
a few project meetings.
option3:
Ask the key stakeholder to co-locate with the Agile team for a few days
a week, to ensure face to face communication and access to the visual
project information.
option4: Set meetings with the key stakeholder so you can update them personally.
explanation:
An Agile project team aims to work in a co-located space, where ideas
and information can flow freely. Face-to-face communication provides the
best opportunity to ask questions, get immediate feedback, and
understand body language in order to acknowledge agreement or clarify
misunderstandings. Kanban boards and other visual tools are also in the
team area.

statement:
As an Agile project manager, you understand the need to operate with
openness and transparency with your team and stakeholders. How will you
encourage knowledge sharing throughout the project?
option1: Tell your team what to do during work hours, so they can learn from you.
option2:
Write a process document for the team to read through in their spare
time.
option3:
Make key information visible in the team area, and ensure all impacted
stakeholders are invited to the daily stand-up, retrospectives, and even
iteration planning.
option4:
Form a key group of executive stakeholders to share project information
with, to ensure your good reputation within the company.
explanation:
An Agile team makes project information visible and accessible to all.
Agile teams also prefer people and interactions over processes and
tools.

statement:
You are testing the product being delivered by an Agile team in
increments. You notice that there have been no defects passed on to the
end user. You also notice they are working with an eXtreme Programming
(or XP) methodology for the team. What are the XP practices that led to
this result?
option1: Regular refactoring and daily deployment of an integrated code base
option2: Having real users test the product when it is released
option3: Moving team members around to different roles for a shared experience
option4: Ensuring the team does exactly what the Scrum Master says
explanation:
eXtreme Programming works with Continuous Integration -- deploying a
single code base on a daily basis and running automated regression tests
to see if anything is broken. In this way defects are picked up quickly.
Regular refactoring, or simplifying and cleaning the code also ensures
it is easier to work with and easier to pick up and fix defects.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager, working with an Agile coach and
planning the next sprint with the team. The Agile coach advises you to
build in some "slack" for the sprint. What does this mean?
option1: To use the messaging service "Slack" to communicate as a team.
option2:
Allowing the best performers in the team to "slack" off as a reward for
their hard work.
option3:
Adding a card for refactoring code, that can be replaced if an emergency
comes up.
option4: Creating a burn-down chart and a burn-up chart together.
explanation:
Building in slack is a way of giving some room in the allocated points
for a team Sprint. The Agile project manager, coach, facilitator or lead
does this by adding in cards for refactoring the code, then basing the
team's velocity off that. Refactoring regularly ensures the code is
clean and correct, and the refactoring card can be replaced if an
emergency comes up. In this way, it is "slack", or lee-way built into
the sprint.

statement:
The team are planning their first upcoming sprint on an Agile project.
They have worked with the product owner who has gathered a backlog of
features in prioritised order. As an Agile project manager, what must
you do to ensure you can plan future sprints correctly?
option1: Find the best performer in the team so you know who you can rely on.
option2:
Find your team\'s velocity over the next few sprints so you can set a
sustainable pace.
option3: Set a working group meeting to gain executive buy-in.
option4: Create the project management plan and draw out the schedule in advance.
explanation:
An Agile sprint is based on the team's velocity. The team's velocity is
the amount of points (or cards, or features, or whatever you choose) the
team is able to complete on average during a regular sprint. You will
need to complete at least one sprint to get an idea of velocity.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager and your organization is moving away
from waterfall to an Agile way of work. After reading the Agile
manifesto, the functional manager you are working with mentions
"individuals and interactions over processes and tools" and asks how you
will do it. What do you tell them?
option1:
Create ceremonies such as the daily stand-up, iteration planning
meeting, demos & reviews and retrospectives that encourage team
interaction.
option2: Create a process for the team to review that shows the way of work.
option3:
Ask the project sponsor to interact with the team by sharing some of
their journey.
option4:
Ensure the tools the team have to work with are state of the art
software solutions.
explanation:
Many Agile practices are based on a collaborative approach, which
enables fast decisions and ownership of the work. One way to do this is
co-locate the team, but others are the daily stand-up, team planning of
the next iteration, and retrospectives to improve the way of work moving
forward.

statement:
You are an experienced functional manager who has been asked to lead an
Agile project team. Your executive manager is calling it the "project
coach" or scrum master role. You know that Agile leadership might be
different to what you are used to. What sort of leadership style will
you embody?
option1:
Servant leadership, where you will lead the team by serving their needs,
growing their skills, and removing blockers.
option2:
Directive leadership, where you tell the team exactly what to do so they
don't have to worry.
option3:
Visionary leadership, where you focus on the high level features and
don't worry about the detail.
option4:
Laissez-faire leadership, where you take a hands off approach, and let
the team self-organise.
explanation:
An Agile leader is primarily a servant leader, a project coach. The
focus is on removing blockers for the team, helping them problem solve
and growing their skills where needed.

statement:
You are working as an Agile project manager in a new project team. Many
project team members are despondent, and do not show up for the meetings
and ceremonies you have set. What will you do next?
option1:
Raise a risk in the risk register around project goals, and ask the
project sponsor to mitigate the risk.
option2:
Have a coaching session with the team teaching the "what" and "why"
behind Agile ceremonies, and ensure each meeting has a clear outcome,
time and agenda.
option3:
Cancel all meetings for now, and write some documentation for the team
on what you'd like them to do.
option4: Make the decisions yourself and dictate the outcomes to the team.
explanation:
An Agile leader focuses on coaching the team, removing blockers and
ensuring interaction. The key Agile ceremonies such as stand-ups, sprint
reviews and retrospectives help support and improve the work and get the
answers people need.

statement:
You are an experienced project manager who is used to a directive
leadership style where people do exactly what you say. Now you are
working in an Agile team, where that style of leadership is not
accepted. What is one thing you can change to move to a more servant
leadership style?
option1: Ensure the executive team makes all the decisions.
option2: Ensure the stakeholders collectively agree on and share decisions.
option3: Defer to the product owner and the business to make all the decisions.
option4:
Find an extremely competent person in the project team and delegate
decisions to them.
explanation:
Agile methods aim to empower the team, which means they need to be
effective decision makers. Decisions are often made by either collective
agreement, where the team agrees with the decision or approach, or a
shared decision, where the team problem solve, share input, and arrive
at decisions together.

statement:
You are working on the requirements for the next iteration and a
decision needs to be made by the team. The product owner suggests using
a show of hands to indicate whether team members are for or against the
decision. As the Agile project manager, you object to this approach.
Why?
option1:
Agile is about software, and the team should use software to vote on the
decision.
option2:
The decision may not go in your favour, so it's best to just do it
yourself.
option3: Decisions should take time to work through, not be voted on quickly.
option4:
By making their decisions visible, team members may be influenced by
others in the team (especially executives) without the benefit of
discussion.
explanation:
With just a "for" or "against" vote, there will be no discussion that
might produce a better or alternative decision for the issue. By making
the votes visible, team members are more easily influenced by others'
decisions, especially if they report to them outside of the project
environment.

statement:
An executive in your organisation approaches you about working in an
Agile team. They have heard about Feature Driven Development and would
like to try it as a "test and learn" in your team. What do they mean by
this?
option1: Your team will rely on previous feature designs for your work.
option2: Your team features certain abilities, and showcases them to other teams.
option3:
Your team enjoys the feature of working with Agile in an Agile
organisation.
option4:
Your team will trial developing an overall model, then planning,
designing and building by those features.
explanation:
Agile Feature Driven Development starts with an overall feature model,
then plans, designs and builds by those features. Once a feature is
complete, the process can begin again, complete with any learning from
the previous feature.

statement:
For Kanban or \"Flow based\" Agile, iterations follow the number of
story cards in the Work in Progress limit, shown on the visual or
virtual board. The team pulls cards from the backlog column on the
Kanban board based on their capacity. These story cards provide an
increment of value and are:
option1: the primary driver of executive behaviour
option2: the primary measure of progress
option3: the primary way to pay for the cost of an Agile team
option4: the primary enabler of people's engagement
explanation:
The key to Kanban is making progress visible. Progress naturally
improves team engagement (no one likes being stuck, and blockers are
clear for people to see and assist with).

statement:
You have just come on board an Agile team and a decision needs to be
made about product requirements. The scrum master asks the team to show
their confidence in the decision by holding up their fingers on one hand
(a closed fist through to five fingers). What does it mean if a team
member holds up fewer than three?
option1: The team member doesn't mind about the decision either way.
option2: The team member has a concern or conflict that needs further discussion.
option3: The team member defers to the executive sponsor to decide.
option4:
The decision is outside of the team member's expertise and they choose
not to vote.
explanation:
When voting in an Agile team using the Fist of Five, members who vote
with three or fewer fingers have a concern or conflict with the decision
and would like an opportunity to discuss it further.

statement:
You are at the beginning of a new iteration and about to plan and decide
what gets worked on in the upcoming sprint. You decide to invite all
project stakeholders to the planning meeting to decide what to work on
next. Why is it important that all stakeholders are involved in
decision-making?
option1: So you can keep an eye on what they are doing
option2:
So business representatives can report back to their managers on the
project
option3: So the team feels important, with lots of stakeholders present
option4: So the project stakeholders don't reject a decision that wasn't theirs
explanation:
Giving ownership of decisions to the team and stakeholders is one way to
gain their buy-in. If stakeholders are involved in decision-making, they
will be more committed to any decision made and to the project.

statement:
You are in an Agile team area and notice it is different to other
project areas you have worked on. The project manager tells you there
are certain ways to ensure that stakeholders stay engaged. What is he
talking about?
option1: Removing any team member who is not engaged in the project.
option2: Giving extra work to the better engaged team members.
option3:
Ensuring visibility, transparency and progress of the work through
charts and information in the common team area.
option4:
Offering a possible promotion to the most engaged team member at the end
of the project.
explanation:
Visible progress helps maintain team engagement, and is facilitated
through the use of the information radiator -- Kanban board, burndown
chart, product backlog being clearly visible to all.

statement:
You have been identified as a stakeholder on an Agile software
development team. As you join the team stand-up, you are surprised that
there are only eight people on the team and not more -- after all the
deliverable is quite large. Why is the team so small?
option1:
The Agile methodology is being trialled as a cross-discipline
self-forming team.
option2: One person was selected for each role necessary, and no more.
option3: Agile methods recommend the delivery team to be 12 or fewer members.
option4: Agile methods call for very specialized roles.
explanation:
Agile methods recommend a team of 12 or fewer, typically between 3 and
9. This keeps the communication channels small and ensures information
travels quickly for the team to do their work.

statement:
As the lead on an Agile project, you have carefully cultivated an Agile
team of "T" shaped people, or generalising specialists. What does this
mean?
option1:
A person that has flat knowledge up top, and long knowledge down the
bottom.
option2:
A person with a wide range of general skills (i.e. design, development,
testing) and one deep skill or knowledge area.
option3:
A person with a wide range of leadership skills, as everyone should lead
on an Agile team.
option4:
A person that has more contacts in management layers, and few contacts
in the team layer.
explanation:
A generalising specialist is a person that has one deep knowledge area
or skill, and a wide range of general interests or skills. An Agile team
is ideally made up of generalising specialists who can do more than one
thing, but does their core role well.

statement:
In 2001, a group of individuals representing the most widely used
lightweight software development methodologies agreed on a common set of
values and principles which became known as the Agile Manifesto. In that
manifesto they valued:
option1: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
option2: Executive reporting over bottom up reporting
option3: Scrum masters over project managers
option4: Doing the work over viewing the work
explanation:
The Agile manifesto is made up of four core values, one of which values
Individuals and interactions over processes and tools.

statement:
You are planning the next iteration in your Agile team, as the Agile
lead. The team is going to select user stories from the prioritized
release backlog, elaborate the user stories, and estimate the work
needed for each user story. The number of stories selected is based on:
option1: What the scrum master says, as he or she knows best.
option2:
The team\'s "Velocity", which is the rate at which a team can complete
work.
option3:
What the team wants to work on, to ensure individuals work within their
strengths.
option4: The Feature Driven Development model to be completed.
explanation:
An Agile team's velocity is the amount of work they complete (on
average) in a given iteration. This can be measured in points, cards,
features, stories, T-Shirt sizings or any other method that works for
you. The number of points in a sprint, however, is based on the average
velocity of points from previous iterations.

statement:
Your team is standing around a board with cards on it. Each person is
answering these questions in a round-robin fashion: "What did I complete
since last time? What am I planning to complete between now and next
time? Is anything blocking me?" What is it your team is doing?
option1: A team lunch and learn
option2: A working group meeting
option3: A daily stand-up
option4: A retrospective
explanation:
During the daily stand-up in an Agile team, the team asks in a
round-robin fashion: "What did I complete since last time? What am I
planning to complete between now and next time? Is anything blocking
me?" They also often talk to the cards in-flight on the Kanban board.

statement:
There are multiple project life-cycles you can choose from, depending on
the type of project, environment, deliverable and stakeholders. You are
working on a project that requires features of an overall product to be
delivered on a regular basis. What sort of project lifecycle will you
choose to work with?
option1: Waterfall project lifecycle
option2: Agile project lifecycle
option3: Iterative project lifecycle
option4: Incremental project lifecycle
explanation:
While others may meet the criteria of regularly delivered features, an
Incremental Project Lifecycle specifically delivers features in
"increments" of value over the lifecycle of a project.

statement:
You are working as an Agile project manager in a new team that is not
familiar with Agile. The executives are not as comfortable using Agile
in full just yet. You suggest using a hybrid approach to begin with, to
help the organisation meet their goals. What does this mean?
option1:
A tailored combination of predictive, iterative, incremental, and Agile
approaches.
option2:
Using a project management plan to plan the project, while delivering
that plan in features.
option3: A combination of reactive and proactive project lifecycles.
option4:
Using Agile only when it is needed, such as in a software development
team.
explanation:
A hybrid project lifecycle approach incorporates parts of Predictive
(waterfall), iterative, incremental and Agile. It allows the project
manager to tailor the approach to suit the situation.

statement:
Your Agile team leader works through servant leadership to the team, and
has built a safe environment for disagreement. How has this empowered
the team to move forward without obstacles?
option1:
The team is able to create a list of reasons why tasks cannot be
completed.
option2: The team has no conflict as they get along so well.
option3:
The team leader takes responsibility for all decisions made in the
project.
option4:
The team is encouraged to participate in discussion and disagreement in
order to make better decisions.
explanation:
Constructive discussion and disagreement is encouraged to ensure risks
and alternative ideas are found. This is only maintainable in a team
with high psychological safety.

statement:
A project management office (PMO) is a group or department that defines
and maintains standards for project management within the organization.
What is the primary objective of a PMO?
option1: To support the customers receiving the product
option2: To support the project managers
option3: To support senior management
option4: To support the industry
explanation:
The primary objective of a project management office (PMO) is to support
the project manager.

statement:
You are working on an agile project as a project manager and performing
sprint planning. You are planning the work in detail to be completed
soon, and long term work is planned at a high level. What is this
called?
option1: Detailed Elaboration
option2: Waterfall Planning
option3: Agile Planning
option4: Rolling Wave Planning
explanation:
Rolling Wave Planning is a type of progressive elaboration where work in
the near term is planned in detail, while the work far in the future is
planned at a high level.

statement:
At what stage of a project is a project manager typically assigned to a
project?
option1: The very beginning, during project initiation
option2: The beginning to middle, during project planning
option3: The middle, during project execution
option4: The middle to end, during project monitoring
explanation:
The project manager is typically assigned at the very beginning of a
project, sometimes even having input into the Business Case and Project
Charter.

statement:
You are in the process of identifying the methods your company uses to
accept third party vendors and procure their services. What are you
looking for?
option1: Rating Selection Criteria
option2: Source Selection Criteria
option3: Scoring Selection Criteria
option4: Proposal Selection Criteria
explanation: Selection criteria are created to rate seller proposals.

statement:
Costs can fluctuate during the different phases of your project.
Generally, over the lifecycle of a project, the cost of changes to
project scope will:
option1: decrease
option2: increase
option3: remain about the same
option4: depend on who assigns the change request
explanation:
As a project advances, project changes from scope creep or error
correction have a larger impact on project cost.

statement:
You are a project manager brought on during the very early phase of the
project. The project sponsor has asked you to create a report on whether
this project can be completed in time, at what cost, and what benefit is
expected. What is the Project Sponsor asking for?
option1: A Project Charter
option2: A commercial database
option3: A Feasibility Study
option4: A project management plan
explanation:
A feasibility study forms a part of the Business Case, to determine
whether a project is feasible or not.

statement:
Future Value is the value of today's sum of money, at a specified date
in the future. The Future Value (FV) of \$14,000 invested for 5 years
with interest rate of 5% each year would be:

Your project can be significantly impacted, depending on the
organizational structure you are working in. Which organisational
structure gives you the most control as a project manager?
option1: Strong Matrix
option2: Functional Matrix
option3: Balanced Matrix
option4: PMO
explanation:
While a strong matrix gives you moderate to high control, a Project
Management Office gives you high to almost total control.

statement:
You are working on a project where you have to plan for your current
software product, and a software product in the same area to be released
afterwards. What is your role in this situation?
option1: Functional Manager
option2: Program Manager
option3: Production Manager
option4: Project Manager
explanation:
A program manager oversees a group of related projects that are managed
together, rather than individually.
Present Value = FV / ( 1 + interest rate )\^time

statement:
You are choosing a project for an organization where Project X has an
internal rate of return (IRR) of 8%, Project Y has an IRR of 7%, Project
Z has an IRR of 9%, Project W has an IRR of 11%. Which project would be
the best choice?
option1: Project W
option2: Project X
option3: Project Y
option4: Project Z
explanation:
The project which has the highest internal rate of return (IRR) is the
best choice. So, Project W with an IRR of 11% would be the best choice.

statement:
You are a project manager and meeting the stakeholders along with your
team. What is the first meeting of stakeholders and team members, which
provides an opportunity to clarify roles and ask questions called?
option1: Meeting
option2: Kick-Off
option3: Launch
option4: Kick-On
explanation:
The kick-off meeting with the stakeholders and team members involves
information on roles, planning, and scheduling during project
initiation.

statement:
You are working in the procurement department for your project. One
vendor is trying to give you an expensive gift with the understanding it
would help them get the contract. What would you do if there are no
rules regarding gifts in your company?
option1: Ask the vendor for an additional gift for your boss also
option2: Don\'t accept the gift
option3: Accept the gift and help them, it is a nice gesture
option4: Seek advice from your boss
explanation:
It is your responsibility when managing procurements to avoid any
conflict of interest.

statement:
You have been working as a project manager for some time, and have found
different types of resistance to the change brought about by your
various projects. What is the main source of resistance for any change
in an organization?
option1: Project
option2: Process
option3: Technology
option4: People
explanation:
People demonstrate the strongest resistance to change in an
organization.

statement:
You are working on a project and your project sponsor asks you to
contribute to a decision on whether to continue the project. The project
had an initial budget of \$500,000, is only half complete and has spent
\$1,000,000. Should the organization consider the \$1,000,000 when
determining whether to continue?
option1: Ask the Project Team to vote so the process is democratic
option2: No, the money spent is gone
option3: Yes, that is our money spent, we should consider it
option4: Refer to the functional manager as they own the business unit
explanation:
The \$1,000,000 represents "Sunk Costs", which are never a consideration
in the decision for a project to continue.

statement:
You are working as a project manager, asking for proposals from a Third
Parties to complete the work. Your brother works for one of the vendors
bidding for the contract. You are part of the executive team which will
accept a vendor. What do you do?
option1: Discourage the vendor bid as it would be a conflict of interest
option2: Help the your brother's vendor in the bid so you can work together
option3: Inform your company and the vendor of the relationship
option4: Don't tell anyone. Why complicate things?
explanation:
Since you are part of the executive project management team and have a
conflict of interest, you must inform your company and the vendor of the
relationship, which will in turn inform the decision.

statement:
You are working as a project manager in a foreign country. It is
customary that gifts be made to the government to obtain the necessary
project approvals, however the country where your company is based does
not allow this practice. What should you do?
option1: Don't bother with getting project approvals
option2: Ensure you offer gifts to obtain the project approvals
option3: Allocate additional hidden funds in the project to obtain the gifts
option4: Do not offer gifts to obtain project approvals
explanation:
You have an obligation to comply with the law of your base country, so
do not offer gifts to obtain project approvals.

statement:
You are working on a complex project requiring expertise from the
functional manager. What is your role as a project manager in a weak
matrix organization?
option1: Coordinator
option2: Administrator
option3: Supporter
option4: Functional manager
explanation:
The project manager role is more of a coordinator or an expediter than a
complete project manager in a weak matrix organization.

statement:
Project Constraints are any restriction that defines a project's
limitations and is the limit of what the project is expected to
accomplish. Which of the following is one of the types of competing
Project Constraints?
option1: Size
option2: Sponsor
option3: Quality
option4: Location
explanation:
The different types of competing Project Constraints: 1. Scope 2.
Quality 3. Schedule 4. Budget (Cost)

statement:
You have been promoted from your role of project manager into a
portfolio manager role. A portfolio is work grouped together to meet a
certain strategic business objective and it involves:
option1: Projects and programs
option2: Projects and operations
option3: Programs and operations
option4: Projects, programs, and operations
explanation:
According to PMI and its PMBOK Guide, a portfolio includes, "Projects,
programs, other portfolios, and operations managed as a group to achieve
strategic objectives."

statement:
You are working as a project manager and created the cost estimate to
bid on a government contract, where the scope was set by the government.
Your manager advises you to reduce the cost to win the contract by
whatever means, but your analysis shows that reducing the cost estimate
will make the project unable to meet the specified scope. What should
you do?
option1: Reduce the cost estimate and submit it
option2:
Formally communicate to your manager about the implications of reducing
the cost
option3: Ask your manager to estimate instead
option4: Submit your estimate without reducing the cost
explanation:
It is your duty as a responsible project manager to be accurate and
truthful in your estimate to meet the expected scope, and direct and
collaborative in your dealings with others.

statement:
Work Performance Data generally contains raw facts and figures such as
start dates for various activities, the number of bugs found in the
software, spent cost, etc. What is a type of Work Performance Data?
option1: Forecasted estimates to complete
option2: On-Track status for change requests
option3: Number of work packages physically completed
option4: The Schedule Variance of the project
explanation:
Work Performance Data are the raw measures of the project. They
include: 1. Actual Work Completed 2. Actual Start and Finish dates of
schedule activities 3. Story points completed 4. Number of change
requests 5. Number of defects 6. Actual costs incurred 7. Actual
durations

statement:
As you work on projects they can be influenced by many different things,
and many different Enterprise Environmental Factors. What sort of
influence are Marketplace conditions on your project?
option1: External
option2: Internal
option3: Regulatory
option4: Public
explanation:
Although external factors are not under your control, or the control of
the organization, they can have as much impact on your ability to
execute a project.

statement:
You have recently been promoted from your role as Project Manager to a
role that manages a related group of projects, and ensures efficient use
of resources among those projects to ensure they are delivered. What is
your role?
option1: Functional Manager
option2: Project Manager
option3: Portfolio Manager
option4: Program Manager
explanation:
The Program Manager role manages related groups of projects to ensure
effective management and resource allocation.

statement:
Different Project Management Office types have different levels of
control and influence on projects within the organization. What is one
of the types of PMO Structures?
option1: Passive
option2: Controlling
option3: Global
option4: Active
explanation:
The different types of PMO Structures are: 1. Supportive 2. Controlling
3. Directive

statement:
You are working on a project in the construction industry and your
project sponsor has allocated a fixed budget and tight timeline to
complete the work. What are the limitations placed upon the project that
the project manager and team must work within?
option1: Restrictions
option2: Limitations
option3: Considerations
option4: Constraints
explanation:
The traditional project constraints are Scope, Cost, Time, and Quality
and they must be managed and balanced as you complete your project.

statement:
Your Project Management Office recommends the use of project management
templates and checklists to plan, implement and manage your projects
professionally. These templates are an example of:
option1: Organizational Governance Meetings (OGM)
option2: Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats (SWOT)
option3: Enterprise Environmental Factors (EEF)
option4: Organizational Process Assets (OPA)
explanation:
Templates and checklists are examples of Organizational Process Assets,
that can help reduce the amount of time required in the planning
process.

statement:
Work Performance Data are the raw observations of your project, without
analysing it against other metrics. What is one of the types of Work
Performance Data?
option1: Implementation status for change requests
option2: Forecasted estimates to complete
option3: Number of defects
option4: Status of deliverables
explanation:
The different types of Work Performance Data from the PMBOK are: 1.
Percent of work physically completed 2. Quality and technical
performance measures 3. Start and finish dates of schedule activities 4.
Number of change requests 5. Number of defects 6. Actual costs 7. Actual
durations

statement:
A Work Performance Report is a document containing organized information
about project performance in a report format to enable decision making &
actions and project communications. Which of the following is a type of
Work Performance Report?
option1: Status reports
option2: Status of deliverables
option3: Forecasted estimates to complete
option4: Implementation status for change requests
explanation:
The different types of Work Performance Report from the PMBOK: 1. Status
reports 2. Memos 3. Justifications 4. Information notes 5. Electronic
dashboards 6. Recommendations 7. Updates

statement:
You are working as a Project Manager and one of the stakeholders of the
project raises a problem with the product requirements, stating they are
not correct. Which document will you use to note this for further
discussion and resolution?
option1: Change Request
option2: Benefits Management Plan
option3: Issue Log
option4: Requirements Traceability Matrix
explanation:
The Issue Log is a document used to write down and monitor issues that
are under discussion between the team members, and options for
resolution.

statement:
You are working on a project in the financial sector, and the project
sponsor asks you for a written document that outlines the intention of
the project with set terms and conditions so she can accept. What is she
referring to?
option1: Collaterals
option2: Indemnitees
option3: Settlements
option4: Agreements
explanation:
Agreements are the formal written documents between two or more people
or groups about the intention of a project with set terms and
conditions.

statement:
New projects go through the Tuckman Model of forming, storming, norming,
and performing. You have just taken the lead of a new project with a
newly formed team, but are surprised to find little conflict and that
the forming stage has gone so quickly. Which of the following might be
the reason?
option1:
The team members have worked together on previous projects and are
following the same Agile ceremonies and methods.
option2: You have made all the project decisions so they don't have to worry.
option3: The team is in line for a promotion, so they want to get along.
option4: The team members really care about the product being delivered.
explanation:
It is important to be aware of the Tuckman model on a new project. Using
the existing Agile methodology and ceremonies can help smooth the
process of coming together, as can having a team who have worked
together on other projects before.

statement:
You are the project manager of a new Agile project for your
organization. You ask questions of your team to elicit their needs and
help them problem solve issues as they arise. What role are you filling
in the Agile team?
option1: Project director
option2: Project coach
option3: Project support
option4: Project executive
explanation:
The project lead can be called many things in Agile but usually centres
around servant leadership and a project coach role, who helps grow the
team and remove blockers to their work.

statement:
You are an Agile project manager leading a new software project. During
the last retrospective there was very little engagement and feedback,
and the team seem to be losing interest in the work. What will you do to
improve the situation?
option1: Hold another retrospective to try and extract more information.
option2: Align the project goals to each team members' personal goals.
option3: Hold a vote on whether to continue the project or not.
option4:
Get executive management involved so team members raise their
engagement.
explanation:
If a previous Retrospective did not elicit information, holding another
one would be doing the same thing but expecting a different result.
Finding out each team member's strengths and helping them do more of
