that work helps engagement, as does aligning their goals to those of the
project. When these things are aligned people naturally work harder and
care more.

statement:
You are working as a project manager in a team that is moving to an
Agile way of work. You have previously engaged the team in a single
weekly meeting, with email as the primary communication. What changes
will you make in the new Agile team?
option1: Ensure reports are created weekly for executive management.
option2: Set up a teleconference with different parts of your project team.
option3:
Co-locate the team where possible and focus on frequent face-to-face
communication.
option4:
Have frequent one-on-ones with your project team to get their point of
view in private.
explanation:
Agile focuses on individuals and interaction, co-locating the team to
gain information by osmosis, with face-to-face discussions being the
recommended method of communication.

statement:
Kanban translates to "visual sign" or card, in Japanese. It is a form of
visual management taken from Lean Manufacturing, for monitoring ...
option1: Work in Progress and enabling "Pull" and "Flow"
option2: the team and enabling executive reporting
option3: work velocity and enabling story card elaboration
option4: the scrum master and enabling team participation
explanation:
Kanban is a framework from the Toyota Production System, utilised in
software development and knowledge work. It focuses on the Kanban board,
which shows and limits current work in progress.

statement:
You are working with an Agile team who use Kanban to manage the flow of
work through the team. The Scrum Master or Agile lead mentions the
concept of "pull", which helps them manage the work. What does this
mean?
option1:
The team gathers around the team velocity board each morning to ensure
work is being done.
option2:
The team pulls work only when they are ready, instead of work in
progress constantly building up.
option3:
The team works with a pull and push approach between the developers and
testers.
option4:
The Scrum Master tells the team to pull the appropriate levers that
enable the right features to be developed.
explanation:
Kanban allows team members to pull new work onto the Kanban board when
they are ready, limits the amount of work on that board, and encourages
an optimal flow of work.

statement:
You are walking through the next feature with the product owner in an
Agile team. He is new to Agile, and unsure of what Kanban really is. He
lists a few things for you to check. Which of the below is NOT one of
the core properties of Kanban?
option1: Visualise the workflow and limit work in progress.
option2: Manage Flow and Enable \"Pull".
option3: Implement feedback loops and improve collaboratively.
option4: Follow procedures and don\'t deviate from the plan.
explanation:
Kanban is a method borrowed from the Toyota Production System, or Lean.
It focuses on limiting the work in progress so work is focusses,
ensuring feedback loops are in place, and making the work visible so
blockages are clear.

statement:
You are working on an Agile project as an Agile project manager. You
have been working using Kanban to monitor the work and keep it visible
for all to see. What is NOT a defining principle of Kanban?
option1: Start with current state and respect the current process.
option2: Agree to pursue incremental, evolutionary change.
option3: Lead at all levels.
option4: Directive leadership over wasteful team participation.
explanation:
Kanban, like Agile itself, encourages team participation over directive
leadership. It keeps the work visible to make blockers clear, so
everyone can assist in removing them and keeping work flowing through
the team.

statement:
In 2001, a group of individuals, representing the most widely used
lightweight software development methodologies, agreed on a common set
of values and principles which became known as the Agile Manifesto.
Which of the following is one of the Agile Manifesto\'s core values?
option1: Directive leading over servant leading
option2: Working software over comprehensive documentation
option3: Making large useful features over making small increments
option4: Pushing work through quickly over limiting work in progress
explanation:
The Agile Manifesto focuses on four core value statements, one of which
is "Working software over comprehensive documentation".

statement:
You are asked to decide which project is a better investment. Project X
takes 3 years to complete with a Net Present Value of \$125,000 and
Project Y takes 2 years to complete with a Net Present Value of
\$100,000. Which will you choose?
option1: Both projects
option2: Project X only
option3: Either project
option4: Project Y only
explanation:
The NPV calculation takes the number of years into consideration
already. This makes Project X the right choice, which has the higher NPV
of \$125,000.

statement:
You are a certified PMP working as a project manager in a company. One
of your colleagues is planning to take the PMP exam too, and asks for
your guidance on preparation. What should you do?
option1: Give them a few instructions and let them work out the rest
option2: Say you're too busy to help them - after all, it's true
option3: Give them whatever guidance you can provide based on your experience
option4: Tell them to go to the PMI website and find the information they want
explanation:
Part of your responsibility as a PMP holder is to help guide those who
are taking it after you, in order to give back to the profession.

statement:
What is the Cost Performance Index of a project with a PV of \$795,000,
EV of \$850,000, and AC of \$950,000?
option1: 1.12, you are over budget
option2: 0.89, you are over budget
option3: 2.05, you are under budget
option4: 0.75, you are under budget
explanation:
In Earned Value Management, the Cost Performance Index is calculated via
the formula EV / AC = 0.89 A figure less than 1 means you have delivered
less than you have paid for -- which means you have paid more, and are
over budget.

statement:
Return on Investment (ROI) is the percentage return we get from
investing in something. Your project cost the company \$200,000 to
complete. That project returns a benefit of \$240,000. The net Return on
Investment (ROI) would be:
explanation:
Return on Investment: = NET Income / Total Investment = (\$240,000 -
\$200,000) / \$200,000 x 100 = 20%

statement:
The Benefit to Cost Ratio is used to compare the benefits to the costs
for a project. As a project manager working for your company which one
of the following projects will you recommend based on the BCRs below?
option1: Project X at 0.75
option2: Project Y at 1.20
option3: Project R at 1.85
option4: Project W at 2.05
explanation:
The Benefit to Cost ratio = Benefit / Cost. A higher value is better,
making 2.05 the correct answer.

statement:
The Benefit to Cost Ratio (BCR) is the ratio of the benefits of a
project relative to its costs. You are working on a project with a total
cost of \$200,000. That project results in a benefit of \$190,000. What
is the Benefit Cost Ratio?
explanation: The Benefit to Cost ratio is Benefit / Cost. = 190,000 / 200,000 = 0.95

statement:
A recent news article spread a rumour about a supplier who is providing
services for the project you are working on. They are reportedly in the
middle of a hostile takeover bid from another larger company. You need
to ensure you project is able to continue. What should you do?
option1: Ignore the news article - they don't know the supplier like you do
option2: Set a meeting and talk to executives in charge at the hostile bidder
option3: Talk to the vendor and find the facts
option4:
Stop working with the vendor immediately, get another vendor to do the
work
explanation:
The best approach as a Project Manager is always "Direct, and
Collaborative." Raise the issue with the vendor directly and work out an
approach to control the risk of the takeover from there.

statement:
You are asked to select a new project for your company. Project X will
take 3 years to complete with Net Present Value of \$101,000. Project Y
will take 2 years to complete with Net Present Value of \$95,000. Which
should you select?
option1: Project Y
option2: Project X
option3: Both projects
option4: Either project
explanation:
The NPV calculation takes the number of years into consideration
already, making Project X the right choice with the higher NPV of
\$101,000. NPV = (Cash flows)/( 1+r)\^t

statement:
What is the Opportunity Cost of selecting Project X over Project Y, when
Project X has a NPV of \$124,000 and Project Y has a NPV of \$179,000?
explanation:
The Opportunity Cost is the value of the project not selected. In this
case, Project X was chosen, which makes the cost of Project Y the
opportunity cost of \$179,000.

statement:
The Benefit Cost Ratio or Profitability Index is commonly used to
identify the most efficient projects. What does a benefit cost ratio of
1.45 imply?
option1: Cost is 1.45 times the profit
option2: Cost is 1.45 times the revenue
option3: Profit is 1.45 times the cost
option4: Revenue is 1.45 times the cost
explanation:
The BCR is the ratio of the benefits of a project relative to its costs.
Revenue is money coming in, before costs, taxes or other expenses.
Therefore, the BCR of 1.45 implies that revenue is 1.45 times the cost.

statement:
One or more deliverables are produced at the end of a project phase.
Multiple phases make up a project lifecycle. What is the completion of a
project phase also called?
option1: Kill Points
option2: Task Points
option3: Feature Items
option4: Release Items
explanation:
The kill point is the stage gate or phase review. At the review, the
progress of the project is evaluated and a decision is made whether to
continue or cancel the project.

statement:
Every project has essential milestones and business goals to meet. A
project is divided into parts, called:
option1: Tasks
option2: Work Breakdown Structure
option3: Phases
option4: Releases
explanation: Multiple project phases form the overall project life cycle.

statement:
You are a project manager responsible for delivering a new mobile app.
You are working on the project part-time, and must ask the functional
manager for each of your decisions. What type of organizational
structure are you working in?
option1: Weak Matrix
option2: Strong Matrix
option3: Balanced Matrix
option4: Project Management Office
explanation:
In a Weak Matrix organization, the project manager has little direct
authority, works more as a project co-ordinator, and is often part time
(and assigned to other tasks as well).

statement:
You are working on a global project in other country, where it is
customary to honour the project manager of a successful project with
gifts. The company you are working for has a policy to never accept
gifts as it may be a conflict of interest. What should you do in this
circumstance?
option1: Accept the gift from the customer, it would be rude not to
option2: Tell the customer you don't accept gifts
option3:
Discuss the situation with your manager and agree what to do -- you must
be aware of social customs in other countries but your company's rules
take precedence over others.
option4: Accept the gift from the customer and give it to somebody else
explanation:
Although you must be aware of customs and social needs in other
countries, you company policy takes precedence over other rules when you
are working abroad. At most you should check with your manager and
decide on the action to take.

statement:
A risk is an undetermined event or condition that can have a positive or
negative effect on at least one project objective if it occurs. During
which part of your project is the risk of the project failure highest?
option1: The beginning, when initiating your project
option2: The middle, when planning your project
option3: The end, when closing your project
option4: The middle, when executing your project
explanation:
Risk of project failure is at its highest at the beginning of a project,
and decreases as items are complete or delivered, until project closure.

statement:
Two team members in your project are having issues working together. You
discuss this problem with the functional manager and jointly agree on a
solution. What type of organizational structure are you working in?
option1: Project oriented
option2: Strong Matrix
option3: Balanced Matrix
option4: Weak Matrix
explanation:
The project manager and functional manager in a balanced matrix have
equal authority, the power is shared between them.

statement:
You as a project manager are listing the things that must be done to
close out the project. What should you do in regards to the contracts
for your third party suppliers?
option1:
Issue a formal written notice of the project completion to the
contractors
option2:
Put a legal notice in the newspapers indicating that all invoices must
be submitted
option3: Request final inspection reports for all vendor-supplied materials
option4: Issue letters of recommendation for the project team
explanation:
To close out the project, a formal and written notice about the contract
completion must be provided to the seller or supplier.

statement:
One of your stakeholders needs to make a proposal for modification to
the scope, policies, procedures, plans, or processes of the project,
after a formal review of the issue. What will they use?
option1: Rectification Requests
option2: Change Requests
option3: Adjust Requests
option4: Amend Requests
explanation:
Change Requests are the proposals for modification to be made in the
scope, policies, procedures, plans, or processes of the project after a
formal review -- most often raised as an issue through the issue log.

statement:
What is the term used for any product, service or results to be achieved
to complete a process, phase or project?
option1: Milestone
option2: Result
option3: Deliverable
option4: Delivery
explanation:
A deliverable is the term used for any product, service or result to be
achieved to complete a process, phase or project.

statement:
You are working as a project manager and you need a source of
information and knowledge on a product to ensure project success. You
recognise this as "Expert Judgement" from the PMBOK Guide. What is the
first place you should look for expert judgment?
option1: The vendor's organization
option2: Hire someone outside your organization
option3: Your own organization
option4: Ask the Government
explanation:
Often the best expertise in a process or product lies within your own
organization, including people who have been through similar projects.

statement:
You have been asked to use an estimation technique that uses data from
past projects to budget for an activity that is very similar in nature
and duration. What is this type of estimating technique?
option1: Corresponding Estimating
option2: Analogous Estimating
option3: Parallel Estimating
option4: Symmetrical Estimating
explanation:
Analogous Estimating is an estimation technique that uses data from past
projects for an activity that is very similar in nature for cost or
duration.

statement:
You have been asked to prepare a document to gain the project sponsor's
sign off to access their function's resources, that details the project
goal and business case, high-level scope and requirements, high level
dates and milestones, a high-level budget, and initial risk analysis.
What are you creating?
option1: Project Management Plan
option2: Project Charter
option3: Risk Management Plan
option4: Lessons Learned
explanation:
The Project Charter is a document used to initiate a project and gain
access to a function's resources. It details the; Project Objective and
Title/Name, Start date of the project, Business need and case,
High-level scope and requirements, expected outcome and deliverables,
High-level budget, Project manager and other resources, Stakeholders
Details, Constraints and Assumptions, Sponsor Signoff and more.

statement:
You are working on an Agile project and have been asked to create a
document that lists team principles, agreements, and guidelines about
the acceptable behaviour by team members. What are you working on?
option1: The Team Guideline
option2: The Project Management Plan
option3: The Project Charter
option4: The Team Charter
explanation:
The Team Charter is a document that lists team principles, agreements,
instructions and guidelines about the acceptable behaviour by project
team members.

statement:
You are managing a project team and need to ensure your team members are
doing what is needed to get the job done. What is a project document you
will use that helps you know the effectiveness of the team?
option1: Team Performance Assessments
option2: Team Progress Assessments
option3: Team Presentation Assessments
option4: Team Achievement Assessments
explanation:
Team Performance Assessments are a project tool and technique you will
use to assess team performance and know the effectiveness of the team.

statement:
You are working in the risk area of a project. The project work is half
way complete and dozens of new risks have been found that could
jeopardise your project. The risk plan is not yet complete. When should
risk management planning have been completed?
option1: At the beginning of your project, during initiation and planning
option2: In the middle of your project, during execution
option3: When monitoring your project -- you can analyse what isn't created yet
option4:
When the project is finalised -- as you will know the risks that have
occurred.
explanation:
The Risk Management Plan drives and supports other risk management
processes, so it should be done as early as possible.

statement:
You are working as a project manager, at the stage where you need to
validate the scope and products you have created with the appropriate
people. What is the output called that is produced by a project and
approved by the end-user or customer?
option1: Accepted Products
option2: Accepted Deliverables
option3: Accepted Results
option4: Accepted Data
explanation:
Accepted Deliverables is the output produced by a project and approved
by the end-user or customer.

statement:
You are working on a major project for your company, where only 50% of
the project has been completed, while 80% of the project budget has been
spent. When making a decision on whether or not to continue, what are
the costs called that have already been spent?
option1: Earned Value
option2: Exception Report
option3: Sunk Costs
option4: Milestone
explanation:
Money that has already been spent is called the sunk cost. Cost already
spent cannot change and should not be considered for decision-making.

statement:
You are working on a brand new project and need to estimate how much it
will cost. You have the cost per unit for all items needed. What is the
name of the estimating technique you will use?
option1: Three point estimating
option2: Executive estimating
option3: Data estimating
option4: Parametric estimating
explanation:
Parametric Estimating is a technique to estimate cost or duration using
parameters, such as cost per meter (foot) or cost her hour.

statement:
You are working on a project and wanting to plan the immediate work in
detail, while leaving the remaining work at a higher level. What are you
doing?
option1: Executive Planning
option2: Rolling Wave Planning
option3: Progressive Planning
option4: Extra Planning
explanation:
Rolling Wave Planning is a technique to plan in detail level for work
which is to be taken up early and planning for remaining work at a
higher level.

statement:
You are working through the scope of your project and wanting to
decompose the high level features into smaller pieces of work that a
team can deliver using a work breakdown structure. What is that level of
the work breakdown structure called?
option1: Work Package
option2: Work Product
option3: Work Deliverable
option4: Work Task
explanation:
The work package is the lowest level in a Work Breakdown Structure and
is assigned to a team or person to deliver.

statement:
Your project needs to hire resources from outside the organisation, and
the vendor has provided a Cost Plus Fixed Fee (CPFF) type of contract
with a target cost of \$280,000 plus a fixed fee of \$29,000. What is
the total cost if the project costs at the end is \$250,000?
explanation:
A cost plus fixed fee (CPFF) contract is where the buyer pays for the
seller\'s allowable costs plus a fixed amount of profit (fee). For this
contract the total of the contract would be: = \$250,000 + \$29,000 =
\$279,000

statement:
As a project manager you are aware that communication can become more
complex, the more people there are to communicate to. How many different
communication channels would there be with a project team of 10 people?
explanation:
Communication Channels = n x (n - 1) / 2 = 10 x (10 - 1) / 2 = 10 x 9 /
2 = 90 / 2 = 45

statement:
You have gathered the requirements and scope for your project, broken
them down into work packages for your project teams and then into
activities that need to be completed and scheduled. What will you use to
find the minimum duration for your project, and any flexibility you have
between activities?
option1: Qualitative analysis
option2: Critical path method
option3: Resource levelling
option4: Variance analysis
explanation:
Critical Path Method is used to find the minimum total duration for a
project by finding the longest sequence of tasks that must be completed.
Other sequences may be shorter and have some wiggle room against the
longer one, which is called "float".

statement:
You are working through your project and performing quality inspections
on the product. During inspection, you notice a key part of the product
is likely to be defective in the future. The team brainstormed, removed
some scope and managed to make the product work without the defective
component. This is:
option1: Risk mitigation
option2: Transfer of risk
option3: Risk avoidance
option4: Work-around
explanation:
Risk avoidance involves taking focussed action to reduce the negative
effect of uncertainty on the project, e.g. the removal of high risk
scope elements from the project. Process Groups Practice Guide (2022),
page 303.

statement:
You are working on a project and it has become apparent that part of the
product your company was creating they won't be able to complete. You
decide to outsource it to a vendor, and offer a bonus to the vendor if
the work is completed in two months. What type of contract will you use?
option1: CPFF (Cost plus fixed fee)
option2: FPWEA (Fixed price with economic price adjustments)
option3: T & M (Time and materials)
option4: FPIF (Fixed price incentive fee)
explanation:
Fixed price with an incentive fee allows the buyer to allocate an
incentive amount, usually tied to metrics around schedule, cost or
quality.

statement:
You are arranging a third party to perform work for your project. You
are creating a document outlining the details of the work to be
completed and the timeframe it needs to be delivered in. This is called
the procurement:
option1: Obtainment Of Work
option2: Recovery Of Work
option3: Statement of Work
option4: Executive Work
explanation:
The Procurement Statement of Work is a document detailing the
procurements needed with sufficient details in scope, time and quality
requirements.

statement:
You are a project manager working in manufacturing and you need to
estimate the resources you will need for the next phase of your project.
What is a fast estimation technique that uses data from past projects
for an activity that is very similar in nature for cost or duration?
option1: Related Estimating
option2: Parallel Estimating
option3: Analogous Estimating
option4: Comparable Estimating
explanation:
Analogous Estimating is an estimation technique that uses data from past
projects for an activity that is very similar in nature for cost or
duration.

statement:
You are scheduling a meeting with your project team to share their
viewpoints and expertise in the project work. Which of the following is
NOT one of the rules of effective Meetings?
option1: Schedule recurring meetings in advance
option2: Set a time limit and keep to it
option3: Ensure flexible time limits
option4: Stick to the agenda
explanation:
The different rules of Meetings: 1. Set a time limit and keep to it 2.
Schedule recurring meetings in advance 3. Meet with the team regularly
but not too often 4. Have a purpose for each meeting 5. Create an agenda
with team input 6. Distribute the agenda before hand 7. Stick to the
agenda 8. Let people know their responsibilities in advance 9. Bring the
right people together

statement:
You have been assigned to a new project, where the work is behind
schedule and the team are not working together well. The team is
relatively new, and conflicts are arising. Team members are blaming each
other for work not done, and some people are not showing up to work on
the project at all. What will you do next?
option1:
Meet with the functional manager to agree on the team members' time
requirements.
option2:
Meet with your team to set a team charter and define roles &
responsibilities.
option3:
Meet with the project sponsor to gain additional funding for resources
who can work together.
option4:
Escalate the issue to the steering committee as soon as possible as a
project risk.
explanation:
The Standard for Project Management asks that we create a collaborative
team environment, including team agreements, definitions of roles &
responsibilities, formal committees tasked with specific objectives, and
standing meetings that regularly review a given topic. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p28, p29, "Create a Collaborative Team
Environment"

statement:
You have recently completed a project for a new product and have been
asked to manage it after its release to keep it relevant in today's
marketplace. Half of your existing project team are rolling off to other
projects soon, and your project budget is nearly depleted. What will you
do next?
option1:
Create a business case and project charter for your project sponsor to
approve.
option2:
Retain as many of your current project staff to continue work on the
product.
option3:
Raise a change request for your current project to add scope and
continue making changes.
option4:
Secure funding for a stable team, then use current research to form a
suite of projects to improve the product.
explanation:
This question is referring to product management. Product management
involves the integration of people, data, processes and business systems
to develop or maintain a product or service throughout its lifecycle.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p17-19, " Product Management
Considerations"

statement:
You are delivering a critical change and several key stakeholders in
your organisation have added seemingly unnecessary steps for you to
take, with extra approvals, reports and meetings. They are also
restricting the people you need from working on your project in a larger
capacity. What will you do next?
option1:
Go directly to the people you need to avoid extra back-and-forth with
the stakeholders.
option2:
Raise a risk in the risk register on the lack of access to sufficient
resources.
option3:
Communicate more often with the stakeholders and gain a deeper awareness
of their ideas and situation.
option4:
Show your stakeholders the Resource Assignment Matrix and project roles
& responsibilities.
explanation:
This question refers to the principle of Effectively Engaging with
Stakeholders. Stakeholders may add steps or requirements, or restrict
team members if not engaged appropriately. Communication and awareness
of their ideas, through knowledge sharing and regular meetings (ideally
face to face) help. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p32, 33,
"Effectively Engage with Stakeholders."

statement:
You are managing a project team mostly made up of men. Recently Jane was
hired for her high expertise in the brands that make up your industry.
You notice she has been left out of some key meetings, and others are
overlooking her opinions. You also notice she is paid significantly less
than her male colleagues. What will you do next?
option1:
Make steps to update her pay to the same range as the others on your
project, and ask her opinion and advice specifically during each meeting
(along with others in your team).
option2:
Take the lower pay as a win for your project, as it will help keep costs
low and her colleagues don't need to know.
option3:
Ask Jane to write down her knowledge of the industry, just in case she
leaves.
option4:
No need to rock the boat -- this is just how people work in this
industry.
explanation:
This question is on Integrity and Stewardship. We should have respectful
engagement of project team members, including their compensation, access
to opportunity and fair treatment. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition,
2021, p25, "Be a diligent, respectful and caring steward".

statement:
During one of your working group meetings, a stakeholder raises the
concern that the product you are working on will be discontinued in the
next five months. You were not aware of this, and your project budget
has already been approved until the scheduled delivery, which is in five
months also. What will you do next?
option1:
Keep the project going as it is -- the discontinued product isn't your
responsibility.
option2:
Set a meeting with your project sponsor to share the information and a
recommendation to terminate the project.
option3:
Change the scope of the project to meet a different product so your
project can remain relevant.
option4:
Ask for additional funding to see if you can improve the product instead
of discontinuing it.
explanation:
This question is about delivering value -- the primary motivator for a
project. If the project is no longer aligned with the business need or
it seems unlikely to provide value, the organisation may choose to
terminate the effort. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p35,
"Focus on Value"

statement:
You have collected the requirements for your project and are creating
the scope statement and work breakdown structure. During this process
you notice some functionality in your database system that will help a
different project you are aware of within your Project Management Office
(PMO). What will you do next?
option1:
Place the feature in the other project's backlog, and ask them to
prioritise it as soon as possible.
option2:
Raise the functionality in your risk register as a threat, with the
response to "mitigate".
option3: Add the scope to your project and complete it as soon as possible.
option4:
Raise the functionality in your risk register as an opportunity with the
response to "exploit", and meet with the other project team and sponsor
to discuss.
explanation:
Systems thinking involves taking a holistic view of how project parts
interact with each other, and external systems. Projects operate within
programs, and small systems affect larger ones. Finding a previously
unknown opportunity, the next best step is to make it known and exploit
the opportunity. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p39, "Respond
to System Interactions: Systems Thinking" p125, "Opportunities"

statement:
You are working on a high profile project. Several high-ranking senior
executives want to take the credit for this initiative, while limiting
the impact to their own resources. This has led to several conflicts and
a scope statement that is unclear, leading to a risk of project failure.
What will you do next?
option1: Create a scope statement yourself to avoid further conflict.
option2:
Facilitate a discussion with the executives as a neutral third party,
focusing on agreed project goals.
option3:
Limit your project resources to the executives until they can agree on a
way forward.
option4:
Escalate the issue to the project steering committee and ask them to
resolve the problem.
explanation:
Leadership is not the same as authority. When senior managers suffer
conflict over priorities, neutral facilitation helps more than detailed
recommendations. Leadership acumen involves focusing a team around
agreed goals, generating consensus on the way forward, negotiating and
resolving conflict, and more. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p42, "Demonstrate leadership behaviours"

statement:
You are managing a project where the majority of the product has been
planned up-front, but an external system is needed from a vendor in
partial pieces where the requirements are unclear. Half of your
stakeholders want to use a waterfall approach, and the other half are
advocating for an Agile approach. What will you do next?
option1:
Use an Agile methodology. The fixed scope is low risk, and you can
capture changes using Agile.
option2:
Use a Waterfall methodology and ask the team to plan the uncertain scope
better.
option3:
Proceed with a hybrid method to start, with pre-planned scope but Agile
ceremonies that promote feedback.
option4:
Use a combination of Waterfall and Agile to capture all the requirements
and keep stakeholders happy.
explanation:
Each project is unique, and success is based on adapting to the unique
context of the project to determine the most appropriate methods. The
tailoring approach is iterative in nature and refined during the project
cycle. This project requires a hybrid of waterfall and agile, with the
option to improve it over time. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p44-46, "Tailoring"

statement:
You are working on a project where senior users have been testing the
product as parts are released. They have found some missing requirements
and defects. Their manager is worried that the project won't deliver a
product that is fit for purpose, without defects and within the
timeframe needed. What will you do next?
option1: Perform some of the testing yourself to ensure a quality product.
option2:
Help every team member to focus on quality, from correct acceptance
criteria to a developed and tested product.
option3:
Limit testing until the very end of the project so you can do it all in
one go when the product is completely ready.
option4:
Increase the testing on your project and add more testing resources so
no defect goes unnoticed.
explanation:
Quality focuses on meeting acceptance criteria for deliverables, and
satisfying stakeholders' expectations on product requirements. Quality
is everyone's responsibility in an Adaptive (Agile) project. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p47-49, "Build quality into processes
and deliverables."

statement:
You are working through the risk assessment with your team, who are
having trouble coming up with ideas for risks. You decide to give them
some guidance on types of complexity that might affect your new product.
What will you do next?
option1: Ask them to brainstorm Qualitative and Quantitative risks.
option2: Ask them to perform the Five Whys and Ishikawa analysis.
option3:
Ask them to brainstorm around possible unwanted human behaviour, system
behaviour, ambiguity and technical innovation impacts.
option4:
Ask them to perform a retrospective to discover what isn't working well,
and what still puzzles us.
explanation:
Complexity is often the result of human behaviour, system interactions,
technical innovation, uncertainty and ambiguity. Complexity can be
introduced by events or conditions that affect value, scope,
stakeholders, risk and more.

statement:
You are working on a project where you discover a certain negative risk
with a potential impact of \$2,000,000. You meet with your project
stakeholders who determine that this is within their risk appetite,
provided you meet a risk threshold of 10%. What will you do next?
option1: Note the acceptable risk level as a threat of \$200,000 (10%).
option2:
Note the acceptable risk level as a threat of \$1,800,000 to \$2,200,00
(10% variance).
option3: Note the impact as outside the acceptable risk level of \$2,000,000.
option4: Note the impact as an opportunity of \$200,000 (10%).
explanation:
Risk appetite describes the level of uncertainty or risk your
stakeholders are willing to accept. Risk threshold is the measure of
acceptable variation around an objective that reflects the risk
appetite. In this scenario a 10% variation around \$2m is \$1.8m to
\$2.2m. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p54, "Risk"

statement:
A risk has been raised in your project around insufficient building
materials. One risk response assigned to the functional manager suggests
to use a brand new material, which has not been fully tested in a
building project. Half of your stakeholder group are happy with this
risk response, but the other half are not, and refuse to sign off on it.
What will you do next?
option1:
Suggest a quantitative risk approach to give a more accurate financial
impact.
option2:
Suggest they proceed with the proposed risk response, and ensure it is
followed through.
option3:
Suggest a different risk owner, as an executive may have more power to
respond to the risk.
option4:
Suggest a different risk response, as they should be timely, cost
effective, realistic, and agreed to by relevant stakeholders.
explanation:
This question refers to risk responses. Risk responses should be
appropriate and timely to the significance of the risk, cost effective,
realistic within the project context, agreed to by relevant
stakeholders, and owned by a responsible person. D is the best answer.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p54, "Risk"

statement:
Your project is starting to go off track due to a large amount of
uncertainty in the requirements and resources. At a significant cost,
you have managed to reign in these issues, however your project
management office would like you to brainstorm ways to improve with your
team so that they do not happen again. What will you do next?
option1:
Ask your team to increase the project Contingency Reserve, to allow for
these impacts.
option2: Ask your team to change the project methodology from Waterfall to Agile.
option3:
Ask your team to use short feedback loops, continuous improvement, and
transparent planning.
option4:
Ask your team to check their work twice before sending it to the next
part of the process.
explanation:
A project team needs to embrace adaptability and resiliency with methods
such as using short feedback loops, continuous learning and improvement,
regular inspection and adaptation of project work, diverse project
teams, transparent planning, using prototypes and more. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p56, "Embrace adaptability and resiliency."

statement:
As your project progresses, many of the business stakeholders are
starting to voice their concerns, to question some of the changes, and
even miss key meetings while trying to sabotage your efforts. What will
you do next?
option1:
Communicate the vision and benefits for the change clearly, along with
the impact to their work.
option2:
Create an impact over influence chart to determine who should be engaged
appropriately.
option3:
Escalate the matter with your project sponsor, and arrange for different
stakeholders.
option4:
Map the current state and the proposed future state of the change for
the stakeholders.
explanation:
This question is on change management, which is a structured approach to
transitioning groups to a future state. Too much change or lack of
understanding can lead to resistance and change fatigue. Methods include
communicating the vision, goals and benefits of the change early, along
with the impact to work processes. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition,
2021, p59, "Enable change to achieve the envisioned future state".

statement:
You are analysing your stakeholder through an impact over influence
chart and have found more than 100 different groups of people, and over
3000 impacted stakeholders. Other parts of your project are starting to
fall behind as you try to keep up with the large amount of people. What
will you do next?
option1:
Ask your project team to engage different groups of stakeholders so
everyone is covered.
option2:
Clearly prioritise the stakeholders then engage and communicate with the
highly impacted and influential groups early and often.
option3:
Monitor the stakeholders for signs of stress and respond to groups most
stressed about the project.
option4:
Create a SharePoint page for changes that any stakeholder can gather
information from anytime.
explanation:
This question is about engaging stakeholders. The process includes:
Identify, Understand and Analyse, Prioritise, Engage and Monitor your
stakeholders. Where you have too many to engage effectively,
prioritisation is the key. Impact and influence are two ways, as well as
Power, Beliefs, Expectations, proximity to the project, interest and
more. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p11, 12, "Stakeholder
Engagement: Prioritise and Engage"

statement:
You are managing a project which communicates to the customer using
mostly pull communication so far. When monitoring the engagement of your
stakeholders, you notice a trend that they are not clear on the impacts
or benefits of the project. You decide to add push communication to your
communication plan. What will you do next?
option1:
Create a webpage using SharePoint with all the project information, that
your stakeholders can see.
option2:
Push for more funding on your project, and communicate this to your
project sponsor.
option3: Push your stakeholders to engage in more two-way communication.
option4:
Send a weekly email to stakeholders with a project update, including the
impacts and benefits.
explanation:
Push communication is sent to stakeholders, using memos, emails, status
reports, voice mail and more. Pull communication is something a customer
can pull at any time -- from a bulletin, a web page, a white board, etc.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p13, "Types of Communication"

statement:
You have taken over on a project with a vendor where scope and
requirements have been missed. You notice that miscommunication and
mixed messages are common. You set a meeting with your team and the
vendor's team. The meeting is extremely important to get right. What
will you do next?
option1:
Speak slowly and clearly with the vendor's team, at a louder volume if
necessary.
option2:
Confirm they hear your message, determine if they agree, and identify
nuanced or unintended messages they may have received.
option3:
Only communicate with the vendor in writing, so you have a trail of
proof if anything goes wrong in the future.
option4:
Prepare a claim for the mishandled scope of your project through your
procurement team.
explanation:
This question is asking about communication. With all forms of
communication, quick feedback loops provide the information you need by
confirming they heard the message, determining if they agree, and
identifying any nuances or additions to the message they may have added
or heard. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p13, "Stakeholder
Engagement: Engage"

statement:
Your stakeholders seem to be engaged, however there are frequent changes
to scope and requirements, causing delays. Issues are often raised and
result in multiple rounds of feedback. You are not sure who or what
could be the cause. What will you do next?
option1:
Perform root cause analysis with your project team on the problem to
brainstorm ideas.
option2:
Raise a risk in the risk register for the project delay, and review
responses with your team.
option3:
Review the issue log, risk register and change log for the most frequent
requestors, then update your stakeholder engagement approach for those
people.
option4:
Ensure your project budget has enough contingency reserves to meet the
multiple reviews and delays.
explanation:
A significant number of changes or modifications to the project
requirements or scope may indicate stakeholders are not engaged or
aligned with the project objectives. A review of the project issue
register or risk register can identify challenges associated with
individual stakeholders. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p15,
"Checking outcomes -- Stakeholder Performance"

statement:
You have been working on a project where the manager leads in a
dictatorship style. Over time, the team have stopped making suggestions,
sick days and mental health days have become more frequent, and the pace
of work has slowed. The manager comes to you for help. You suggest a
servant leadership style instead. What will they do next?
option1: Serve the team as much as they can, ensuring their every need is met.
option2: Focus on obstacle removal, encouragement and development opportunities.
option3:
Ensure their team are serving other team members and helping each other
succeed.
option4:
Serve the team new ways to do the work and engage them through
additional variety.
explanation:
Servant leaders allow project teams to self-organise when possible, and
increase levels of autonomy by passing appropriate decision making
opportunities to project team members. Servant leadership behaviours
also include obstacle removal, being a diversion shield, and development
opportunities. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p18, "Distributed
management and leadership."

statement:
You have been allocated to a new project team with members from
different parts of the functional business area. It soon becomes clear
that very few of them have worked on a project before. There is also a
lot of miscommunication and re-work occurring amongst the team, which is
starting to cause delays. What will you do next?
option1:
Set a daily stand-up with your team to ensure they update what they
completed.
option2:
Review the Resource Assignment Matrix with your team and ensure they are
doing their job.
option3:
Help the team set their vision and objectives, roles and
responsibilities, and team operations.
option4:
Send your team members on training in their roles to increase their
capability.
explanation:
When project teams form across different organisations, more work may be
required up front to establish a "one-team" mindset, ensuring everyone
understands how they contribute. Team development such as establishing a
vision, clear roles & responsibilities, and a way of working are key.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p18, "Common aspects of Team
Development\"

statement:
You have collected the requirements for your project, matched them to
project scope, and broken them down into smaller parts and the
activities required. In doing this, you notice that the manager of the
marketing department has added extra scope that is unrelated to your
current project. What will you do next?
option1:
Start an open dialogue with the functional manager, raise the incorrect
scope items and their impacts.
option2:
Override the marketing manager's decision on scope as you are the senior
project manager.
option3:
Raise a change request for the additional scope and let the Change
Control Board decide.
option4:
Place the additional scope into the product backlog for the product
owner to prioritise it.
explanation:
Without an open dialogue, you may not know if the scope was a mistake,
previously approved, or any other reason for it being there. Positive
discourse and courage are key aspects of a winning project team culture.
Others include support, respect, celebrating success, and transparency.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p21, "Project Team Culture"

statement:
Your project team are protected by a union agreement. You notice that
they don't know how to use the product you are improving, are slow to
respond and regularly complain about the work. Your project sponsor has
had enough and threatens to shut the project down if improvements can't
be made. What will you do next?
option1:
Set up a new project with different team members and transfer the
project work to that team.
option2:
Set a clear project purpose and vision and pair your team with the
customer to show them how they are making a positive difference.
option3:
Tell your project team that they will lose their jobs in they don't
improve the speed of their work.
option4:
Create a project Gantt Chart schedule and burndown chart so the project
work is transparent to all.
explanation:
This question calls for leadership skills, where setting a vision and
purpose and showing your team how they are making a difference can
increase their intrinsic motivation. Transferring to another team in the
same company may risk the same result, and threatening your team may
embed their behaviour further. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p24,25, "Leadership Skills: Establishing a Vision; Motivation"

statement:
During a key scope meeting, an argument arises. The business manager
accuses the technology manager of not delivering to their needs on past
projects, and the technology manager accuses the business manager of
changing requirements that impact the delivery schedule. The meeting is
at risk, with the stakeholders being closed off to any further
discussion. What will you do next?
option1:
Take the side of the technology manager, as scope that impacts delivery
should be controlled.
option2:
Take the side of the business manager, as we should always deliver to
the customer's specification.
option3:
Facilitate to keep communication respectful, focus on the issue in the
present and search for alternatives together.
option4:
Ensure they both agree to the change control process to avoid
disagreements in the future.
explanation:
Not all conflict is negative. How conflict is handled can either lead to
more conflict or to better decision making and stronger solutions. The
PMBOK Guide notes open and respectful communication, focusing on the
issue not the person, the present not the past, and searching for
alternatives together. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p29,
"Conflict Management"

statement:
You are working on a project where the project team is completely
distributed around the country in multiple sites, including working from
home. Some of the project team members have not been able to contribute
to the project, and there are concerns from the program that the project
will fall behind. What will you do next?
option1:
Raise a request to the program to co-locate all your project team
members as soon as possible.
option2:
Proceed with daily stand-ups and working group meetings with any team
members in your area.
option3:
Set team leaders for each area around the country, to report back to you
as the centralised project leader.
option4:
Use technology to maintain ongoing contact with audio and video for
meetings, with a team web site to keep all project information
available.
explanation:
This question is on distributed project teams. To improve you can use
audio and video capabilities for meetings, use technology (including
messaging) for ongoing contact, build in time to get to know remote team
members, and have an open project site for information. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p30, "Distributed project teams".

statement:
You are initiating a project to a sales team where the process will
change significantly, even though the outcome of a completed sale will
remain the same. The functional manager is concerned about the change
and any disruption it might bring. What will you do next?
option1:
Kick off the project with multiple deliveries to deliver small parts and
reduce the change impact.
option2:
Kick off the project work with a single delivery at the end of the
project when the process change is complete.
option3:
Kick off the project with continuous delivery in mind, to ensure fast
value to the business.
option4:
Kick off the project using periodic deliveries, delivering value as it
is ready.
explanation:
This question covers delivery cadence and their various benefits. A
process re-engineering project may not have any deliveries until near
the end of the project when the new process is rolled out and ready.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p34, "Delivery Cadence"

statement:
You are working on a project where the requirements are clear but the
solution ideas are uncertain. The product owner and the technical
experts can't be sure which ideas will work in practice, and which ones
won't. The organisation cannot afford to get this project wrong, as they
have previously made mistakes and customers are starting to leave. What
will you do next?
option1: Use an Incremental development approach for the project.
option2: Use an Iterative development approach for the project.
option3: Use a Predictive development approach for the project.
option4: Use a hybrid development approach for the project.
explanation:
For development approaches, an iterative approach is used when you need
to capture ideas that might change, while still delivered in one
release. Incremental progressively delivers features (or increments),
Predictive plans everything up-front, and hybrid is a combination of
predictive and adaptive. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p37,
"Development approaches." Agile Practice Guide, 2017, p18,
"Characteristics of project lifecycles."

statement:
You are working on a new product for your organisation where the
requirements are complex and are likely to change a few times as the
project progresses. The organisation also needs usable parts of the
product to be released as soon as possible to try and capture some of
the market they are aiming for, away from their competitors. What will
you do next?
option1: Recommend an Iterative development approach.
option2: Recommend a Predictive development approach.
option3: Recommend an Adaptive development approach.
option4: Recommend a Hybrid development approach.
explanation:
Adaptive approaches are useful when requirements are subject to
uncertainty and change. A clear vision is established at the start of
the project, and initial known requirements are refined or replaced in
accordance with user feedback or unexpected events. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p38, "Adaptive approach"

statement:
A new project has been kicked off in your organisation due to a recent
government inquiry into your industry, which found multiple processes
with significant regulatory oversight that need to change. The inquiry
ordering these changes has given you a due date of 10 months' time
before you start incurring penalties. What will you do next?
option1:
Proceed, with an Agile development approach to deliver change as quickly
as possible.
option2:
Proceed, with a Hybrid development approach to respond to changes while
planning up front.
option3: Proceed, with a Predictive methodology to ensure quality control.
option4: Proceed, with an Iterative methodology to improve using feedback.
explanation:
In considerations for selecting a development approach, environments
that have significant regulatory oversight may need to use a predictive
approach due to the required process, documentation and demonstration
needs. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p40, "Considerations for
selecting a development approach."

statement:
Your organisation is moving to an Agile way of work. Your project team
are strongly opposed to using Agile as they have been burned using
iterations, when executives used the team velocity to try and force the
team to work faster instead of keeping a sustainable pace. What will you
do next?
option1:
Add extra resources to your team to deliver faster and avoid executives
getting involved.
option2:
Proceed with a predictive development approach, and deal with the
consequences later.
option3:
Ask your team to work with Agile, velocity and iterations despite their
hesitations.
option4:
Suggest the team uses flow-based scheduling, with a Kanban board for
visual work.
explanation:
Kanban is one of the largest parts of Agile approaches, aside from other
major parts such as Lean, XP and Scrum. A flow-based scheduling approach
does not use sprints, lifecycles or velocity but optimises deliveries
based on resource capacity, and minimises waste such as
context-switching and hand-offs. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p45, "Flow-based scheduling."

statement:
Your team have not worked on projects before, and don't know about
waterfall, agile, or any of the different models. Your project may also
have requirements that are certain or uncertain, with varying levels of
risk, so are not sure which approach to use as you begin this new
project. What will you do next?
option1:
Use Waterfall and plan everything up front, so the team have more
stability.
option2:
Use general phase definitions such as Feasibility, Design, Build, Test,
Deploy and Close.
option3:
Use Agile to ensure you can respond to change while your team's
capability increases.
option4:
Don't use any project approach -- it doesn't matter as long as the work
is getting done.
explanation:
All projects will go through the general phases of Feasibility, Design,
Build, Test, Deploy and Close. Waterfall projects will do these in
sequence once, while Agile projects will do these multiple times, for
each feature or increment. The underlying principle remains the same, so
it is a good place to start. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p42, "Lifecycle and phase definitions\"

statement:
You a project manager in an organisation with a directive Project
Management Office, and are currently in between projects. The PMO asks
you to prepare for an upcoming project in the accounting area by meeting
with the functional manager there. You have no team, no scope or
requirements and no timeline as yet. What will you do next?
option1:
Ask the functional manager for their wish-list of improvements, so you
can take these to your PMO.
option2:
Create a project management plan so the project, scope, budget and
schedule are more clear.
option3:
Create a small backlog of work and start your first sprint, then
progressively update the backlog as the project progresses.
option4:
Progressively elaborate the vision statement and project charter to
define a coordinated approach.
explanation:
Before initiating a project of any sort, we start with a vision
statement, project charter or business case in order to identify a
co-ordinated path to achieve the desired outcomes. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p52, "Planning Overview"

statement:
You are working on a project where part of the project delivers a high
value, high risk component. Another part of the project delivers a
routine change to the system. Your project sponsor wants to gain the
business value as quickly as possible, but your project team want to
start with the work they are familiar with first. What will you do next?
option1: Prioritise the high risk items at the end of the project.
option2: Prioritise the high risk items at the start of the project.
option3:
Use an incremental approach and deliver part of the routine and high
risk work in each sprint.
option4:
Plan out the work in detail and secure a scope baseline, with formal
change controls to reduce uncertainty.
explanation:
This question refers to the concept of the last responsible moment. Work
that is novel or risky can be prioritised at the start of the project to
reduce uncertainty with the project scope before a large investment has
been made. Routine work can be delayed until the cost of further delay
would exceed the benefit. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p54,
"Delivery", paragraph 3

statement:
You are working on an agile project where the functional area you are
delivering to only has a high level idea of the system requirements they
will need. The functional manager suggests a Work Breakdown Structure,
however your team feel that is more for a predictive (waterfall)
delivery approach. What will you do next?
option1: Use and create a WBS anyway, as it is just a tool.
option2:
Use rolling wave planning, and keep the work at a high level until you
are ready to work on it, when you can plan in detail.
option3:
Note the high level themes or epics, decompose them into features, and
again into user stories.
option4:
Ask the functional area for more detailed requirements before the
project begins.
explanation:
This question refers to "decomposition" or breaking down the work. We
could use a WBS, or rolling wave planning, but for an incremental
approach breaking down the work from epics to features to user stories
is a form of decomposition that fits the delivery approach. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p54, "Delivery", paragraph 3

statement:
You are at the beginning of planning a new project, and have researched
and gathered the requirements from the area, broken them down into
deliverables and activities, which you believe could take 26 weeks to
deliver. The project sponsor is asking you for an estimate on how long
the project will take. What will you do next?
option1: Tell the project sponsor 26 weeks.
option2:
Tell the project sponsor that you will use an iterative approach, where
value is delivered until the project stops.
option3: Tell the project sponsor approximately 24 to 28 weeks.
option4: Tell the project sponsor approximately 20 to 45 weeks.
explanation:
This question is related to estimation and range. Estimates should have
a broad range at the start of the project, starting at -25% to +75% when
there is not much information. Once the project team has a smooth
delivery cadence and experience in the product, a smaller range such as
-5% to +10% can be used. A 0% range is when everything is known -- and
the product has been delivered. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p55, "Estimating, Range".

statement:
After gathering your project requirements, project scope, and breaking
it down into activities, you need to estimate the project schedule with
your team. After some analysis and discussion, your team come up with a
range of 53 to 75 days assigned across all tasks. What will you do next?
option1:
The estimate is high accuracy but low precision -- ask the team to come
back with a more precise estimate.
option2:
The estimate is low accuracy but high precision -- ensure the estimates
become more accurate as the project unfolds.
option3:
The estimate is in high confidence but low precision -- proceed as is
and adjust using an incremental approach.
option4:
The estimate is in low confidence but high accuracy -- allow the team to
become more confident as the project unfolds.
explanation:
This question is on estimates and their accuracy. The lower the
accuracy, the larger the potential range in values. Precision is
different from accuracy, as it refers to the exactness of the estimate,
for example "2 days" is more precise than "sometime this week". (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p54, "Estimating: accuracy, precision,
confidence"

statement:
You are working on a business case where you know that the industry is
undergoing a significant change at the moment, and there could be many
different impacts to your project benefits before it is delivered
completely. The project sponsor asks you for the best estimate of
benefits you can give under the circumstances. What will you do next?
option1: Ask your team for a probabilistic estimate.
option2: Ask your team for a deterministic estimate.
option3: Ask your team for an absolute estimate.
option4: Ask your team for a relative estimate.
explanation:
Probabilistic estimates include a range of estimates, along with the
associated probabilities within the range such as a confidence level or
a probability distribution. If the estimate is uncertain, this is the
best approach. Deterministic is a number, absolute is specific, actual
numbers and relative estimates are shown in comparison to other
estimates. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p57, "Presenting
estimates."

statement:
You have noted nine epics for delivery as part of your most recent
project. Your team have broken down these epics into user stories, and
would like to see the effort involved for these deliverables. You ask
your team to find the smallest user story, size it with a "1", and then
determine the size of the other cards based on how they compare to the
original card. What approach are you taking?
option1: Relative estimating with your team.
option2: Deterministic estimating to determine the effort.
option3: Absolute estimating to get an estimate with absolute certainty.
option4: Probabilistic estimating with the highest probability items.
explanation:
This question is about estimating, and making an estimate in comparison
to other estimates is relative estimating. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p57, "Relative Estimating"

statement:
You are working on a project where a competitor is also working on a
similar feature, and being first to the market will have a significant
impact on this year's profit. Your product owner would like to measure
how quickly story cards are being completed, once they start being
developed. What will you do next?
option1: Check the Lead time of the story cards.
option2: Check the Gantt chart with assigned schedule activities.
option3: Check the Cycle time of the story cards.
option4: Check the schedule network and precedence diagram.
explanation:
Cycle time is the total elapsed time it takes one unit to get through a
process. Lead time is the time from when the customer makes the order
(or the requirement is noted) to the time the item is delivered or
released. Schedule network diagram is useful for finding the Critical
Path, and precedence diagramming is used to find what deliverable or
activity relies on others. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p58,
"Flow-based estimating"

statement:
You are working on a project that has recently moved to a more agile way
of work. The team have not completely taken on the Scrum approach
however, and are comfortable working with a more flow-based approach
moving user stories across a Kanban board. You are wanting to get an
estimate on how much work can be completed in the next three months,
based on the team's existing progress. What will you do next?
option1: Multiply the velocity by the number of remaining user stories.
option2: Multiply the throughput of the team for the last month, by three.
option3: Multiply the lead time by the schedule performance index.
option4: Multiply the planned value by the actual value.
explanation:
Scrum uses velocity -- Kanban or Flow-based estimates are developed
using cycle time and / or throughput. Cycle time is the total elapsed
time it takes one unit to get through a process. Throughput is the
number of items that can complete a process in a given amount of time.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p58, "Flow-based estimating."

statement:
Your product owner wants to bring the next feature forward so that it is
done in parallel to the current feature, but is still released in the
existing feature order. You make the necessary adjustments to the
product backlog, and decide to adjust the product roadmap, which you
have visualised as a Gantt chart. What will you do next?
option1:
Use a schedule lead on the feature, and change the feature to
finish-to-start
option2:
Use a schedule lag on the feature, and change the feature to
start-to-start
option3:
Use a schedule lead on the feature, and change the feature to
finish-to-finish
option4:
Use a schedule lag on the feature, and change the feature to
start-to-finish
explanation:
A schedule lag is moving an item back, a schedule lead is bringing an
item forward. Precedence diagramming method states for Finish-to-Finish
items, the next item cannot finish until the previous item has finished.
This allows the items to be worked on in parallel, while still keeping
the same delivery order. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p59,
"Schedules; Lead, Lag." (PMBOK Guide) -- Sixth Edition, 2017, p180,
"Precedence diagramming method"

statement:
You are working on a project where you have been completing the
deliverables in sequence, and the project work is starting to fall
behind. The project sponsor asks you to speed up the work and reduce the
time for the project to be delivered, but also makes it very clear that
there is no more money to use when speeding up the work. What will you
do next?
option1: Apply leads and lags to your project schedule.
option2: Apply fast tracking to your project schedule.
option3: Apply schedule crashing to your project.
option4: Apply resource levelling to your project.
explanation:
Project fast tracking is a schedule compression method in which
activities or tasks that are normally done in sequence are performed in
parallel. Schedule crashing includes adding people to activities,
working overtime, or paying to expedite deliveries. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p59, "Schedules; Fast tracking, crashing."

statement:
After reviewing your project schedule you notice you are behind by three
weeks and the schedule will need to be compressed. Your project does not
have enough budget to crash the schedule. You check with the lead for
each area and review the schedule as to whether they can fast track
their deliverables. What will you do next?
option1: Fast track team items with discretionary dependencies.
option2: Fast track team items with mandatory dependencies.
option3: Fast track team items with external dependencies.
option4: Fast track team items based on industry regulation.
explanation:
When compressing the schedule, some activities cannot be fast-tracked
because of the nature of the work, others can. A discretionary
dependency is based on project preferences, and may be modifiable.
External and Mandatory dependencies usually cannot be modified. Internal
dependencies may be modifiable, except when based on specific regulatory
needs. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p60, "Four types of
dependencies."

statement:
Your team are new to agile, and had previously been working with
Waterfall. Your key customer asks you for a project schedule. The
project team believes using a project schedule is from the old waterfall
way of working and they shouldn\'t have to create one. What will you do
next?
option1:
Create a project Gantt chart to visualise the activities within the
project.
option2:
Determine the project's planned versus actual value, instead of working
with a schedule directly.
option3:
Advise the customer that we don't need to plan ahead in an adaptive way
of work.
option4:
Ask your team to develop a high level release plan like a product
roadmap, showing the features to be included in each release.
explanation:
Adaptive schedule planning uses incremental planning. A high level
release plan is developed that indicates basic features and
functionality to be included in each release. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p61, "Adaptive schedule planning."

statement:
You are working in a project team that has recently moved to an Agile
way of work. Your business analysts want to gather the requirements,
solutions, match them to the scope and do a WBS for the whole
deliverable. Your product owner says this will take too long, and is not
agile enough for their needs. What will you do next?
option1:
Ask your team to review the scope management plan for the proper
process.
option2:
Break the deliverable down into smaller pieces so you can deliver those
faster.
option3:
Ask your team to timebox work on the highest priority items in the
backlog, update the estimates then reprioritise as necessary.
option4:
Review the project charter for the high level scope, and use this to
begin delivery quickly.
explanation:
Adaptive approaches often use timeboxes. The work is based on a
prioritised backlog. The project team determines the amount of work they
can do in each timebox, estimates the work and self-manages to
accomplish the work. At this point, the backlog may be reprioritised for
the next timebox. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p62,
"Timeboxes"

statement:
When reviewing the current progress with your project sponsor, they
notice that the system solution is missing a critical piece that will
impact the benefit of the project. The project sponsor approves this
scope. Your project has a Planned Value of \$520,000 and an Actual Cost
of \$535,000. What will you do next?
option1:
Work with the PMO to unlock the project management reserves for the
extra work.
option2:
Ask the Product Owner to reprioritise the backlog and see if the new
work will fit.
option3: Perform the work within your normal budget as your project is on track.
option4:
Raise a change request for the changes and gain approval from the Change
Control Board.
explanation:
Management reserves are set aside for unexpected activities related to
in-scope work. Depending on the organisation's policies, management
reserves may be managed by the PMO at the program or portfolio level.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p62, "Budget"

statement:
You are in the planning stages of an Agile project where the functional
manager and senior users have provided the high level requirements and
scope. You have been asked to put together a project team that will be
able to deliver quickly. What will you do next?
option1:
Select project members from each city to ensure diversity of knowledge
within the team.
option2:
Put together a resource management plan outlining the resources
required.
option3:
Ask your PMO for current available resources and assign them to the
work.
option4:
Select a small team that can work in the same area so they can solve
problems as they arise.
explanation:
When planning for the team, the project manager considers the ability
and necessity for them to work in the same location. Small project teams
that can work in the same room are able to take advantage of osmotic
communication and can solve problems as they arise. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p64, "Project Team Composition and Structure"

statement:
You are putting together a project team to deliver an accounting system
to offices around the country. The accounting system you are moving to
has not been used by anyone in your organisation before. The project is
quite high risk, and the delivery needs to be right the first time. What
will you do next?
option1:
Select a small team that can work in the same area so they can solve
problems as they arise.
option2:
Use a predictive project approach and source part of your team
externally if they have skillsets in the new system.
option3:
Train existing internal staff in the new system then have them work on
the project.
option4: Perform a make or buy analysis on the different software options.
explanation:
The benefit that outside skills bring to the project are weighed against
the costs that will be incurred. With higher risk and a single delivery,
a waterfall or predictive project delivery approach is best. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p63, "Project Team Composition and
Structure"

statement:
You are putting together a project plan for a change that affects nearly
ten thousand stakeholders in your organisation. You have identified the
affected stakeholders, analysed and prioritised them, and are putting
together a communication plan to ensure the right engagement is made.
What will you NOT include in your plan?
option1: Why should information be shared with stakeholders?
option2: What is the best way to provide information?
option3: How can they make changes to the communication plan?
option4: When and how often is information needed?
explanation:
Planning communication for the project entails considering the
following: Who needs information? Who has the information needed? Why
should information be shared with stakeholders? When and how often is
information needed? What is the best way to provide information? What
information does each stakeholder need? (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p64, "Communication"

statement:
You put together a resource plan for a large amount of physical
inventory to be purchased from overseas. You ordered the inventory
during project execution, however global supply chains were impacted and
the shipment was delayed by three months, significantly impacting your
project. What should you have done differently?
option1:
Hired a resource manager to take care of the resource tasks on your
project.
option2:
Planned strategically about the timing from order to delivery to usage,
managing resource risks and their responses.
option3:
Ensured a means to track the inventory from arrival on site to the
delivery of an integrated product.
option4:
Sourced the inventory locally to reduce the impact of global supply
chains.
explanation:
Project teams whose projects require significant physical materials
think and plan strategically about the timing from order, to delivery,
to usage. This can include evaluation of bulk ordering versus the cost
of storage, global logistics and more. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition,
2021, p65, "Physical resources."

statement:
You are half way through your agile project as a Product Owner, when
there is a significant change to the project scope after it was found
that the project would not be able to deliver the benefits it had
promised. You check your project budget and there are contingency and
management reserves available. What will you do next?
option1: Take the project scope change to the change control board for approval.
option2:
Use the available contingency reserves to make the appropriate change to
project scope.
option3: Review the change management plan for the change control process.
option4:
Reprioritise the product backlog, including the new scope and begin work
on the highest priority straight away.
explanation:
Changes may occur as a result of a risk event, environment change, a
deeper understanding of requirements, customer requests and more.
Project teams should prepare a process for adapting plans throughout the
project. In an Agile team, this may take the form of reprioritising the
backlog instead of a change control process with project baselines.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p66, "Changes\"

statement:
You have recently taken over on a project that is behind schedule, and
slightly over budget. You raise an issue at the steering committee
meeting, where they advise that there is no additional funding available
and the project must finish on time. You believe you can optimise the
project process to try and bring the project back on track. What will
you do next?
option1: Add additional people and resources to the project to speed it up.
option2:
Utilise the project's contingency reserve to source the resources you
need.
option3:
Perform project activities in parallel, completing multiple items at
once instead of one at a time.
option4:
Use Lean production methods to reduce non-value add activities, and
retrospectives for the team to suggest improvements.
explanation:
Ways of optimising the project processes for the environment include
Lean production methods, retrospectives or lessons learned, and finding
where the next best funding is spent. Adding people and resources may
not always improve things -- it can also increase the complexity and
communication channels. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p71,
"Project Processes"

statement:
Your Project Management Office (PMO) is optimising all the projects
within their portfolio. They ask for your advice in tracking the type of
work each team member is doing and how long it takes, suggesting that
they categorise and record their work in addition to the work itself.
What will you do next?
option1:
Ask your team to record and categorise their work, as gathering project
data is important.
option2:
Recommend an automated reporting tool where possible, as categorising
their work is NVA.
option3:
Recommend an automated reporting tool where possible, as recording their
work is VA.
option4:
Ask your team to ignore the request and focus on delivering customer
value only.
explanation:
