The time taken to track or record time can be viewed as "non-value
added" work, that does not directly benefit the output or the receiving
customer. We should respect the PMO request, but in addition to being
efficient, processes should be effective. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p72, "Project Processes"

statement:
You are working on an agile project as a scrum master, where the
functional manager has worked with some people in your development team
before. She approaches them directly to see if they can add a feature to
the product in the current sprint. What will you do next?
option1: Adjust the sprint backlog to allow for the additional feature.
option2: Adjust the product backlog to add the customer requirement.
option3:
Ask the manager to speak with the Product Owner for product priorities,
then protect the team from direct requests in the future.
option4:
Ask the manager to create a change request so it can go through the
proper process.
explanation:
Project Managers have a responsibility for assessing and balancing
project team focus and attention. This may involve protecting the team's
production capability -- their team health and satisfaction. In Agile,
this also means being a diversion shield for the team and removing
blockers that get in the way of them making progress in their work.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p73, "Maintaining Project Team
Focus"

statement:
Part of your project management plan is a detailed communications plan,
which has the stakeholders on the project, their communication needs,
and how information will be communicated. While this is being signed off
by the project sponsor, you receive many ad-hoc requests for information
from new stakeholders. What will you do next?
option1:
Review the communications management plan to include the stakeholders,
the information they need and their communication preferences.
option2:
Create an information radiator and ask the stakeholders to pull the
information they need.
option3:
Ignore the new requests, as you have captured all the stakeholders you
need to in your plan.
option4:
Ask the new stakeholders to review your communications management plan
to see how you are distributing information.
explanation:
Once collected, information is distributed as indicated in the project
management communications plan. An abundance of ad-hoc communication
requests may indicate that the communication planning was not sufficient
to meet stakeholder needs -- it is a good idea to review it. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p72, "Project Communications and
Engagement"

statement:
You are working on a high value construction project to include
smart-home components within multi-million dollar buildings. After
completing a make-or-buy analysis, you find that it will cost \$700,000
to make the new system, or \$500,000 to buy it from a vendor. You
prepare a procurement management plan to purchase it. What will you do
before conducting a procurement?
option1:
Work with your project team to complete as much of the system in-house
as possible.
option2:
Work with contracting professionals to develop RFP, SOW, and terms and
conditions.
option3:
Adjust your stakeholder register to include the new vendor, and add the
scope to your work breakdown dictionary.
option4:
Work with your project team to develop an RMP, SMP, and list of
resources.
explanation:
Prior to conducting a procurement, the project manager and technically
qualified project team members work with contracting professionals to
develop the Request for proposals (RFP), Statement of Work (SOW), terms
and conditions, and other necessary documents to go out to bid. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p74, "Working with Procurements"

statement:
A missing item has been uncovered in the project scope that your project
team cannot deliver. The item is not complex but you are unsure of which
vendors might be able to provide it. You have already reviewed your
Procurement Management Plan and are ready to move to source selection
and choose a vendor that will meet your needs. What will you do next?
option1:
Prepare a Request for Proposal for vendors to provide a solution to your
needs.
option2:
Prepare a Request for Quote to determine the best price from the
vendors.
option3:
Prepare a Request for Information to gather more information and viable
sources from the market.
option4:
Review the procurement management plan for your project's procurement
process.
explanation:
A Request for Information is used to gather more information from the
market prior to sending out other bid documents to a set of selected
vendors (like a Request for Quote). A Request for Proposal is used where
scope is complex or the buyer is looking for the vendor to provide a
solution. Request for Quote is when price is the deciding factor. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p75, "The Bid Process"

statement:
You are working on a project where part of the work is planned carefully
upfront, and another part is subject to change. You negotiate terms and
conditions, including cost, delivery and payment dates. The vendor is
concerned about confusion that might occur between the pre-planned and
changeable work. What will you do next?
option1:
Use a Firm Fixed Price contract, to ensure cost is fixed but scope can
remain flexible.
option2:
Use a time and materials contract, to ensure only the time and materials
used are paid for.
option3:
Adjust your project plan to suit either a predictive or adaptive
approach to reduce the confusion.
option4:
Use a master agreement for the overall contract, with adaptive work
placed in an appendix.
explanation:
For projects that use an adaptive approach for some deliverables and a
predictive approach for others, a master agreement is used and the
adaptive work may be placed in an appendix or supplement. This allows
the changes to occur on the adaptive scope without impacting the overall
contract. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p76, "The Bid Process:
Contracting"

statement:
You are working on an agile project where there are vastly differing
levels of product knowledge within your team. The scrum master suggests
that you try to gain knowledge by osmosis, because the project
information may be lost when only communicating by email. What will you
do next?
option1:
Co-locate the team to capture the benefit of each person's tacit
knowledge.
option2:
Refer to the organisational process assets to capture the team's
explicit knowledge.
option3:
Refer to the project's knowledge management system to capture the
project's explicit knowledge.
option4: Create a process to capture the team's tacit knowledge.
explanation:
Learning by Osmosis is capturing information from the immediate
surroundings, such as co-locating the team. Tacit knowledge is
information that is not straight forward, such as experience, insights,
and practical knowledge or skill. Explicit knowledge can be readily
codified using words, a process, pictures. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p77, "Explicit and tacit knowledge"

statement:
You are working as a Product Owner, and have placed high level
information about the product in the product backlog, and the highest
priority feature at the top. Senior users informing the project are
unable to clearly articulate their requirements, and are concerned that
the product will not truly meet their needs. What will you do next?
option1: Proceed with the highest priority item during the first sprint.
option2:
Work with the customer to create mock-ups and prototypes to discover
what works for them.
option3:
Ask your stakeholders to submit a change request and update the project
scope accordingly.
option4: Begin breaking the work down into user stories to place in the sprint.
explanation:
On projects that do not have clearly defined requirements prototypes,
demonstrations, storyboards and mock-ups can be used to evolve the
requirements. In these situations, Stakeholders are more likely to take
an "I'll know it when I see it" approach. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p83, "Evolving and discovering requirements."

statement:
You are gathering requirements for a new feature in a customer
management system. The product owner has given a user interface feature
as the next highest priority, and you are co-located with the team
receiving the benefits. What will you do next?
option1:
Show the customer team how the new user interface will look, as they
will be using it.
option2:
Communicate the requirements to developers with a coherent, logical flow
of ideas.
option3:
Elicit requirements into user stories using acceptance criteria that is
clear, verifiable and traceable.
option4:
Show the mock up or prototype to the developers and ask them to develop
based on that.
explanation:
Elicitation means to draw out. Well-documented requirements are Clear,
Concise, Verifiable, Consistent, Complete and Traceable. A prototype
useful but may need more information for developers to turn into a
product. Clear purpose, coherent ideas, controlling flow, concise
expression and correct grammar are part of the Five C's of
communication. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p83,
"Requirements Elicitation\"

statement:
You are working in an agile team who are trying to break down the
project requirements and scope. They have created a scope statement and
work breakdown structure, but are not sure how it will work now that
they are using sprints and user stories. What will you do next?
option1:
Assign activities to each of the scope items in the WBS, and place them
on a Gantt Chart.
option2:
Create a product roadmap with the team, using the lowest, most detailed
part of the WBS.
option3:
Group the work by large themes of customer value, shown as Epics, then
break them down into smaller user stories to complete in each sprint.
option4:
Ask the team to trace the requirements properly using a Requirements
traceability Matrix.
explanation:
One way to identify scope is identifying the themes of customer value,
associated by a common factor such as functionality, data source or
security level. These are shown as Epics, which are too big to complete
in a sprint, and have smaller user stories within them. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p84, "Scope decomposition."

statement:
Your project team are trialling an agile way of work. The Product Owner
has a clear vision for the product. There is confusion in the team as to
how much work should be done on a user story before it is taken to the
sprint review, and how they will know a story is complete. What will you
do next?
option1:
Refer to the user story acceptance criteria and your team's Definition
of Done.
option2:
Review the user story with multiple customers and senior users to ensure
it is correct.
option3: Ask the product owner to sign off on the completed user story.
option4: Ensure quality testing on the user story is completed and passed.
explanation:
Different ways to describe component completion include acceptance or
completion criteria, technical performance measures, and Definition of
Done. Quality testing will be done to the acceptance criteria, as will
Product Owner sign off. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p85,
"Completion of deliverables"

statement:
You have been working closely with the quality manager of your project,
planning the budget for the project phase that is due to begin in the
next two months. They mention the need to budget for appraisal costs, to
avoid quality issues in the future. What will you do next?
option1:
Ensure there is enough for training the staff to reduce the number of
errors.
option2:
Ensure there is enough for a quality tester to verify the deliverables
against agreed specifications.
option3: Ensure there is enough to create a quality assurance plan.
option4:
Ensure there is enough to allow for rework or rectification when defects
are found.
explanation:
This question is on the Cost of Quality. Prevention costs are training
or quality planning. Appraisal costs are verification or audits.
Internal failure is waste, defects, rework. External failure is customer
complaints, repairs and servicing, warranty claims. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p89, "Cost of Quality"

statement:
Your agile project has recently had multiple quality issues. Project
features have been prioritised by the Product Owner, and broken down
into user stories. What will you do to help reduce the cost of quality
issues the most?
option1:
Quality test each user story according to its acceptance criteria before
release.
option2: Advise the team that any quality defects will come out of their bonus.
option3:
Perform testing all in one go at the end, to reduce the cost of testing
resources.
option4:
Ensure a "triad" of developer, tester and customer is involved during
requirements and design.
explanation:
This question is about the Cost of Change. The cost is least during
requirements and design, is 20x during build, 50x during test, and 150x
if a defect has to be rectified in production. If the requirements,
design, and acceptance criteria is incorrect, extra quality testing will
not help. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p90, "Cost of Change"

statement:
You are working in an agile team where executives in the team you are
delivering to are asking for a large display of reports to be created
every two weeks, showing how the project is tracking and updating them
on the cost and benefits. What will you do next?
option1:
Collect the planned value versus earned value to show the executives any
variance in the plan.
option2:
Co-locate the team with the executives so they can answer any questions
as they arise.
option3:
Suggest a demonstration of the feature developed during each sprint
instead, and a burndown chart of future product features.
option4: Create the desired reports for the executives to keep them happy.
explanation:
The value of measurements is not in the collection and dissemination of
the data, but is in how to use the data to take appropriate action.
Agile projects demonstrate usable value and features delivered and use
live information radiators instead of large reports to stakeholders.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p94, "Measurement Performance
Domain"

statement:
Multiple features have been released, but they are not having the effect
the product owner believes they should. Your team are using some Key
Performance Indicators to track performance, and you agree to move to
more Leading indicators. What could you use as examples of Leading
indicators for your project?
option1:
An increase in customer usage once the feature is released, such as
sales and repeat purchasing.
option2: Stakeholders who are not engaged and lack of a risk management process.
option3:
Velocity of the work completed each sprint and cycle time of user
stories.
option4: Variance in the schedule between planned and actual time taken.
explanation:
There are two types of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs): Leading and
Lagging. Leading predict changes or trends in the project, and include
Lack of a risk management process, stakeholders who are not engaged,
poorly defined project success criteria. Lagging indicators include work
completed or schedule variance. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p96, "Leading Indicators"

statement:
You are working in an agile project team that has increased in size and
complexity since the project began. In reviewing the throughput of work
with your team, you notice fewer and fewer user stories being completed,
and the roadmap of features scheduled to go live is being pushed out.
What will you do next?
option1:
Place a limit on the work in progress and work with the team to reduce
the cycle time.
option2: Increase meetings with the team so everyone can collaborate together.
option3: Ask the team to work overtime until the number of features reduces.
option4:
Put a Pull system in place, where the team can pull work only when they
are ready.
explanation:
With more work, different tasks and increased complexity, the team are
likely context switching between too many different things. Kanban aims
to limit the work in progress to avoid multi-tasking. Other things we
can use are reducing queue size and batch sizes, and reviewing process
efficiency. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p99, "What to
measure: Delivery"

statement:
You are working on an agile project where the project customer wants a
reliable way to measure the project performance, and you know you first
need a baseline. What measures will you show to your customer as part of
your agile project?
option1: Start and finish dates.
option2: Project team velocity.
option3: Rate of Features completed.
option4: Schedule and Cost variance.
explanation:
An agile project tracks and demonstrates actual features delivered. With
a stable team, cost is most often fixed. The schedule is also fixed, and
the scope is variable depending on what the customer wants to prioritise
in the given time. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p100,
"Baseline performance"

statement:
The product owner has worked with a team of senior users to gather
feature ideas. They now have a list of more than fifty ideas for your
team to develop, which is far too many to complete in the timeframe you
have. What will you do next?
option1:
Ask the new product owner to return to the senior users and ask for a
shortened list.
option2:
Start work on the smallest features first, to ensure you are delivering
value quickly.
option3:
Workshop the features with a wider audience to get a completely diverse
view on their priority.
option4:
Ask the new product owner to prioritize the features using Benefit-Cost,
then deliver in priority order until time or money runs out.
explanation:
Product Owners prioritise features based on business value. Metrics that
measure business value include Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR), planned to
actual benefits delivery, return on investment, and Net Present Value.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p102, "Business value."

statement:
Your agile project team has only recently formed. The product is still
unclear, and there have been disagreements and minor conflicts over the
past two weeks as the team find their rhythm. You would like things to
improve, but can't quite pinpoint or address the problem. What will you
do next?
option1: Hold a workshop with your team to ask them why they are unhappy.
option2:
Have the team complete a Mood Chart with comments, and ask for
improvement ideas at the next retrospective.
option3:
Raise the issue with their line managers, to ensure all levels are aware
of and working on the issue.
option4:
Have the team pair up together for their tasks, so they can learn from
each other.
explanation:
Measuring stakeholders can be done with a Net Promoter Score or
satisfaction score, especially with a comment, or a mood chart.
Improvement ideas are captured during retrospectives for most agile
teams. Pairing is another agile practice, but is not as relevant here.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p103, "Measuring: Stakeholders\"

statement:
The project has faced serious impacts in getting materials, with supply
chains being disrupted and a global pandemic, and is currently three
weeks behind schedule. You take this information to the next project
steering committee meeting, where they advise they need more information
on how much the project will end up costing. What will you do next?
option1: Use the current project budget with no deviations until it runs out.
option2: Use the project burndown chart to see the burn-rate of accounting funds.
option3:
Use a regression analysis on the current rate of spending to see where
the final cost will end up.
option4:
Use the project's estimate at completion to show the steering committee
the most likely final cost.
explanation:
This question is talking about budget forecasts. Regression analysis may
work but the best answer is the Estimate at Completion, which is the
(BAC / (EV / AC). (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p104,
"Forecasts"

statement:
You are working on an agile project where the project sponsor and
executive managers require lengthy reports about the project work each
week. This takes you and the project team a considerable amount of time
to do, and reduces time spent creating the actual product. What will you
do next?
option1:
Reuse most of the information each week so your time spent on them is
reduced.
option2:
Suggest the leaders come to the sprint review and gather what they need
from the information radiator.
option3:
Ask your project sponsor to wait until a steering committee meeting for
the information.
option4:
Share your working project schedule and budget with the executives
instead of creating the reports.
explanation:
Agile teams showcase actual work completed at the sprint review.
Information radiators, also known as big visible charts, are visible
displays that allow anyone to "pull" the information from. They are
often "low tech and high touch" and contain information on work
completed or remaining, risks, and things like a Kanban board and
burndown chart. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p108,
"Information Radiators"

statement:
You are working on a project that has planned as much as they can
up-front. You would like them to move to a more agile way of work.
Currently, the work is hidden, the team catches up only once a week in a
working group, and it is hard to see what each person is working on and
keep track of it. What will you do next?
option1:
Ask your team to report on their work in a written status update each
day so you can keep track.
option2:
Have more working group meetings so you are always up to date on what
your team is working on.
option3:
Ask the team to move their work to a central, visible task board, then
have a short meeting each day to discuss progress on the cards.
option4:
Remove the team meetings altogether and trust your team to get the work
done.
explanation:
This question is talking about stand-ups and a Kanban board. "Task
boards" are part of the visual controls used, where an increment of work
moves from To Do to In Progress to Done. The team meets for 15 minutes
each day to discuss progress and raise any impediments. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p109, "Visual Controls: Task boards"

statement:
You are working on a hybrid project, where the majority of the work has
been planned up front. The team manages their work via a Kanban board,
and report on progress and blockers each day. As the project evolves,
stakeholders have added more and more scope to the product, with one
executive raising an urgent feature that needs to be added. What will
you do next?
option1:
Limit the number of cards in the "Ready" or "To Do" column, and ask the
Product Owner to work with stakeholder requests, noting the need to swap
the new item for an existing item.
option2:
Match the number of user stories to the team's velocity, to ensure a
sustainable pace.
option3:
Work with the stakeholder to raise a change request, and note the
outcome in the change log.
option4:
Review the impact of the scope change with the stakeholder using the WBS
dictionary.
explanation:
As a hybrid project using Kanban and some Agile ceremonies, the most
appropriate way to manage the work and scope is by using a WIP Limit on
the board. The Product Owner has the final say on prioritising features
and scope, so works with stakeholders to ensure they understand
something may be de-prioritised by adding a new feature. (PMBOK Guide)
-- Seventh Edition, 2021, p110, "Kanban Board"

statement:
You are working as a product owner in an agile team, where the main
measure of project success is the number of features delivered into
production. Over the past six months, the team have delivered the
highest number of features your organisation has ever seen, however
customers are starting to complain about issues and bugs in the product.
What will you do next?
option1:
Ask the team to work harder in delivering more features, to fix the
product issues.
option2:
Adjust the product metrics to include customer satisfaction and quality
measures.
option3:
Add a slack card of 5 points into the sprint backlog, to deal with the
technical debt.
option4:
Remove customer comments and feedback until you have finished delivering
all the features.
explanation:
This question is about the Hawthorne Effect, where what we measure
influences the behaviour of our team. In this case, we measured the
number of features only, and the team delivered, but at the cost of
quality and customer satisfaction. Adding measures for those two things
will balance the team's behaviour. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition,
2021, p112, "Measurement pitfalls: Hawthorn Effect"

statement:
You have been working on a project for the past eight months. Your
project team have been approached by multiple stakeholders for reporting
on various performance measures about the project and the product area.
Now, a large amount of their time is spent on creating these reports for
others. They have escalated this issue to you. What will you do next?
option1: Ask the project stakeholders to stop interacting with your project team.
option2:
Spend a portion of your project budget to automate the reports for your
stakeholders.
option3:
Work with the team, including the stakeholders, to only measure things
that will facilitate a decision, help avoid an issue or increase project
performance.
option4:
Invite your stakeholders to your sprint reviews, so they can see a
working feature in practice.
explanation:
The intent in measuring and displaying data is to learn and improve. To
optimise project performance and efficiency, only measure and report
information that will allow the project team to learn, facilitate a
decision, improve some aspect of performance, help avoid an issue, or
help prevent performance deterioration. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p114, "Growing and Improving"

statement:
You are working on a project where there is a significant amount of
uncertainty about the requirements. There is also a legislative change
coming, and no one knows what it will mean for your industry. As a
project manager, how will you deal with this uncertainty?
option1:
Perform additional planning for your project to improve your view on
scope management.
option2:
Hold additional retrospectives, to capture challenges and improvements
from your team.
option3:
Create a risk assessment of all possible project risks, with their risk
ratings and owners.
option4:
Gather information and prepare for multiple outcomes, and build team
resilience through processes and training.
explanation:
This question is about general uncertainty and risk. Uncertainty cannot
be predicted precisely, and methods for responding to uncertainty
include gathering information, preparing for multiple outcomes, using
set-based design, and building in resilience. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p118, "General Uncertainty"

statement:
You are working on a project with a vendor where there is significant
ambiguity involved and multiple options the team could go with. Many
outcomes are possible that have varying degrees of impact to improve the
situation. What will you do next?
option1:
Use experiments and prototypes, and progressive elaboration to work your
way through it.
option2:
Ask your team for additional reporting around the different options so
you can make an informed decision.
option3:
Add all the different options into your project scope, so all your bases
are covered.
option4:
Work more closely with your senior users so they can inform you of the
product requirements.
explanation:
This question is on Ambiguity. There are two types of ambiguity:
Conceptual (where it is hard to understand) and situational (where there
are multiple options and outcomes). Use progressive elaboration,
experiments and prototypes to work through this ambiguity. (PMBOK Guide)
-- Seventh Edition, 2021, p120, "Ambiguity"

statement:
You are working on a project to improve some software that has been in
production for over 17 years, and has been modified extensively over
that time. It also integrates with many other systems within your
organisation. You cannot decide which features to deliver first. How
will you deal with the complexity?
option1:
List out the possible features you can deliver and prioritise them by
cost over benefit.
option2:
Simulate decoupling connecting parts to simplify the number of
variables, then reframe your view by brainstorming with a diverse set of
stakeholders.
option3:
Provide an incentive for your project team for whoever can solve this
complex problem.
option4:
Form a business-case for a third party system to replace the existing,
complex system, to transfer the risk of delivery.
explanation:
This question is about Complexity. Methods for dealing with complexity
include Systems based -- decoupling and simulation; Reframing --
Diversity and balanced views; Process based -- Iterating, engaging
stakeholders and using a fail safe or error proofing. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p121, "Complexity."

statement:
You are working on a project that requires a very particular programming
skillset that is not very common. It is also the hottest job-market the
industry has seen in two decades, people are hard to come by and leave
often. The programming language is also evolving at a quick pace. What
will you do next?
option1: Develop the product from scratch in a more common programming language.
option2:
Ask your whole team to learn the programming skillset, to cover the
risk.
option3:
Use alternatives analysis to gather different options, and ensure a
sufficient cost reserve to help complete your project.
option4:
Raise a risk to your project completion, with the threat of project team
member skillsets.
explanation:
This question is referring to Volatility, which is an environment that
is subject to rapid and unpredictable change, including ongoing
fluctuations in available skillsets or materials. Alternatives analysis
and cost reserves are ways to deal with volatility. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p122, "Volatility\"

statement:
Your project team have found a significant data vulnerability in one of
the features planned in your product backlog, where customer data is
exposed without password access. This feature is partially made by a
third party vendor, and the relationship is owned by a functional
manager in your organisation. What will you do next?
option1:
Avoid the risk and wait for the third party vendor to fix the
vulnerability.
option2:
Transfer the risk by asking a different vendor to create a feature with
no vulnerability in their design.
option3:
Escalate the threat to the manager with appropriate authority to act on
the response.
option4:
Accept the risk and move on the other features so the work continues to
get done.
explanation:
With project risks and responses to threats, there are five main types:
Avoid, Escalate, Transfer, Mitigate and Accept. Escalation is
appropriate when the project team agrees that a threat is outside the
scope of the project, or exceeds the project manager's authority. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p123, "Overall Project Risk: Threats"

statement:
You brainstorm risk responses with your project team and project
sponsor, and decide you cannot transfer a certain high impact risk. You
also cannot avoid the risk, and there is no one in the organisation to
escalate it to. Your project sponsor agrees that you should actively
accept the risk. What will you do next?
option1:
Develop a contingency plan that would be triggered if the event
occurred.
option2:
Shift the ownership to a third party to manage the risk and bear the
impact if it does occur.
option3: Act to eliminate the threat or protect the project from its impact only.
option4:
Take action to reduce the probability of its occurrence, and the impact
of the threat.
explanation:
This question is describing the various risk responses to threats.
Actively accepting a risk can include developing a contingency plan that
would be triggered if the event occurred. Passive acceptance means doing
nothing. Acting to eliminate the threat is avoiding it, escalating
requires an outside manager's authority, shifting the ownership is
transferring the risk, and mitigating the risk is taking action to
reduce the probability of occurrence or impact. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p123, "Risk: Threats"

statement:
You are working on a project to build a new customer database system for
an engineering team. In working with the requirements, you realise that
this system can also work with other teams within your organisation, and
the effort to add those teams is quite small. What will you do next?
option1:
Work with the other teams and their managers to escalate the
opportunity.
option2:
Work with those other teams and their managers to exploit the
opportunity.
option3:
Work with those other teams and their managers to enhance the
opportunity.
option4: Work with the other teams and their managers to accept the opportunity.
explanation:
When dealing with opportunities in your project there are five main
responses: Exploit is acting to ensure an opportunity occurs, Escalating
is when it is outside the authority of the project manager, sharing
allocates ownership to a third party who is best able to capture its
benefit, enhancing is increasing the probability of occurrence, and
accepting merely acknowledges its existence without doing anything
further. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p125, "Risks:
Opportunities"

statement:
You are working as a product owner in an agile team, and during a recent
risk review the team have uncovered various risks that will impact your
project. You brainstorm risk responses with your team. The project
customer would like them to be mitigated as soon as possible. What will
you do next?
option1:
Ask your team to work on the risk responses and get them done as soon as
possible.
option2:
Ignore the risks initially, some may dissipate over time and only the
important ones will be left.
option3:
Assign the risk responses to all stakeholders around your project team
to share the load.
option4:
Prioritise the risks by comparing the expected monetary value of the
risk to the anticipated return on investment of upcoming deliverables.
explanation:
Prioritising risk responses in a risk adjusted backlog is just as
important as prioritising features, to avoid negative risk impacts and
technical debt. Comparing the EMV of a risk to the anticipated ROI of a
deliverable allows product owners to incorporate risk responses into the
planned work. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p15, "Checking
outcomes -- Stakeholder Performance"

statement:
You are planning a project where supply chains have been impacted, and
the requirements are complex and intertwined with other areas of the
business. Your project sponsor asks you to manage these risks before the
project moves to execution. What will you do next?
option1: Move to project execution, so you can work through risks as they happen.
option2:
Move to an agile way of work so you can deliver faster and also deal
with the complexity.
option3:
Note the risks with appropriate controls, and add a contingency reserve
in the project budget.
option4:
Assign a management reserve to your project budget and deal with the
risks before moving to project execution.
explanation:
Reserves are an amount of time or budget set aside to account for
handling risks. Contingency reserve is for identified risks, should they
occur. Management reserve is for unknown events such as un-planned,
in-scope work. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p127, "Management
and contingency reserve"

statement:
You are working on an agile project and delivering a usable feature
every few weeks. The project sponsor is from a waterfall background and
is concerned that there is not enough focus on risk. They would like to
hold a regular risk review with a wide group of stakeholders. What will
you suggest instead?
option1:
There is no need for a separate meeting as risk is everyone\'s
responsibility in an agile team.
option2:
Use daily stand-ups to report blockers early, and fortnightly
demonstrations of the product to catch any stakeholder dissatisfaction.
option3:
Use the Kanban board to see the work and how it flows from the backlog
to \"done\".
option4:
Hold the risk meeting, but only with the immediate project team to avoid
unnecessary participants.
explanation:
In agile teams the risk review can take the form of the daily stand-up,
reporting blockers that could become threats if they continue to delay
progress. Frequent demonstrations of the product can also surface
threats and opportunities, as can retrospectives. While quality is
everyone\'s responsibility, and the Kanban board does visualise the
work, B is the best answer. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p127, "Risk Review"

statement:
Your Project Management Office has no prescribed way of work, so are
working with a vendor who supplies an eXtreme Programming framework at a
significant cost. Your organisation is using it, but projects are still
running behind schedule, over budget, and with low customer
satisfaction. What will you do next?
option1:
Tailor the approach to suit the organisation, operating environment and
project needs.
option2:
Use a waterfall approach instead, to ensure the proper planning is
completed for every project.
option3:
Switch to Scrum instead, as it is a more popular framework and has been
tested in the market.
option4:
Tighten the controls around the project environment with a more
directive PMO approach.
explanation:
Projects operate in complex environments that need to balance competing
demands. Tailoring is performed to better suit the organisation,
operating environment and project needs. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p132, "Tailoring Overview"

statement:
Your Project Management Office is in the middle of a company-wide \"ways
of working\" transformation. They have taken a popular project framework
and are tailoring it to the organisation with branded templates and
processes that meet their existing governance requirements. You have
been asked to help finalise the process. What will you do next?
option1:
Gather feedback from the functional business areas to ensure the process
meets their needs.
option2:
Roll out the new way of working to the PMO as a pilot first, then to the
rest of the organisation.
option3:
Enable a process for tailoring based on size and complexity, and
implement ongoing improvement.
option4: Form a working group comprised of executives and project professionals.
explanation:
This question is referring to the tailoring process, which is: Select
the initial development approach, tailor for the organisation, tailor
for the project, and implement ongoing improvement. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p137, "The Tailoring Process"

statement:
You are a manager of a PMO in a large organisation, which has recently
undertaken a company-wide way of working transformation. You need to
select an initial development approach for your projects, which you will
then tailor to suit them further. What will you do next?
option1:
Use the framework that best engages your executive team through
easy-to-use graphics and presentations.
option2:
Select the most popular current framework to keep up with what other
organisations are doing.
option3:
Select multiple development approaches as a baseline for your
organisation.
option4:
Use a suitability filter to assess the most appropriate development
approach.
explanation:
A suitability filter tool helps project teams consider whether a project
has characteristics that lend themselves toward a predictive, hybrid, or
adaptive approach. Select one as a base, then enable the ability to
tailor to your project or organisation\'s circumstances. (PMBOK Guide)
-- Seventh Edition, 2021, p138, "Select Initial Development Approach."

statement:
Your organisation is starting to move to an agile way of work and they
are setting up a Value Delivery Office (VDO) to complement the existing
PMO. The executives in the area have begun working through what the
function looks like and the roles and responsibilities that might exist.
They approach you for help. What will you tell them?
option1:
The VDO is responsible for the value delivered, and owns the programs
within the organisation.
option2:
The VDO focuses on coaching project teams, building adaptive skills and
capabilities and mentoring sponsors and product owners to be more
effective.
option3:
The VDO is responsible for agile resources such as scrum masters and
product owners to be allocated effectively to project teams.
option4:
The VDO creates the organisational framework to be used on each project
throughout the company.
explanation:
A VDO serves an enabling role, rather than a management or oversight
function. It focuses on coaching project teams, building adaptive skills
and capabilities throughout the organisation, and mentoring sponsors and
product owners. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p141, "Tailor
for the organisation: VDO\"

statement:
You have been planning a project for the last two months, gathering
requirements and scope and assigning tasks to a schedule. As you get
closer to the execution phase of your project you need to decide what
delivery approach you will use. The requirements are likely to change,
require a medium amount of compliance, but can be released in one go.
What will you do next?
option1: Set up a Predictive delivery approach with your team.
option2: Set up an Incremental delivery approach with your team.
option3: Set up an Agile or adaptive delivery approach with your team.
option4: Set up an Iterative delivery approach with your team.
explanation:
Gathering feedback and improving over time, while delivering in one
release is an iterative delivery approach. Incremental releases
increments of value (like features), Predictive plans everything up
front once then delivers in one go, and Agile gathers feedback and
releases incrementally. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p141,
"Tailor for the project: Product / Deliverable"

statement:
You have been asked to make a process improvement in the accounting area
of your organisation. You have been assigned a small team of nine
people, each with all the skills you need to complete the project. The
team is co-located, and have unlimited access to the project customer.
How will you tailor your delivery approach?
option1: Use a predictive / waterfall delivery approach.
option2: Use an agile / adaptive delivery approach.
option3: Use an incremental delivery approach.
option4: Use a hybrid delivery approach.
explanation:
An agile / adaptive delivery approach works best when the team is small
(fewer than 9 -- 12 people), has close access to the customer, is
cross-functional and is co-located. Predictive is best when lots of
planning is needed, Hybrid is best when there are two different
approaches needed. Incremental delivers features over time. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p142, "Tailor for the project: Project
team."

statement:
You have been working as a project manager in an organisation for some
time, where an agile-centric delivery approach and way of work has been
selected and tailored for the organisation. Over the past few months,
you notice several things that are not working and could be improved as
you begin new projects. What will you do next?
option1:
Review the product backlog to see the flow of features going through
project teams.
option2: Look into issues, threats and quality assurance statistics.
option3:
Suggest a retrospective with PMO leaders to determine ongoing
improvement opportunities.
option4:
Review a control chart and value stream map to see non-value-added
activities and remove waste in the process.
explanation:
In an agile environment, holding a retrospective to gather what is
working well and what is challenging us or could be improved is the best
way to find ongoing improvements. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition,
2021, p151, "Tailoring: Diagnostics"

statement:
You have been working on a project to deliver a new customer data
system, and have noticed that the work is starting to slow down. Fewer
user stories are appearing in your sprint backlog, as they are not
ready. You hold a retrospective with your team and discover that there
are long delays waiting for requirements and acceptance criteria
approvals. What will you do next?
option1:
Streamline approval decisions through fewer people (such as a Product
Owner) authorised to make decisions up to certain value thresholds.
option2:
Ask your team to place the user stories in the sprint backlog, reducing
the need for customer approval.
option3:
Note the long delays as a risk to your project, with a high likelihood
as it is currently happening.
option4:
Escalate the issue to the functional manager to ensure approvals are
happening quickly.
explanation:
Where there are long delays or waiting for approvals, the tailoring
suggestion is to streamline decisions through fewer people authorised to
make decisions up to certain value thresholds. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p151, "Common situations and tailoring suggestions"

statement:
You have been assigned to take over an existing project as the previous
project manager leaves for another opportunity. In working with your
team you notice there is a large amount of work currently in progress
all at once, with team members working on multiple items at a time and
constantly context-switching between tasks. What will you do next?
option1: Add more people to your project to get the work done at a faster rate.
option2:
Use value stream mapping and Kanban boards to visualise the work and
brainstorm solutions with your team.
option3:
Ask your team to work overtime to cover the extra work and bring it back
into line.
option4:
Review the scope of the project and reduce items with a low impact to
customer satisfaction.
explanation:
Where there is too much Work In Progress (WIP) or high rates of scrap,
it is recommended to use techniques like value Stream Mapping and Kanban
Boards to visualise the work, identify the issues, and propose
solutions. Adding more people can also complicate communication and
processes. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p151, "Common
situations and tailoring suggestions"

statement:
The product owner has prioritised the product backlog of features, and
the team creates user stories with estimates and places enough to fill a
two week sprint. You notice that the pieces being completed are being
rejected by the customer for defects and missed requirements. What will
you do next?
option1:
Create the project scope from scratch with the project team and
customers so it is correct.
option2:
Have an "Iteration zero" planning sprint with your project team to set
it up for success.
option3:
Add more feedback loops such as shoulder checks, peer review, unit and
system testing.
option4:
Have less frequent customer demonstrations, so they only see the product
when it is correct.
explanation:
When a product is facing poor quality deliverables, the tailoring
suggestion is to add more feedback verification loops and quality
assurance steps, including things such as demonstrations and reviews,
peer review, shoulder checks, pair programming, quality audits or
testing (unit, system, user, regression, and automated). (PMBOK Guide)
-- Seventh Edition, 2021, p151, "Common situations and tailoring
suggestions"

statement:
Your project team has started to find their rhythm, yet you notice that
they seem disengaged in their work and are dragging their feet on normal
tasks. You check your project budget and note that the team are all paid
fairly for the market and within the same range for the role. What will
you do next?
option1:
Look for ways to reward team members with bonuses above their normal pay
rate.
option2:
Move team members around to different roles frequently, so they
experience job variety.
option3: Give the team a paid day off to show how much you appreciate their work.
option4:
Establish ways for the team to grow and advance, such as career paths or
learning pathways.
explanation:
Frederick Herzberg conducted a study of motivational factors in working
life, and found hygiene factors (company policies, salary and the
physical environment) and motivational factors (achievement, growth and
advancement). If hygiene factors are insufficient, they cause
dissatisfaction. If they are sufficient, then use motivational factors
to provide satisfaction. Paying bonuses or a paid day off may seem like
a good idea, but will fade and not address the root cause of engagement.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p158, "Hygiene and motivational
Factors"

statement:
You are working as a project manager on a regulatory change for your
organisation. You notice that the team are not communicating as well as
they could be. You put together a plan to improve team communication by
increasing its effectiveness and richness. What will you do?
option1:
Include methods that will handle multiple information cues, facilitate
rapid feedback, and utilise natural language.
option2:
Include methods to escalate issues easily, with executives being open
for anything the team needs.
option3:
Include ways to get sign off in writing, so it can be recorded and
reviewed later when needed.
option4:
Include communication that ensures everyone gets a voice and input into
decisions.
explanation:
Alistair Cockburn developed a model that describes communication
channels along the axes of effectiveness and richness. Media richness
includes the ability to handle multiple information cues, facilitate
rapid feedback, establish a personal focus and utilise natural language
-- for example face to face communication, or co-located teams. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p157, "Effectiveness of communication
channels"

statement:
Your deliverables have been signed off by the Product Owner and are
ready to be released to the customer, which is the financial analytics
team of your organisation. The new system will be quite a change from
their normal day-to-day work. The team already have the desire to use
the new system. What will you do next?
option1: Tell them the vision and why the change is necessary.
option2: Use training and education to show them the new processes and systems.
option3:
Give them unlimited hands-on practice so they can get used to the new
system.
option4: Include rewards, recognition, feedback and measurement.
explanation:
The ADKAR model for change management has five steps: Awareness, Desire,
Knowledge, Ability and Reinforcement. Once people have the desire for
the change, the next step is knowledge which includes training and
education for the new processes and systems. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p161, "ADKAR Model."

statement:
You are leading a large organisational change that affects more than a
thousand people across multiple teams. You have engaged with the senior
managers of that area, created a brief vision statement that summarises
the change, and created a strategy to realise the vision. You
communicate the vision throughout the change process. What will you do
next?
option1:
Release multiple usable features to the affected teams so they can get
used to it gradually.
option2:
Anchor the changes into corporate culture by telling success stories and
recognising people who embody the change.
option3:
Address the obstacles to change, including outdated processes and people
resistance.
option4:
Set goals for continued improvement on the change throughout the
organisation.
explanation:
John Kotter's 8-step process for leading change is a top down approach:
1: Create urgency, 2: Form a powerful coalition, 3: Create a vision for
change, 4: Communicate the vision, 5: Remove obstacles, 6: Create short
term wins, 7: Build on the change, 8: Anchor the changes in corporate
culture. After communicating the vision comes removing obstacles. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p162, "The 8-Step Process for Leading
Change\"

statement:
You are a project manager assessing the risk of future releases with
your team. You have a clear set of requirements from your customer,
however there are a range of possible correct solutions, that may or may
not get the outcome the customer needs. There are a range of known
unknowns. How will you approach this situation?
option1: Use current best practices to make decisions.
option2:
Use emergent practices that allow you to probe the environment, sense
the situation, and respond with action.
option3: Assess the facts, analyse the situation and apply known good practices.
option4:
Sense where there is some stability then take steps to try and stabilise
the situation.
explanation:
In the Cynefin Framework, complicated relationships exist when there is
a set of known unknowns, or a range of correct answers. In these
situations it is best to assess the facts, analyse the situation, and
apply good practices. Answer B is for complex (unknown unknowns), D is
chaotic (no clear cause or effect), A is for obvious cause and effect.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p 164, \"Cynefin Framework\"

statement:
You have been asked to initiate a project to deliver a new system. The
requirements are not clear, and it will take some work to uncover them.
The system has been used by some team members but not all and has a
medium level of uncertainty around it. How will you approach this
project?
option1:
Release small usable features and improve your approach based on
feedback.
option2: Do additional planning up-front to ensure every component is thought of.
option3:
Split the team into two and work on different parts of the system to
simplify it.
option4:
Form a business case for starting from scratch with new, easier to use
technology.
explanation:
The Stacey Matrix looks at two dimensions to determine complexity:
uncertainty of the requirements, and uncertainty of or within the
technology. This labels a project simple, complicated, complex or
chaotic. Agile methodologies work best in complicated and complex
environments. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p165, \"Stacey
Matrix\"

statement:
Your project team came together from different parts of the
organisation, and were introduced formally to each other. Now you notice
there is some conflict within the team. Some people don't respect
others' decisions, are not working together well, and there are some
personality clashes within the team. What will you do next?
option1:
Help team members get to know each other, their names, position on the
team, and other background information.
option2:
Help the team understand their roles and responsibilities and form a
regular rhythm of meetings, collaboration and work.
option3:
Help the team jockey for position within the team, and foster healthy
conflict.
option4:
Help the team complete the project work and disperse to work on other
things
explanation:
This scenario describes the five stages of team development -- the
Tuckman Ladder. When there is conflict and difficulty working together,
the team is in the Storming stage -- we should help them understand
their roles, responsibilities and find a regular rhythm so they can move
to the Norming stage. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p166,
"Tuckman Ladder"

statement:
You have formed a new project team and have worked through the purpose
and mission (the \"why\") of the project with them. You expanded on
*who* is on the project team and the skills and abilities each person
brings. You would like to foster a high performing culture before you
begin project delivery. What will you do next?
option1:
Work through changes on the project team and the project such as
deliverables and stakeholders.
option2:
Decompose the high level plan into greater detail, including a detailed
schedule or backlog.
option3:
Define a plan to achieve the project goals, including milestone
schedules, release plans and high-level budgets.
option4: Clarify the goals of the team and exactly what you are here to deliver.
explanation:
This question refers to the Drexler/Sibbet Team Performance model. The
steps include: Orientation, Trust Building, Goal Clarification,
Commitment, Implementation, High performance and Renewal. After
Orientation (the \"why\") comes Trust building (the \"who\"), and then
goal clarification (the \"what\"). After that we can define a plan to
achieve those goals (the \"how\"). (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition,
2021, p167, \"Drexler/Sibbet Team Performance Model\"

statement:
Your team members have raised an issue as they believe the current
project scope will not reach the intended project goal of increasing
customer retention. You take their concerns to the Project Sponsor who
controls the funding and resources. The sponsor dismisses them and
directs you to proceed with the scope currently set. What will you do
next?
option1: Problem solve with the sponsor and find a resolution.
option2:
Compromise with the sponsor so each party gets what a part of what they
want.
option3: Accommodate the sponsor to maintain harmony and good will.
option4:
Force the issue so the sponsor accepts the change in requirements from
the team.
explanation:
Ken Thomas and Ralph Kilmann describe six ways to address conflict,
depending on the relative power between the individuals. There is
confronting/problem solving, collaborating, compromising, accommodating,
forcing and withdrawing. Since there is a desire to maintain a good
relationship with the sponsor, adopting an accommodating posture may be
appropriate. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p168, 169,
\"Conflict Model\"

statement:
The functional manager raises a concern that they have not been engaged
correctly and the program is missing some key requirements as a result.
You believe you have captured all the items required. Your program
manager asks you to handle the situation and approach it from a win-win
perspective. What will you do next?
option1:
Escalate the decision to the steering committee to ensure your point of
view is heard.
option2:
Demonstrate integrity, find mutual trust, and look at the situation from
their point of view.
option3:
Choose to give some lee-way in your approach so the functional manager
can add their requirements.
option4:
Get your requirements baselined first then compete with the manager to
ensure a win.
explanation:
Steven Covey\'s principle of \"Think Win-Win\" shows different possible
outcomes: Win-Win, Win-Lose, Lose-Lose. A win-win approach is found when
there is mature character, trust, and the ability to approach from the
other\'s point of view. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p169,
\"Negotiation\"

statement:
You have been planning a project to deliver a process change for the
past four weeks. The requirements and scope have been signed off by the
business owner, and the team moves into delivery and execution. The work
has now begun, in line with the project plan and scope. What will you do
next?
option1: Monitor and control the work being done to ensure it meets its goals.
option2:
Execute the project work to complete the work defined in the project
plan.
option3: Plan the project work to ensure sufficient understanding.
option4: Formally sign off and complete the project phase.
explanation:
The project management process groups are Initiating, Planning,
Executing, Monitoring and Controlling, and Closing. They may be
performed in sequence, or iteratively and ongoing. As project work is
executed, it needs to be monitored and controlled. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p170, \"Process groups\"

statement:
You are working as a Product Owner. While your sales and revenue have
been increasing over the past eight months, the overall profit for your
product has decreased by 17%. Your executive manager asks you to address
the situation. What will you do next?
option1:
Stop the current work and set a planning session with your team to
address the situation.
option2:
Ask your team to work overtime on the current features in your backlog
and get the work done.
option3:
Perform benchmarking with a similar yet non-competitive company, and add
any process improvements to your product backlog.
option4:
Look for opportunities to partner with other products in your
organisation and increase your sales further.
explanation:
As a product owner, your responsibility is to increase users, sales
revenue and profit for your product. If profit is decreasing while sales
revenue is increasing, the product is costing more to produce or sell.
Performing benchmarking to find gaps in your process then executing them
with your team will help. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p175,
\"Data gathering and analysis: Benchmarking\"

statement:
You have been given authority to manage the upcoming features your team
will release. You do not have a high enough level of expertise in the
product, so you gather senior users from the product\'s area to create a
features list. Each stakeholder wants their feature to be delivered
first. How will you prioritise them and move forward?
option1:
Go with the features wanted by the top level executives first, to avoid
issues later on.
option2: Perform a MoSCoW analysis with the stakeholders.
option3:
Perform a root cause analysis and go with features that address the root
cause.
option4:
Use a cost benefit analysis and compare the time needed to recover the
project cost.
explanation:
Cost to benefit, and the payback period (time to recover an investment)
are the best accepted ways to prioritise business value. Root cause
analysis is better for specific problems. Performing MoSCoW with the
stakeholders may result in each of them wanting their feature to be
\"Must Have\". (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p175,\"Business
justification analysis methods\"

statement:
You are creating a business case and project charter with a small
initial project team. You have a high level feature, decomposed into
smaller release milestones, and you would like to note a high-level
schedule using wideband delphi or a high-level version of planning
poker. What will you do next?
option1:
Hold a round of cards with points on them, where each team member places
their cards in the centre for the total estimate.
option2:
Ask subject matter experts to complete multiple rounds of estimates
individually, compare, and explain the highest and lowest estimates for
each round.
option3:
Show the wideband high level range from lowest to highest for each
feature.
option4:
Find the business functionality for each feature and estimate the effort
based on that.
explanation:
The Wideband Delphi technique asks subject matter experts to complete
multiple rounds of estimates individually, compare, and explain the
highest and lowest estimates for each round. Planning poker is a
variation of wideband Delphi. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p178, \"Estimating: Wideband Delphi\"

statement:
You have been asked to help facilitate a senior planning meeting in your
organisation. High level executives have made a presentation on the
strengths and opportunities of the organisation, painting a rosy
picture. Revenue has fallen recently, and the chief executive asks you
to level out their analysis a more complete view. What will you do next?
option1: Add your organisation\'s competitors and compare their products.
option2:
Showcase the pipeline of upcoming work to be delivered in the next
quarter.
option3:
Add in the background and abilities of the executive team, that will
allow the organisation to deliver.
option4:
Brainstorm and capture the weaknesses of the organisation and threats to
the organisation.
explanation:
This question is referring to SWOT analysis: Strengths, Weaknesses,
Opportunities and Threats. Once strengths and opportunities have been
captured, a complete picture also involves the weaknesses and threats.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p175, \"SWOT analysis\"

statement:
You are adding an essential feature to your product roadmap. Your team
believe they can complete the feature in six weeks, however the feature
is quite large and a bit more complicated than normal. One of your lead
developers says they worked on a similar feature in another company, and
it took around four months to complete. What will you do next?
option1:
Use relative estimating, assigning the amount of points your team agree
on to the product roadmap.
option2:
Use analogous estimating, and place the feature with around four months
to complete.
option3:
Use parametric estimating, and place the feature with around four months
to complete.
option4:
Use function point estimating to show the amount of business
functionality in the feature.
explanation:
When the effort on a feature or deliverable needs to be estimated, and
there are people who have experience delivering a similar item, you can
use analogous estimating to estimate the initial effort quickly and with
a medium-level of accuracy. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p178, \"Estimating: Analogous estimating\"

statement:
You have a prioritised product backlog of features, and have decomposed
the next feature to be delivered into user stories, small enough to
complete in one sprint of two weeks. The team would like to estimate the
effort involved on those user stories, but are unsure on how to proceed.
What will you do next?
option1:
Ask each team member to add their own estimates as they complete the
acceptance criteria.
option2:
Estimate the effort for the overall feature to reduce the need for
individual story estimates.
option3:
Help the team perform relative estimating together, where the stories
are sized in points relative to the smallest piece.
option4:
Base the estimates on cost per hour, and add them after the work is done
to ensure it is correct.
explanation:
Relative estimating is the most common method for user stories, where
they are sized in comparison to the smallest piece - often in story
points and often on a Fibonacci scale of 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p178, \"Relative estimating\"

statement:
You are half way through your current sprint of two weeks. The work is
progressing well, however you notice that there is not enough work ready
and elaborated for when the next sprint begins. What will you do next?
option1:
Hold a backlog refinement meeting and work with the team to identify the
work that could be accomplished.
option2:
Raise the issue as a blocker in the daily stand-up and ask your team to
swarm around the problem.
option3:
Raise the issue in the next retrospective and take an action for the
team.
option4:
Place the cards on the Kanban board so the team can see the work and get
started.
explanation:
At a backlog refinement meeting, the backlog is progressively elaborated
and (re)prioritised to identify the work that can be accomplished in an
upcoming iteration. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p179,
\"Meetings and Events: Backlog refinement\"

statement:
You have completed a make-or-buy analysis in your project and found that
part of your deliverable is best created by an external vendor. You work
with your project team to create the procurement strategy, source
selection criteria and statement of work. What will you do next?
option1:
Gather seller proposals, including technical details and price
(separately).
option2:
Meet with prospective sellers to ensure all vendors have a common
understanding of the procurement.
option3:
Perform source selection analysis on what you want from prospective
vendors.
option4:
Create the procurement agreement for the third party vendor to provide
their specified services.
explanation:
These are outputs of Plan Procurement Management. A bidder conference is
meeting with prospective sellers prior to the preparation of a bid or
proposal, to ensure all prospective bidders have a clear and common
understanding of the procurement. This meeting may also be known as
contractor conferences, vendor conferences, or pre-bid conferences.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p179, \"Bidder conference\"

statement:
One of the stakeholders in your project has raised a change request,
after some of the deliverables contained defects and a missed
requirement. Your project has a formal change control process, as
opposed to a more agile prioritised product backlog. What will you do
next?
option1:
Gather feedback from other stakeholders to see if they also need the
change.
option2:
Note the change in the change log, then place the requested item in the
next sprint.
option3:
Take the change to the CCB, then communicate the decision to approve or
reject the change.
option4:
Raise the request as an issue that will affect your project schedule and
budget.
explanation:
Where a formal change control process is in place, a Change Control
Board includes the people who are accountable for reviewing, approving
or rejecting changes to the project. The decisions made at this meeting
are recorded and communicated to the appropriate stakeholders. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p179, \"Change Control Board\"

statement:
Your agile project has recently gained some new stakeholders after a
department restructure. They complain to you that they are not seeing
enough traction of work delivered through the project, and are worried
it will not meet their requirements. What will you do next?
option1:
Invite them to the daily stand up, so they can see the work that happens
on a daily basis.
option2:
Invite them to iteration planning, so they can choose what the team
works on.
option3:
Invite them to the retrospective, so they can raise their issue there
and come up with a solution.
option4:
Invite them to the Iteration Review, so they can see the usable
increment the team delivers.
explanation:
The iteration or sprint review is held at the end of an iteration
(usually of two weeks) to demonstrate the work that was accomplished
during the iteration to the project customers and stakeholders. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p179, \"Iteration Review\"

statement:
You have recently taken over a project. Your project team approach you
to say there is a regulatory approval that has not been granted yet, and
it may block development of your project in the next five months. You
check your project documentation but cannot see any mention of this.
What will you do next?
option1:
Hold a risk review meeting, capture it as a risk, and ensure an adequate
risk response.
option2:
Hold a retrospective with your team to capture what challenges the team
are facing.
option3:
Adjust your product roadmap to reflect the changes to the timeframe of
that deliverable.
option4: Escalate the regulatory approval to the project steering committee.
explanation:
The risk review is a meeting to analyse the status of existing risks and
to identify new risks. This includes changes to probability, impact or
urgency, and determining if responses are adequate. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p180, \"Risk Review\"

statement:
You are an agile project manager, and holding daily stand-ups with your
team. You notice people accusing other team members of not doing their
job and there are some tasks that are not mentioned anywhere and no one
is responsible for. The team velocity has slowed in the last three
sprints. What will you do next?
option1:
Refer to the team charter, and the team roles and agreed ways of
managing conflict.
option2:
Raise it as an issue in the next retrospective and come to a solution
with the team.
option3: Assign team roles to those you think are best suited.
option4:
Work with your team to size user stories correctly, so velocity
improves.
explanation:
The agile approach is to problem solve with the team. A retrospective is
a regularly occurring workshop, similar to a lessons learned meeting,
where the team explore their work in order to improve their process and
the product. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p180,
\"Retrospective\"

statement:
You are managing a large construction project. Due to some delays, the
project schedule has been pushed back by one week. As a result, one of
the project risks has increased slightly, but is still within the
acceptable limits. What will you do next?
option1: Hold a risk review to update the risk response and owners.
option2: Raise a change request for the change to the project schedule.
option3:
Capture the change in your project reporting and take it to the next
status meeting.
option4: Update the sprint backlog to reflect the delayed work.
explanation:
A status meeting is a regularly scheduled event to exchange and analyse
information about the current progress of the project and its
performance. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p180, \"Status
meeting\"

statement:
You are an agile project manager and your team have delivered three
features to improve a customer app over the last three months. Your
product owner needs to know if the features are having the desired
effect. It is a large organisation and there are multiple measures
available. What will you do next?
option1:
Ask them to gather the team velocity and throughput over the past three
months.
option2: Ask them to gather the Benefit-Cost Ratio over the past six months.
option3: Ask them to gather the NPS over the past six months.
option4: Ask them to gather the average app availability and uptime.
explanation:
A product owner prioritises features to improve usability and
profitability of a product. The Net Promoter Score (NPS) measures the
willingness of customers to recommend a product or service to others,
and is used to gauge overall customer satisfaction and loyalty to the
brand or product. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p181, \"Net
promoter score\"

statement:
You are an agile project manager in an agile team developing new
features for an app. There is no Product Owner in your team. An
executive stakeholder with a high influence in the organisation
approaches you and asks you to add a feature that they like. They asked
their friends, who think it is a good idea too. What will you do next?
option1:
Place the feature in the product backlog and prioritise it, using
cost-versus-benefit.
option2: Raise a change request to add the new feature into the scope baseline.
option3:
Gather the requirements for the new feature and ask your team to
elaborate on it.
option4:
Analyse the impact to your project schedule and cost, the add the
feature to your project scope.
explanation:
In agile, we have a product backlog that we prioritize. Prioritization
schema are methods used to prioritize portfolio, program or project
components such as features, requirements or risks. Examples include
multicriteria decision matrices, MoSCoW, Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) and
more. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p181, \"Prioritization
Schema\"

statement:
Your project team are gathering solution ideas for a particular customer
requirement, which is prioritised to be worked on next. The item is
complicated and they have been trying to find a way forward for the past
three weeks. There is not enough work for the upcoming sprint. What will
you do next?
option1:
Start work on the item in the next sprint anyway and elaborate as the
sprint begins.
option2:
Arrange the right people to elaborate the issue with a spike, and
timebox it to two days.
option3: Deprioritise the item until the team can figure out the issue.
option4:
Adjust the project schedule to reflect the extra time taken on
elaboration.
explanation:
A spike is a short, dedicated user story to research or analyse
something. A timebox is a short, fixed period of time in which work is
to be completed, often a few days to a few weeks. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p181, \"Timebox\"

statement:
Your project team have planned the requirements, scope, schedule and
cost for constructing a new building in your organisation. An executive
from another area approaches you, as your project is funded and about to
begin execution, and asks if you can help them deliver a website as part
of your work. What will you do next?
option1: Raise the request as an issue in your project's issue log.
option2:
Add the website to your project scope and adjust your project scope,
schedule and cost.
option3:
Raise a change request for the added scope and review the impact to your
project schedule and cost.
option4:
Offer to help them create a business case for the new idea and get
funding for a new project.
explanation:
The scope and team required to build a website is very different from a
physical building -- a new project with its own funding is the best
option. A business case is a value proposition for a proposed project
that may include financial and non-financial benefits. It typically
precedes a project charter and a formal project approval and kick-off.
(PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p184, \"Business case\"

statement:
You are working on an agile project and are planning your product
backlog for the next three to six months. You have some high level
feature ideas from various parts of your organisation, from front-line
staff to executives, to direct customer research as well. There is
currently not enough detail in each feature, and you are not sure if any
are worth proceeding with. What will you do next?
option1:
Break down each feature into Epics and user stories, then size the work
with your team.
option2:
Add all of the items into your product backlog and work on the ones from
managers first.
option3:
Work with the requestors and your technical experts to create a business
model canvas for each feature.
option4:
Perform precedence diagramming on each feature to determine their
dependencies.
explanation:
The business model canvas is a one-page visual summary that describes
the value proposition, infrastructure, customers and finances. They are
often used in lean start-up situations. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p184, \"Business model canvas\"

statement:
You are working in a new agile team, where the work has started to slow
and there is uncertainty and low team engagement. You set time to create
a team charter with your team, agree on roles and responsibilities and
team cadence. What will you do next?
option1:
Set a team goal for them to be more engaged in the work over the next
three months.
option2:
Create a product vision statement, showing the value you are bringing
and the "why" behind the work.
option3:
Ask the team to contribute more to all pieces of work during each team
meeting.
option4:
Raise it as a people issue for your project and manage it with the
project steering committee.
explanation:
The project vision statement is a concise, high-level description of the
project that states the purpose, and inspires the team to contribute to
the project. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p184, \"Project
Vision Statement\"

statement:
You are integrating a third party system as part of your project. The
developers in your project team have not worked with this system before,
but they believe the language and layout is the same and they should be
able to implement it, adding a bit of time for the uncertainty. They
give a high-level estimate of five weeks. What will you do next?
option1:
Note the estimate of five weeks and update the assumption log with the
technical assumption.
option2:
Don't add the estimate, break down the integration into user stories and
use a bottom-up approach.
option3:
Check the estimate using parametric estimating, to increase its
accuracy.
option4:
Gather your whole team and customers together, and estimate using
wideband delphi (planning poker).
explanation:
An assumption is a factor that is considered to be true, real or
