certain, without proof or demonstration. A constraint is a factor that
limits the options for managing a project, program, portfolio or
process. An assumption log records all assumptions and constraints
throughout the project. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p185,
\"Assumption log\"

statement:
Your agile project has delivered three features so far and is halfway
through delivering the latest improvement. During a brainstorming
session, one of your customers comes up with a new feature idea. You
check the benefit it could have, and it is high. The complexity is low.
What will you do next?
option1:
Add money to your budget to accommodate the additional feature as soon
as possible.
option2:
Elaborate on the feature, break it down into user stories, and estimate
them.
option3: Place the item in the backlog and reprioritise it to be next.
option4:
Create a business case for the new feature outlining the benefit and
costs involved.
explanation:
A backlog is an ordered list of work to be done. Projects may have a
product backlog, a requirements backlog, impediments backlog, etc. Items
in a backlog are prioritised. The prioritised work is then scheduled for
upcoming iterations. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p185,
\"Logs and Registers: Backlog\"

statement:
One of the senior users testing your product found is was missing a key
requirement that he had assumed would be there. It was not noted in the
project scope, which has been baselined. You check the impact to cost
and schedule, raise a change request on his behalf, and take it to the
change control board where it is approved. What will you do next?
option1: Ask your team to elaborate and turn the requirement into user stories.
option2: Place the new requirement into the sprint backlog for the next sprint.
option3:
Add the missed requirement to the issue log and problem solve with your
team to ensure it does not happen again in the future.
option4:
Note the outcome in the change log and communicate it to your
stakeholders.
explanation:
A change log is a comprehensive list of changes submitted during the
project and their current status. A change can be a modification to any
formally controlled deliverable, project management plan component, or
project document. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p185, \"Logs
and Registers: Change log\"

statement:
You are working on a hybrid project to replace an aging customer system
that has just had a feature upgrade release. The senior developer raises
a concern during the daily stand-up that the system doesn't seem to be
able to connect to some parts of the organisation after the upgrade.
What will you do next?
option1:
Re-plan the project to ensure the correct scope is worked on that will
connect to the organisation.
option2:
Raise it in the issue log, assign it to a responsible party and work
with them to find a resolution.
option3:
Raise the concern as a risk and brainstorm risk responses with your
team.
option4: Escalate the issue to the technical manager so they can resolve it.
explanation:
An issue log is a current condition or situation that may have an impact
on the project objectives. An issue log is used to record and monitor
information on active issues. Issues are assigned to a responsible party
for follow up and resolution. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p185, \"Logs and Registers: Issue log\"

statement:
You are working on an agile project, where your project team have raised
multiple risks to project delivery. Some of the risks may impact your
cost and schedule significantly. Your program manager asks you to manage
them using a risk adjusted backlog. What will you do next?
option1:
Adjust your risk register to include the work not completed if the risks
are triggered.
option2:
Adjust your backlog velocity so your end date is not impacted by the
raised risks.
option3:
Gather risk mitigation responses from your retrospectives and assign
them to an owner.
option4: Place at least one risk response in each sprint to manage and close.
explanation:
A risk adjusted backlog is a backlog that includes work and actions to
address threats and opportunities. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition,
2021, p185, \"Risk adjusted backlog\"

statement:
You are delivering a project to a functional area in your organisation.
The project is highly complex and there are many risks to project
delivery. The functional manager would like to see the biggest risks
that are assigned to her. What will you do next?
option1: Show her the risk adjusted backlog.
option2: Show her the risk register.
option3: Show her the risk management plan.
option4: Show her the qualitative risk assessment.
explanation:
A risk register is a repository in which outputs of risk management
processes are recorded. Information can include the person responsible
for managing the risk, the probability and impact, planned risk
responses, and more. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p185,
\"Risk register\"

statement:
Your project has been in progress for the past six months. Someone you
have not met before approaches you and says they have not been engaged
sufficiently, even though he believes his team is highly impacted by the
change. He advises he may block the project as a result. What will you
do next?
option1: Perform stakeholder engagement assessment with the stakeholder.
option2:
Add him to the stakeholder register, including his salary and interests
information.
option3:
Add him to the stakeholder register, including his impact and influence
information.
option4: Raise a risk to project delivery due to the nature of his actions.
explanation:
A stakeholder register records information about project stakeholders,
which includes an assessment and classification of project stakeholders.
We need to know our stakeholder, ensure his details are recorded, and
where he fits before we take further action. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p185, \"Stakeholder register\"

statement:
You are a product owner on an agile project and have created a product
backlog of features in a prioritised order for delivery. You ask the
team to break down the first feature into user stories, and estimate
their effort. What will you do next?
option1: Create an iteration plan with your team.
option2: Create the product roadmap with your team.
option3: Perform a sprint review with your team.
option4: Begin daily stand-ups with your team.
explanation:
An iteration plan is a detailed plan for the current iteration. It
usually matches the estimated work for the sprint to the average sprint
velocity (or how many points are usually completed). Items from the
product backlog are broken down into user stories and placed into
upcoming sprints. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p186, \"Plans:
Iteration plan\"

statement:
You have worked with your project customer to create a list of features
that will bring the most value to them. They would like to see when some
of this value will be delivered and the expected outcomes of each. What
will you do next?
option1:
Show the features at the next sprint review so the customer can see what
they look like.
option2: Place the features on a release plan and show this to the customer.
option3:
Check the features against the velocity chart to see when they might be
delivered.
option4:
Place the features into the next sprint plan and show this to your
customer.
explanation:
The release plan sets expectations for the dates, features and outcomes
expected to be delivered over the course of multiple iterations. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p186, \"Plans: Release plan\"

statement:
You are in the planning stages of you project, where you need to find
out exactly what the project customer needs so you can find a solution
and make it happen. Your business analysts are new to their roles and
are not sure on what their process will be. What will you do next?
option1:
Ask the business analysts to begin writing user stories to place in a
sprint.
option2:
Create the project management plan to outline how the project will be
delivered.
option3:
Work with the business analysts to create a requirements management
plan.
option4: Create a work breakdown structure on the project scope.
explanation:
The requirements management plan is a component of the project
management plan that describes how requirements will be analysed,
documented and managed. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p186,
\"Requirements management plan\"

statement:
You are working on a new software application with a lot of complex
interdependencies. The senior user providing and testing most of the
requirements is concerned that some of those dependencies will cause
errors upon release, if they are not checked thoroughly. What will you
do next?
option1:
Ask the senior user for a written list of all his requirements, then
check they are included in the project scope.
option2: Raise a risk on the software complexity with the project team.
option3: Create an affinity diagram with the senior user.
option4: Review the test plan with the senior user.
explanation:
A test plan is a document that describes the deliverables that will be
tested, tests that will be conducted, and the processes that will be
used in testing. It forms the basis for formally testing the components
and deliverables. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p186, \"Plans:
Test plan\"

statement:
You are planning your project and have captured the requirements from
the project customer. You ask your team to plan the project scope, but
they need to see exactly what needs to be delivered and how each part
relates to each other. What will you do next?
option1: Create a product breakdown structure with your project team.
option2: Create the requirements traceability matrix with your project team.
option3: Create the risk breakdown structure with your project team.
option4: Create the scope management plan with your project team.
explanation:
A product breakdown structure is a hierarchical structure reflecting a
product's components and deliverables. It provides an exhaustive,
hierarchical tree structure of deliverables that make up the project,
arranged in whole-part relationship. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition,
2021, p187, \"Product Breakdown structure\"

statement:
You have just finished breaking down the scope with your project team,
that will meet your customer's requirements. Your program manager wishes
to control the project scope so any changes go through the proper
process. What will you do next?
option1: Create the change management plan, to note how changes will be managed.
option2:
Baseline the project scope and note it in the configuration management
plan.
option3:
Create the scope management plan, with a management rule minimising
changes.
option4:
Create the resource assignment matrix, limiting roles who can approve
scope changes.
explanation:
A baseline is the approved version of a work product or plan, where
actual performance is compared to the baseline to identify variances.
Baselined items are noted in the configuration management plan. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p188, \"Baselines\"

statement:
You have planned and begun the current sprint, where the project team
have committed to delivering and releasing a particular feature. You
need to see how the team are tracking towards their sprint goal. What
will you do next?
option1: Review the team Kanban board.
option2: Review the team velocity chart.
option3: Review the sprint burndown chart.
option4: Perform a sprint review with the team.
explanation:
A burndown chart is a graphical representation of the work remaining in
a timebox (i.e. an iteration or sprint). It compares the ideal trend
with the items actually completed. A sprint review demonstrates what was
created during the sprint once the sprint is complete. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p188, \"Burndown chart\"

statement:
Your agile project has recently been merged into a larger program, and
the program manager is reviewing how the project is tracking. She asks
you how much has been completed in design, but also how much has been
completed in development, testing and sign-off. What will you do next?
option1: Show her the sprint Kanban board.
option2: Take her through the team product backlog.
option3: Show her the team cumulative flow diagram.
option4: Show her the team velocity chart.
explanation:
A Cumulative Flow Diagram indicates features completed over time,
separated at each stage such as features in the backlog, then design,
development, testing, and more. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021,
p188, \"Cumulative Flow diagram\"

statement:
The features developed by your agile team have added some complexity to
your website, delivered quickly with no time to refactor the code. You
believe the user stories are moving slower as a result, but you can't be
sure. What will you do next?
option1: Check the cycle time chart of the team.
option2: Shadow the developers to overview the work they are doing.
option3: View the product roadmap for proposed release dates.
option4:
Review the points in sprint backlog versus the last three sprints'
velocity.
explanation:
A cycle time chart shows the average cycle time of the work items
completed over time. It can be a scatter diagram or a bar chart. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p189, \"Cycle time chart"

statement:
You are working on an agile project with a full product roadmap for the
next six months. Your project team are not sure what the next feature
does or how they should turn it into user stories. What will you do
next?
option1: Review the scope baseline with your team.
option2: Perform story mapping with your team.
option3: Perform sprint planning with your team.
option4: Perform a retrospective with your team.
explanation:
A story map is a visual model of all the features and functionality
desired for a given product, created to give the project team a holistic
view of what they are building and why. It can also break the feature
down into user stories based on functionality. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p190, \"Story map"

statement:
You are a program manager on a large scale agile program, where each
project team has its own scrum master. You need to know the rate at
which work is flowing through each team, so you can see if there are any
blockers or issues and whether they will remain on track. What will you
do next?
option1:
Raise your query at scrum of scrums, so each team knows what they need
to do.
option2: Attend the sprint review of each team at the end of the sprint.
option3: Review the schedule management plan for managing the schedule.
option4:
Review the team velocity charts, and what was committed versus
completed.
explanation:
The velocity chart tracks the rate at which the deliverables are
produced, validated, and accepted within a predefined interval. (PMBOK
Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p190, \"Velocity Chart"

statement:
Your project has been executing for the past five months, and due to an
organisational change a new project sponsor has come on board. He asks
where the project is up to and whether it is on track. You tell him, but
he wants more information. What will you do next?
option1:
Show him the signed project charter, to ensure he honors the original
project agreement.
option2:
Show him the project management plan, including the schedule management
plan.
option3: Review the status report and previous status report with the sponsor.
option4: Invite him to the next sprint review so he can see the progress made.
explanation:
The status report provides a report on the current status of the
project. It may include information on progress since the last report
and forecasts for cost and schedule performance. (PMBOK Guide) --
Seventh Edition, 2021, p190, \"Reports: Status report\"

statement:
Your agile project needs a third party vendor to help complete the work.
The product owner has multiple features on their product roadmap which
may change over time, making the scope of your project uncertain. What
will you do next?
option1:
Ask your product owner to fix the features and scope so the project is
more certain.
option2: Create a cost plus incentive fee contract with your procurement team.
option3: Create a firm fixed price contract with your procurement team.
option4:
Use the external vendor funding to create a project team in-house and
complete the project.
explanation:
Cost reimbursable contracts involve payments to the seller for actual
costs incurred for completing the work, plus a fee representing seller
profit. They are used when the scope is not well defined or subject to
frequent change. Includes Cost plus Award fee, Fixed Fee or Incentive
fee. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p191, \"Cost-reimbursable
contracts \"

statement:
Your project is releasing a number of upgrades to a commercial building
over the next 12 months. Material costs have changed a lot recently. You
need to budget for the materials required, however the volume of
materials may change significantly as the project evolves. What will you
do next?
option1: Create an IDIQ contract with your supplier.
option2: Ensure multiple suppliers are on call to provide materials, when needed.
option3: Ensure a sole supplier for your materials, to guarantee the best price.
option4: Create a firm fixed price contract for the materials.
explanation:
Indefinite delivery, indefinite quantity (IDIQ) provides for an
indefinite quantity of goods or services, with a stated lower and upper
limit, and within a fixed time period. Fixed price will work better if
the quantities are known. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p191,
\"Agreements and Contracts"

statement:
You have completed a make-or-buy analysis and you agree to hire a
third-party vendor to complete part of your product. You create the
statement of work and have a list of acceptable sellers that meet the
source selection criteria. The work is not complex and they understand
the scope. What will you do next?
option1: Send a request for proposal to the sellers.
option2: Send a request for information to the sellers.
option3: Send a request for quotation to the sellers.
option4: Hold a bidders conference to ensure all sellers are on the same page.
explanation:
Bid documents are used to request proposals from prospective sellers.
The best answer here is Request for Quote, as we have the requirements
ready. RFP is used when the item is complex, RFI is when more
information is needed. Bidder conference is used to ensure all sellers
have enough information. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p192,
\"Bid Documents\"

statement:
Your project is new and the team have just come on board. There have
been multiple disagreements and the work is slow as they try to
understand where they fit in the project and the team. What will you do
next?
option1: Create a definition of ready and definition of done with your team.
option2: Create a project charter with your team.
option3: Create a project management plan with your team.
option4: Create a team charter with your team.
explanation:
The project team charter records the team values, agreements and
operating guidelines. It establishes clear expectations regarding
acceptable behaviour by project team members. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh
Edition, 2021, p192, \"Project team charter\"

statement:
You have created a high level view of the features your project will
deliver, and prioritised them. The development team is ready to start
work, but they don\'t know where to start as the information is too high
level. What will you do next?
option1: Ask the team to begin with the highest priority item first.
option2:
Help the team break the features down into User Stories to place in a
sprint.
option3: Hold a retrospective with the team to understand what is blocking them.
option4:
Reprioritise the features into an order that makes sense for the
developers.
explanation:
A user story is a brief description of an outcome for a specific user.
It has enough information at a level which team members can work on an
item. (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p192, \"Other artifacts:
User story"

statement:
You are working on a project to build a new parkland for the community.
The person in charge of parkland design approaches you as they are not
sure what they are supposed to be working on. What will you do next?
option1: Review the Project Scope Statement to see what the team is delivering.
option2:
Review the WBS Dictionary to see the detailed description of the scope
of the work and who it is assigned to.
option3:
Review the Requirements Traceability Matrix to see where the scope comes
from.
option4: Review the Assumption Log, to see who we assume should do the work.
explanation:
The Work Breakdown Structure Dictionary is a detailed description of the
Scope of Work, broken down to the work package level (where it can be
assigned to someone to work on). It includes acceptance criteria, an
assignee, cost information, schedule information and resource
requirements.

statement:
You have taken over as Project Manager for a project that has had issues
delivering on time. You review the Earned Value Management and find the
corresponding SPI and CPI values are 0.86 and 1.12. You advise the
project sponsor, who asks you to bring the project back on track within
the next two months. What will you do next?
option1: Apply schedule crashing to bring the project back on track.
option2: Apply fast tracking to bring the project back on track.
option3:
Update the Risk Register and assign the risk of project failure to an
executive with the power to bring it back on track.
option4: Reduce the project scope to bring the project back on track.
explanation:
The project is behind on Schedule (delivered less than 1.0), but under
budget and ahead on Cost (delivered more than 1.0). This means we have
money to spent, and we need to reduce the schedule. Of the techniques
here, Schedule Crashing throws money and resources at the project to get
it done faster, and becomes the best option presented.

statement:
You are working on a change that includes stakeholders at all levels
from front-line workers to senior executives. After performing
stakeholder analysis you and your team group the stakeholders according
to their level of influence and the impact the change will have on them.
The project sponsor asks what engagement strategy you will use for the
front-line workers. What will you do next?
option1: Manage them Closely
option2: Monitor them only
option3: Keep them Satisfied
option4: Keep them Informed
explanation:
Analysing stakeholders using an Impact/Influence grid, you will have
four quadrants: High Influence and High Impact: Manage closely. High
influence and low impact: Keep satisfied. Low Influence and Low Impact:
monitor them only. Front-line workers in this case have a low influence
but receive a high impact from the project: Keep them informed.

statement:
You are working directly with the project sponsor, in the initial phase
of a brand new project. The project sponsor asks for your help in
creating a document that formally authorises the project, including
details such as the cost-benefit analysis, feasibility study, and high
level risks. What will you do next?
option1: Prepare a project milestone chart, and review with the project team
option2:
Prepare a Business Case, and review with the functional manager and
receiving team
option3:
Prepare a Project Charter, and review with the portfolio governing body
for approval
option4:
Prepare a Project Management Plan, and host a project kick off meeting
to begin
explanation:
The Project Charter is the document that formally authorises the
project. The Business Case, cost benefit analysis and feasibility
studies are inputs into this document and process, to help provide a
link between the project and the strategic objectives of the
organization.

statement:
You are working as a Project Manager and your team is about to deliver
the completed system to the customer. During the final round of testing,
the customer points out that a major piece of functionality is missing,
which should have been in scope but was not previously thought of. What
will you do next?
option1:
Deliver the system to the customer as it is with the promise to add the
functionality later.
option2: Ask the sponsor to accept the risk and move on.
option3:
Utilise your project budget's Contingency Reserves to add the
functionality before release.
option4:
Utilise your project's Management Reserves as part of the budget to add
the required functionality before release.
explanation:
Management reserves are a specified amount of the project budget
withheld for management control purposes and are reserved for unforeseen
work that is within scope of the project. Process Groups: A Practice
Guide (2022), p291, "Reserve Analysis".

statement:
Your team have completed the work on your project and the customer has
confirmed the formal acceptance of the product that has been delivered.
You set a meeting with your team to document lessons learned, release
and reassign personnel as needed. What will you do next?
option1: Gain final sign off on the risk report with the steering committee.
option2: Archive all project information for future use by the organization.
option3: Review project documents for any open issues or risks.
option4: Ask the customer for a formal contract closure letter.
explanation:
Of all the options available, only "Archive all project information for
future use by the organization" is part of the project closure
activities. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p198, "Close
Project or Phase"

statement:
You are working as a project manager for a medium-sized software
project. The project sponsor believes that the team have not completed
enough work in the last two weeks, and comes to talk to you about it.
What will you do next?
option1:
Review the team Burndown Chart to see the flow of work versus what was
committed.
option2:
Review the project risk register, to raise a risk on project
performance.
option3: Ask the team to work harder, putting in overtime where possible.
option4:
Tell the project sponsor to focus on their area and let you manage the
project.
explanation:
It is best to solve problems with real data -- and ensure they are real
problems to begin with. The iteration burndown chart tracks the work
that remains to be completed in the iteration backlog. It shows the
variance against the work committed from iteration planning. Process
Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p277, "Iteration Burndown Chart".

statement:
You are working on a project that involves the renovation and removal of
an old industrial plant. When investigating the area with your team to
capture the project scope, you notice here are old chemicals left
behind, which hurt your lungs when you breath them in. What will you do
next?
option1: Accept the risk and remove the industrial plant anyway.
option2: Mitigate the risk by asking for volunteers to deal with the chemicals.
option3:
Transfer the risk by hiring a specialist external company to remove the
chemicals.
option4: Avoid the risk by only working on areas which don't have chemicals.
explanation:
This question asks that we have knowledge of risk responses. Of all the
options where genuine danger or high risk is involved, Transferring the
risk to a third party or through insurance is the best approach.
Mitigating the risk (through proper controls) would also be a good
approach, but this option was not presented here. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p304, "Strategies for Threats".

statement:
During User Acceptance Testing a senior user points out a major change
needed to the project scope for it to be successful. You realise you may
not have enough money or resources to complete your project, but will
need approval to increase either. What will you do next?
option1:
Review the Change Management Plan for the correct change control
process, including approvers on the Change Control Board.
option2:
Review the Configuration Management Plan to find out who can configure
the scope change.
option3:
Review the Scope Management Plan to see how changes to scope should be
handled.
option4:
Review the Quality Management Plan to see rules for User Acceptance
Testing and their results.
explanation:
The Change Management plan provides the process for managing change
control, including the CCB. The Configuration Management Plan shows
which documents will be baselined. The Scope Management Plan shows how
scope will be defined, validated, and controlled. The Quality Management
Plan is the testing plan, showing the test roles and process. Process
Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p32, p205, \"Change management plan\".

statement:
A new executive has started in the functional area you are delivering
to. They are concerned the project is not delivering what they need, and
they put pressure on you to deliver more than was originally agreed. You
agree to meet and talk through the Scope Baseline so you are on the same
page. What will you do next?
option1: Gather the Requirements Traceability Matrix and Feature Gantt Chart.
option2: Gather the Feature list and current Sprint backlog.
option3: Gather the approved Scope Management Plan.
option4: Gather the Project Scope Statement, WBS and WBS dictionary.
explanation:
The project scope statement, WBS and WBS dictionary are all part of the
Scope Baseline. Work packages and planning packages are also included.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p236, "Scope Baseline".

statement:
You are developing new scheduling software for your organisation, and
the project is at risk of not meeting its scheduled release date. The
Functional manager tells you they are now removing three key resources
from the project who are currently assigned to critical path tasks. What
will you do next?
option1: Submit a change request to change the project schedule.
option2: Escalate the situation to the project sponsor.
option3: Overturn the decision of the Functional manager.
option4: Negotiate with the Functional manager and reach an agreement.
explanation:
Submitting a change request is only needed if the baselined schedule,
cost or scope are changing. The project sponsor may not have control
over the functional area, and escalation can come later. The Project
Manager doesn't have the authority to overturn area resource decisions
by the functional manager. The next best step is to use negotiation, and
trying to reach an agreement first with the Functional Manager before
escalating or submitting a change request.

statement:
You are leading a waterfall project, where there has just been a change
to government regulation. It impacts the scope of your project and
requires a change. You perform impact analysis and find that the change
will use up the project's cost management reserves completely. What will
you do next?
option1:
Raise a risk in the Risk Register, and assign an owner and risk
mitigation.
option2: Document a Change Request and take it to the Change Control Board.
option3:
Discuss the situation with sponsor and ask her to make the final
decision.
option4:
Make the change to project scope as it is within the managed budget
reserves.
explanation:
In this situation we are talking about the change request process. We
know there is a change to project scope, that will require a change
request. We have also performed impact analysis so we can include that
in the change request and take it to the CCB. Risks are noted before
they occur -- after they occur they become issues. The Project Sponsor
may be a part of the Change Control Board, but if a CCB exists it would
be inappropriate to go to them for a decision without a Change Request.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p168, \"Perform Integrated
Change Control\".

statement:
You are working on a COTS (commercial-off-the-shelf) system project for
your organisation with a third party. The system provider has been
getting further and further behind in their part of delivery, and it is
starting to impact your project schedule. What will you do next?
option1:
Use your project scope contingency reserves to speed delivery of the
item.
option2: Raise an issue in the issue log and note the status.
option3: Raise a claim to the third party for the COTS delay.
option4:
Review the Procurement agreement for the original timeframe and
escalation process.
explanation:
The process is Controlling Procurements -- keeping any third party
agreement and contract under control. Agreements is one of the inputs to
this process. Claims are contested changes (including defects or missed
requirements) to the item, and the claims process may also be in the
procurement contract, including an alternative dispute resolution
process if the two parties cannot agree. Process Groups: A Practice
Guide (2022), p188, "Control Procurements", p252 "Claims Administration"

statement:
Your project team have completed development and quality testing. You
have had the product validated and signed off with the receiving
Functional Manager. The functional manager asks you to perform variance
analysis on the outcome once the product is released. What will you do
next?
option1:
Review the different products and their potential impact on the
business.
option2:
Review the cost of the product versus the benefit it will bring to the
business.
option3:
Review the problem the product is trying to solve, and the reasons
behind it.
option4:
Review the forecast business benefit with the actual benefit post
release.
explanation:
Answer A describes alternatives analysis, B describes Cost-Benefit
analysis, C describes Root Cause analysis. Answer D describes comparing
the "planned" with the "actual" performance of the product, or the
variance between the two. Among all options provided, D is the correct
answer. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p312, "Variance
Analysis"

statement:
You are working as a project manager on a new kitchen product for your
organisation. The product has been through a thorough design process and
has now been developed. You have had a quality testing team check over
the product and they are happy. What will you do next?
option1: Accept the deliverable and begin archiving project information.
option2: Test the new product with a small amount of customers to reduce risk.
option3: Ask the project sponsor to begin production on the new product.
option4: Show the project sponsor the verified deliverable.
explanation:
The process of deliverable acceptance is this: Control Quality (testing)
\> Verified Deliverables \> Validate Scope process (with
customer/sponsor) \> Accepted Deliverables. Verified deliverables are
project deliverables that are completed and checked for correctness
through the Control Quality process, and they are an input into the
"Validate Scope" process. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p345,
"Verified Deliverables".

statement:
You have the project scope defined on your project, and have created the
list of activities needed to deliver that scope. The Project Sponsor is
worried that there will not be enough resources to complete the work.
What will you do next?
option1:
Sequence the activities, estimate their duration, then create the
schedule for the Project Sponsor.
option2: Ask the Project Sponsor to hire more resources so you are prepared.
option3: Create a schedule network diagram and find the critical path.
option4: Create a Resource Assignment Matrix to see who is assigned to the work.
explanation:
This scenario is describing Project Schedule Management. You have
defined the scope and the list of activities to deliver the scope. The
next steps are to sequence (or prioritise) the activities with their
dependencies, estimate their duration and then place them on a schedule
assigned to resources. Doing this will also show stakeholders the time
and resources required, so they will know whether they have enough.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p89 "Plan Schedule Management",
p22 \"Process Groups\"

statement:
Your project team have been working to create a part of a software
deliverable for two months, and an issue has just been raised where they
believe it will cost three times the current budget and time available.
They have identified a few third party vendors who specialise in this
item. What will you do next?
option1:
Ask the project team to keep going -- time and money has already been
spent for them to create it.
option2:
Create a procurement strategy and ask the project team for a Statement
of Work.
option3:
Ask the procurement team to create a fixed price contract so cost
doesn't over-run any further.
option4:
Select a seller and award a contract so work can begin as quickly as
possible.
explanation:
Answer A is incorrect -- this indicates the Sunk Cost Fallacy -- remove
money already spent from the decision. Before accepting or hiring a
Third Party to do work, you will need the outputs from "Plan Procurement
Management" - a Procurement Strategy (including the delivery method and
type of contract) and Statement of Work (including item description and
requirements). Then you can send a Request for Quote or Proposal to
vendors using this information. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022),
p215, "Procurement Documentation".

statement:
You are working as a Project Manager on a waterfall project, and the
functional manager approaches you to say that the project is not going
well and they don't think the product is going to meet the requirements
they agreed on. What will you do next?
option1:
Provide a product review for the manager at the end of the next
iteration.
option2: Review the Requirements Traceability Matrix with the manager.
option3: Add more scope to your project to satisfy the functional manager.
option4: Review the Scope Management Plan with the functional manager.
explanation:
The requirements traceability matrix is a grid that links product
requirements from their origin to the deliverables that satisfy them.
This is the best option to see whether deliverables meet the
requirements. A product review is an Agile method, but also may only
provide a small snapshot of the product (completed in an iteration).
Adding more scope could be "Gold Plating" the product. The Scope
Management Plan will tell you your process of managing scope, but not
the scope itself. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p226,
"Requirements Traceability Matrix".

statement:
You are working on a highly complex and high profile project and are
under pressure from the customer to deliver something quickly. However
the subject-matter-experts who are providing the product requirements
are still in the process of figuring out around 70% of them due to the
complexity of the product. What should you do?
option1: Ask the project team to use Rolling Wave Planning.
option2: Ask the team to consult the Statement of Work.
option3: Ask the project team to create a working prototype.
option4: Ask the project team to do a Work Breakdown Structure.
explanation:
Rolling wave planning is an iterative technique where work in the near
term is planned in detail, while work further in the future is planned
at a higher level. This would allow something to be delivered while
other work is being planned. A statement of work is used when procuring
a vendor. A prototype might help see the working product, but only if
most of the requirements are already known. A WBS needs the high level
features to break down into smaller workable pieces. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p295, "Rolling Wave Planning"

statement:
As a Project Manager, you are developing the approved version of the
time-phased project budget. You have created the cost estimates, and
added a contingency reserve to combat known risks. What will you do
next?
option1: Add the Basis of Estimates to ensure reasons for costs are known.
option2: Update the configuration management plan to include your cost estimate.
option3:
Add the management reserve to create the total Project Funding
Requirements.
option4: Review the Cost Performance Index to ensure the budget remains on track.
explanation:
The cost baseline is the approved version of the time-phased project
budget, and it includes contingency reserves (for known risks) but
excludes any management reserves (for any unforeseen scope). The next
best thing to do is to add the management reserve to complete your
budget, to create the total Project Funding Requirements, which are
derived from the Cost Baseline and is the other output of the Determine
Budget process. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p326, "Cost
Baseline"

statement:
You are nearing the end of a difficult project where the vendor
providing the system to your company has taken longer than anticipated.
The deliverables have been quality tested, validated and verified. Your
project sponsor is keen to finish the project and close it off. What
will you do next?
option1:
Review the risk register and issues register for outstanding risks and
issues.
option2: Review the project management plan and approved change requests.
option3: Review the procurement agreement and accepted deliverables.
option4:
Review the requirements management plan and requirements traceability
matrix.
explanation:
In this scenario where a procurement is involved with the closing phase
of a project, the procurement agreements and the accepted deliverables
are inputs to close the project. When project deliverables have been
verified, they are accepted by the project sponsor and you can close the
project or phase. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p196, "Close
project or phase".

statement:
You are managing your team through difficult project, which has been
made more difficult by many team members being new and inexperienced,
and not having the right capability to get the work done. You are
getting pressure from your steering committee to improve the performance
of your project. What will you do next?
option1:
Use Tuckman's ladder of Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing with your
team.
option2:
Use co-location, a shared knowledge portal, training and team
assessments.
option3:
Use conflict management, influencing and emotional intelligence to get
your team onside.
option4:
Use brainstorming and root cause analysis to get to the heart of the
problem.
explanation:
This example is referencing the process of "Develop Team" in resource
management. Tools and techniques in this process include co-location,
interpersonal skills, communication technology, training, team
assessments, recognition and rewards, and more. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p146 "Develop Team -- Tools & Techniques".

statement:
You have planned the scope, schedule, cost and resources with your team.
You have also identified a large number of risks within your project.
You are in the process of assigning the probability and impact to the
risks when one of the risks occurs. Your project sponsor immediately
worries about a worst case scenario. What will you do next?
option1: Utilise the management reserve for your project to mitigate the risk.
option2:
Review (or create) the risk response in the risk register then use the
appropriate contingency reserve to implement it.
option3:
Perform a long, in-depth quantitative analysis on the risk to get the
full range of financial impacts of the risk.
option4: Accept the risk for now, as the risk analysis is not yet complete.
explanation:
This scenario describes performing *qualitative* risk analysis, where
the risk has already occurred. Once done, risks are placed in the risk
register with their responses, and an appropriate **contingency
reserve** is added to the work package for these "known-unknown" risks.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p229, "Risk Register".

statement:
A significant change has been made to the project scope after finding a
serious system flaw. This scope change will impact the scope, cost and
schedule of the project. You analyse the impacts to each, raise an issue
in the issue log and are about to raise a change request. What will you
do next?
option1:
Review the configuration management plan for the baselined project
items.
option2: Add the detail as a project delivery risk in your project risk register.
option3: Update the change log and implement the changes required.
option4:
Check the Change Management plan for the Change Control Board members
and process.
explanation:
The Perform Integrated Change Control process includes a change control
board (CCB), which is a formally chartered group responsible for
reviewing, evaluating, approving, deferring, or rejecting changes to the
project and for recording and communicating such decisions. The change
management plan provides the direction for managing the change control
process and documents the roles and responsibilities of the change
control board (CCB). Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p324
"Change Management Plan".

statement:
Five major defects have been found during quality testing, resulting in
the scope changing significantly and affecting your schedule and budget.
You have analysed the impacts and raised change requests for each. The
Change Control Board has approved two changes, rejected two changes and
deferred one. What will you do next?
option1:
Record the outcomes in the change log and communicate the deferred and
rejected changes to the change requestor.
option2:
Review and re-prioritise the project scope to fit must-have items into
delivery.
option3:
Raise a risk in the risk register for threats to the benefit of your
project.
option4:
Raise a claim on the affected changes to give them a better chance of
approval.
explanation:
The outcome of all Change Requests are recorded in the Change log, and
deferred or rejected change requests are communicated to the person or
group requesting the change. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022),
p202, \"Approved Change Requests\".

statement:
You are working through the scope of a newly kicked-off project, and the
senior user is unsure on how the requirements and scope come together to
create a deliverable that will work for him and the functional manager.
Your program manager suggests you use "decomposition" in this
circumstance. What will you do next?
option1: Estimate activity resources for who is required to deliver the item.
option2: Define the project scope for the functional manager.
option3: Create a WBS and Define Activities in your schedule.
option4:
Create the Project Charter and Project Management Plan for a clear view
of the project.
explanation:
Decomposition is "breaking things down" into smaller and more manageable
parts. It is a tool and technique for both the Create WBS and the Define
Activities processes. So, among all option C is the right answer.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p266, \"Decomposition\".

statement:
You have been assigned to take over a project in your organisation that
has gone off track, with scope creep causing the schedule and cost to
increase. You can't quite understand the current cadence and way of
working within the team, so you decide to find out their development
approach, including any modifications. What will you do next?
option1: Review the Project Charter, as this was the original agreed document.
option2:
Review the schedule management plan, as this will have team cadence
schedules.
option3:
Review the Project Management Plan, as this will house the cadence
approach.
option4: Review the Stakeholder Register, for stakeholder cadence needs.
explanation:
The Development approach, including any modifications, is included in
the Project Management Plan. This defines whether predictive, Iterative,
agile, or a hybrid development approach will be used on the project.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p32, \"Development Approach\".

statement:
You are working on a complex engineering project, where a significant
amount of risk has been found with your team and the customer. The
functional manager approaches to ask if she can work with your team to
plan any risk responses. You agree to help her update the Risk Register,
with:
option1:
Risk triggers, agreed response strategies, and residual risks after
mitigation.
option2:
Risk contingency reserves, quantitative analysis and financial impact
responses.
option3:
The list of risks, their probability and impact, and their overall risk
rating.
option4: Risk owners, risk assumptions, and risk mitigations.
explanation:
This situation is describing the "Plan Risk Responses" process. The risk
register is updated with information like agreed-upon response
strategies, triggers, warning signs of risk occurrence, residual risks
that are expected to remain because they are deliberately accepted, and
the register is an output of this process. Process Groups: A Practice
Guide (2022), p122, \"Plan Risk Responses\".

statement:
Your project is nearing completion. You have monitored and controlled
quality through testing, and risks with mitigations throughout the
project life. The project sponsor is concerned that she won't know if
the product being delivered has the desired effect on her business area
once it is released. What will you do next?
option1:
Consult the Quality Management Plan, to ensure the quality is right for
the sponsor.
option2:
Consult the Benefits Management Plan, and act in accordance to that
process.
option3: Consult the Business Case, to see the proposed benefits from the change.
option4:
Consult the Risk Register, for any remaining risks that might reduce the
effect.
explanation:
What the sponsor is talking about here is the benefit to her business.
The Benefits Management Plan is a type of business document that
describes how and when the benefits of the project will be delivered,
and process that should be in place to measure those benefits. During
closing, its reviewed to measure whether the benefits of the project
were achieved as planned. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p203,
"Benefits Management Plan".

statement:
You are the project manager on a high profile software project that
involves automation technology not used before in your company. Because
of this, your team have no resources to complete that part of the
project. The steering committee approves outsourcing this work to a
suitable vendor. What will you do next?
option1: Perform a make-or-buy analysis to ensure a correct decision.
option2:
Approach your procurement department for suitable contracts to proceed
with, such as Cost plus Incentive Fee.
option3: Create the procurement statement of work and source selection criteria.
option4:
Review current agreements and change requests impacting third-party
work.
explanation:
In this scenario we are in the beginning stages of procuring a vendor.
The right process is Plan Procurement Management, where the outputs are
to create the procurement statement of work and the source selection
criteria. Other items are the bid documents (i.e. request for proposal),
and the Procurement Strategy. The time for make-or-buy analysis has
passed, with the third party approach already being approved. Process
Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p128, \"Plan Procurement Management\"

statement:
You are a project manager working in the initial phases of a project,
and trying to put together a document for your stakeholders to have your
project approved. You remember the Five Cs of communication from your
studies, and decide to use them here. What will you do next?
option1: Connect each part of the communication.
option2: Create a bond between the message sender and receiver.
option3: Ensure clear purpose and expression directed to the needs of the reader.
option4: Care about the subject and the person.
explanation:
In this scenario we are talking about the five Cs of communication:
Correct grammar and spelling, Concise expression and elimination of
excess words, Clear purpose and expression directed to the needs of the
reader, Coherent logical flow of ideas, Controlling flow of words and
ideas. (PMBOK Guide) -- Sixth Edition, 2017, p363, "Key concepts for
project communications".

statement:
You have been working with the stakeholders on your project for some
time, and the communication has been getting more and more tense with
each interaction. You perform a stakeholder engagement assessment and
discover the majority of your stakeholders are either resistant or
neutral. What will you NOT do next?
option1:
Ensure you are listening actively with each interaction, speaking less
and listening more.
option2: Increase your awareness of cultural and personal differences.
option3: Identify, set and manage stakeholder expectations.
option4:
Escalate the stakeholder issue to their functional manager to bring them
back in line.
explanation:
In this scenario we are talking about Communication. There are four
things that support the Five Cs of communication, and they are:
Listening actively; Awareness of cultural and personal differences;
Identifying, setting, and managing stakeholder expectations; Enhancement
of skills (in negotiation coaching, and conflict resolution). (PMBOK
Guide) -- Sixth Edition, 2017, p363

statement:
You have been working on a large database transfer project, when the
manager for data in your organisation identifies the aging systems as a
threat to your project. You review this with your project team and
decide to perform qualitative risk analysis on the threat. What will you
do next?
option1: Review the quality of the data that led to the risk identification.
option2: Assign a probability of occurrence and an impact if it does occur.
option3:
Perform analysis on a range of outcomes to see the possible financial
impact.
option4: Add a risk trigger, mitigation and owner to the threat.
explanation:
This scenario is talking about qualitative risk analysis, which is
described as the process of prioritizing individual project risks for
further analysis or action by assessing their probability of occurrence
and impact as well as other characteristics. Process Groups: A Practice
Guide (2022), p117 "Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis".

statement:
You are managing a project that has formed organically around an
important process improvement. You have been given limited resources and
a very small budget from the project sponsor. You are planning Risk
Management with your team, but you do not have the resources to complete
all the necessary risk tasks. Which step will you remove in such a
project?
option1: Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis.
option2: Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis.
option3: Identify Risks.
option4: Plan Risk Management.
explanation:
In the PMBOK Guide, quantitative risk analysis consumes additional time
and cost. The use of quantitative risk analysis is most likely
appropriate for large or complex projects, or strategically important
projects. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p120, \"Perform
Quantitative Risk Analysis\".

statement:
You are the project manager on a waterfall project, and you have planned
the requirements, scope and schedule with your team. You now need to
create the total funding requirements for your project. What will you
NOT include as part of your total funding requirements?
option1: Contingency amounts to account for identified risks.
option2: Amounts for ongoing operation of the delivered product.
option3: Aggregated estimates for project activities.
option4: A management reserve for unforeseen scope changes.
explanation:
Total Funding Requirements include the cost baseline - which are the
aggregated project costs, plus the contingency reserve for any known
risks, and the management reserve for any unforeseen project scope.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p219, "Project Funding
Requirements".

statement:
You have just taken over as project manager on a difficult waterfall
project. Multiple project stakeholders have approached you and asked to
make changes to different parts of the scope, cost and the delivery
(schedule) of the project. You are trying to figure out which items need
change approval, and which can just be done. What will you do next?
option1: Review the Change Management Plan for the change approval process.
option2: Refer the Scope Management Plan for scope items that need approval.
option3: Review the Configuration Management Plan for baselined items.
option4: Examine Requirement Traceability Matrix for the requirement requestor.
explanation:
The change management plan explains the change process, however it is
the configuration management plan that shows which items in your project
are baselined and need to go through the change management process.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p207, "Configuration Management
Plan"

statement:
You are new to a project and are approached by the functional manager.
No one has been able to tell them exactly what the project is about, and
what they need to be aware of, including high level scope, schedule,
risks, and who approved it in the first place. What will you do next?
option1: Check the project activity list and schedule.
option2: Hold a sprint review to see what the team have developed so far.
option3:
Review the project charter and identify the stakeholder impacted by the
change.
option4:
Visit the project management office (PMO) to gather organizational
process assets.
explanation:
The Project Charter holds the high level information you need to get up
to speed, including the project purpose, high-level requirements and
deliverables, summary milestone schedule list, project risk,
stakeholders and more. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p222,
"Project Charter".

statement:
The project manager on your project recently left, and you agreed to see
the project through as it is nearly complete. The deliverables have been
accepted, and you just need to communicate the release to any and all
affected stakeholders in the way they agreed. What will you do next?
option1:
Review the Communication Management Plan for how communication is
managed.
option2:
Review the Stakeholder Engagement Plan for how stakeholders prefer to be
engaged.
option3:
Review the Stakeholder Register for the people you need to communicate
to.
option4:
Review the Resource Management Plan for the resources affected by your
project.
explanation:
The communications management plan describes how project communications
will be planned, structured, implemented, and monitored for
effectiveness. The plan contains things such as Stakeholder
communication requirements, Information to be communicated, Reason for
the distribution of that information and more. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p206, "Communications Management Plan"

statement:
You have been working on a project in your organisation for some time.
In the latest working group meeting, the project sponsor adds a new
executive member to the group. As soon as they arrive, they raise a new
scope item that was not in the original plan. What will you do next?
option1: Raise a change request to change the scope baseline.
option2: Update the stakeholder register with the new stakeholder details.
option3: Raise an issue for the scope change in the issue log.
option4: Update the change log with the initial details of the scope change.
explanation:
The stakeholder register contains identification information (i.e. name
and role), assessment information (i.e. requirements and potential for
influencing), and stakeholder classification such as impact over
influence. As the stakeholder has just joined the project, this is the
very next thing you should do. The change control process can come next,
but usually not before analysing the impact to your project. Process
Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p239, \"Stakeholder Register\".

statement:
Your project team have been complaining about poor stakeholder
engagement in a certain part of the organisation for some time. It is
now impacting the project schedule and you may need to use your
contingency reserve to crash the schedule and bring it back on track.
What will you do next?
option1:
Review the cost management plan and access the appropriate contingency
reserve.
option2: Perform root cause analysis on the problem with your team.
option3:
Raise the engagement as a risk and escalate it immediately to the
functional manager.
option4: Change the way of working to agile to increase stakeholder engagement.
explanation:
Root cause analysis is an analytical technique used for identifying root
causes of a problem and solving them. We won't access a contingency
reserve until we've properly analysed the impact to our project. Risks
must also go through the process of analysis, assignment and mitigation
before deciding to escalate as a response. Changing the way of work
won't magically increase engagement straight away. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p295, "Root Cause Analysis".

statement:
You are delivering a brand new product line into your organisation,
partly utilising a third party system. You have been working with the
functional manager and her team to gather their expert judgement
throughout development, when they raise a concern that the third party
system has a high chance of being discontinued and losing support in the
next 12 months. What will you do next?
option1: Update the Risk Register.
option2: Update the Issue Log.
option3:
Ignore the concern, you can still deliver the product without the
system.
option4: Escalate the concern to the project sponsor.
explanation:
What we are describing here is a Risk, to the delivery of the product
and its benefits. A risk can get discovered at any point of time in the
project, and all identified risks can be recorded to Risk Register
throughout the life cycle of the project. One of the outputs to
Identifying Risks is the updated Risk Register. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p115, "Identify Risks"

statement:
You have joined a car manufacturing company as a new project manager.
Senior Management have asked you to take the necessary steps that will
enable you to kick-start a project in a corresponding area for designing
a new electronic dashboard. What will you do next?
option1: Review the marketplace conditions and current industry standards.
option2:
Create a cost benefit analysis and feasibility study for a business
case.
option3:
Gather the high level requirements, scope, milestones and risks for a
project charter.
option4:
Start the project through a kick-off meeting immediately, and begin
gathering resources.
explanation:
This question is describing the Initiating phase of a project. Develop
Project Charter is the first step in the Initiating phase. It is a
document that formally authorizes the existence of a project and
provides the project manager with the authority to apply organizational
resources to project activities, and includes a summary milestone
schedule; high-level project description and key deliverables, overall
project risk and more. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p22,
\"Process Groups\", 222, "Project Charter".

statement:
Your project team have just finished defining the scope of the project.
They have matched the scope to the original requirements on a
Requirements Traceability Matrix. The team cannot agree on what to do
next. What will you ask them to do?
option1: Ask your quality team to verify the scope so your project can proceed.
option2: Approach the project sponsor for acceptance of the project scope.
option3:
Break down the scope into activities and place them on a project
schedule.
option4:
Review the Scope Management Plan for direction on creating the Work
Breakdown Structure.
explanation:
The order of Scope Management processes in project planning are: Collect
Requirements, Define the Scope, Create the WBS. The process for creating
the WBS should be outlined in the Scope Management Plan, which outlines
the process for creating the scope statement, WBS, Scope Baseline, and
how scope is accepted. Validating scope is done by the accepting
customer (or sponsor) after quality testing verifies them. Process
Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p77, \"Planning Process Group\"

statement:
You have just taken over a project that is approximately half-way
through, and has used around 75% of its budget. A change to one of the
features has been requested after extensive user acceptance testing, and
your project team cannot agree on what to do next or whether changes
should be made. What will you do next?
option1: Check the Change Management Plan for details on how to make changes.
option2:
Check the Requirements Traceability Matrix for how the requirements
should be traced.
option3:
Blame the previous project manager loudly for not having a process to
deal with this situation.
option4: Check the Scope Management Plan for details on how to manage the scope.
explanation:
The change management plan defines the process for managing change on
the project. The scope management plan documents how the project and
product scope will be defined, monitored and controlled, verified and
validated. The requirements traceability matrix is a grid that links
product requirements from their origin to the deliverables that satisfy
them. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p82, \"Scope Management
Plan\".

statement:
You have been asked to work on a process improvement within your team.
Your manager has secured a developer to assist you with automating the
process once it is streamlined, but you will need to make sure they
understand the process thoroughly, including the criteria to test the
automation against. What will you do next?
option1: Gather your team and the developer and create a flowchart together.
option2: Gather your team and create an affinity diagram together.
option3:
Gather your team and the developer and perform a cost-benefit analysis
for the automation.
option4: Gather your team and create a quality management plan together.
explanation:
A flowchart shows the process flow from start to finish, including
decision points and people, departments or systems involved. It is also
a Tool for Plan Quality Management. Process Groups: A Practice Guide
(2022), p273, \"Flowcharts\".

statement:
You are working on a new project where the design and project work,
including all the scope and cost have been planned up front. However,
part of the project relies on a vendor creating a new add-on for your
project, which will be subject to multiple changes as the core product
is created. What project development lifecycle approach will you
recommend to the team?
option1: A Predictive or Waterfall approach, as the work is planned up-front.
option2:
An Iterative approach, as changes will need to be made before the final
delivery.
option3:
An Incremental approach, so you can deliver parts continuously and align
them.
option4:
A Hybrid approach combining pre-planned activities, iterations and
ceremonies that promote collaboration and feedback.
explanation:
A hybrid life cycle is a combination of a predictive and an adaptive
life cycle. Those elements of the project that are well known or have
fixed requirements follow a predictive development life cycle, and those
elements that are still evolving follow an adaptive development life
cycle. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p15, \"Project and
Development Lifecycles"

statement:
The project you are managing has defined the scope and created the Scope
Baseline with the Scope Statement, WBS and WBS dictionary. The project
sponsor is concerned that project planning is taking too long, and would
like to see the project plan finished. What is the next best action you
should take?
option1:
Create the project budget, including contingency and management
reserves.
option2: Define and sequence activities and estimate their durations.
option3: Create the Resource Management Plan
option4:
Review the project charter for the high level project scope, schedule
and costs.
explanation:
You cannot create the budget without knowing the activities to create
the scope, their durations, and the resources required to perform them.
The next best action is to define and sequence activities and estimate
their durations. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p89, \"Plan
Schedule Management\"

statement:
You are working on a project charter for the initiating stage of a new
project that will deliver a new data system. You approach your
enterprise data team and ask them for an estimate to deliver these
changes, however your project sponsor believes a third-party vendor will
provide the best solution. What will you do next?
option1: Create the project charter and let the steering committee decide.
option2: Perform make-or-buy analysis with a payback period.
option3: Ask the enterprise data team for their lowest quote to ensure they win.
option4: Take an agile approach to the project and deliver it in increments.
explanation:
Make or buy analysis is used to decide between two or more options in
delivering a project, with one of those options being your own
organisation. It may include a payback period (initial outlay versus
ongoing costs for each), Net Present Value, Cost Benefit analysis and
more. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p213, "Make or buy
decisions".

statement:
You are working on a project where the functional manager is unhappy
with the changes being brought about to his part of the organisation. He
argues constantly, misses key meetings, demands multiple sign-offs to
slow progress and more. The project sponsor asks you to go for a win/win
approach to this conflict. What will you do next?
option1: Smooth and accommodate the manager, emphasising areas of agreement.
option2: Collaborate and problem solve with the manager.
option3: Compromise and reconcile with the manager.
option4:
Force and direct the manager, pushing your viewpoint and the expense of
others.
explanation:
This is a question on managing conflict. Smoothing and accommodating is
lose/win. Compromising and reconciling may sound nice, but you both give
up something and it ends in a lose/lose. Forcing your viewpoint is
win/lose at the expense of others. Only problem solving and
collaborating can end in a win/win. Process Groups: A Practice Guide
(2022), p258, "Conflict Management".

statement:
You are working on a large multi-year project. You realise that the
estimates from a year ago are extremely inaccurate, and the project is
off track. You would like to try a different approach that gives a high
level view of future work, and a detailed view of near-term work. What
will you do next?
option1: Recommend a Decomposition approach.
option2: Recommend the process in the Schedule Management Plan.
option3: Recommend an Iterative approach to development.
option4: Recommend a Rolling Wave Planning approach.
explanation:
Rolling Wave Planning is when you plan near-term work in detail, and
future work at a high level. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022),
p295, "Rolling Wave Planning".

statement:
You are in the initiating stages of a project working for a car
manufacturer. Their vehicles have been losing market share due to the
high price of fuel, and the organisation wants to build more fuel
efficient cars. You have been asked to put together a business case
outlining the various options, with a specific order to perform
benchmarking. What will you do next?
option1:
Find which competitor products have been benchmarked for future
production.
option2:
Compare the specifications of popular branded fuel efficient cars
available in the market with your products.
option3:
Create a project charter to use as a benchmark for your future project
management plan.
option4:
Benchmark your current Scope, Cost and Schedule to use as values for
project performance.
explanation:
Benchmarking involves comparing actual or planned products, processes,
and practices to those of comparable organizations to identify best
practices, generate ideas for improvement, and provide a basis for
measuring performance. The organizations compared during benchmarking
can be internal or external. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022),
p249, "Benchmarking".

statement:
When reviewing the project performance with your team, you notice the
following: 1) Activity A, on the critical path, is delayed by 5 days. 2)
Activity B, not on the critical path, is delayed by 10 days. 3) Activity
C, on the critical path is delayed by 3 days 4) Activity D, not on the
critical path, is delayed by 7 days. Where will you ask the team to
focus their efforts on first, and then in which order, to bring the
project back on track?
option1: Activity A, Activity B, Activity D, Activity C.
option2: Activity B, Activity D, Activity A, Activity C.
option3: Activity A, Activity C, Activity B, Activity D.
option4: Activity B, Activity A, Activity D, Activity C.
explanation:
Tasks on critical path should be addressed first, as they are critical
to the project schedule. After that, items that are delayed by the most
should be addressed as soon as possible. This makes C is the most
reasonable choice. (PMBOK Guide) -- Sixth Edition, 2017, p210, "Critical
Path method".

statement:
As your project begins, it becomes clear the most of the business
analysts, developers and testers are not familiar with system they will
be modifying. The Project Sponsor asks you to address this as a
potential future quality issue, and asks you to add a series of training
sessions for the team, with associated cost. What will you do next?
option1:
Add it as Appraisal Costs, and take the change request to the CCB for
approval.
option2:
Add it as Preventive Costs, and take the change request to the CCB for
approval.
option3:
Add it as Internal Failure Costs and take the change request to the CCB
for approval.
option4:
Add it as Sunk Costs and take the change request to the CCB for
approval.
explanation:
This is the Cost of Quality. Appraisal cost is the cost of auditing or
checking the work. Internal failure costs are defects caught before
release. Sunk cost is the money already spent on something, while making
future decisions. Preventative costs is the bets answer here, as through
training we are preventing future quality issues.

statement:
You have finished project planning. All baseline documents are approved
and the project team is ready to begin project execution or delivery.
Your team have planned and estimated the activity resources required.
What will you do next?
option1:
Define with the team how to estimate, acquire, manage, and use team and
physical resources.
option2:
Estimate team resources and the type and quantities of materials,
equipment, and supplies necessary to perform project work.
option3:
Obtain team members, facilities, equipment, materials, supplies, and
other resources necessary to complete project work.
option4:
Monitor the planned versus actual utilization of resources and taking
corrective action as necessary.
explanation:
After planning and estimating resources comes acquiring resources during
project execution. Acquiring resources is described as: Obtaining team
members, facilities, equipment, materials, supplies, and other resources
necessary to complete project work. Process Groups: A Practice Guide
(2022), p22 "Process Group Mapping", p143 "Acquire Resources".

statement:
You have been working on a large waterfall project for some time, and
one of the business analysts working with you has shown interest in
taking on higher duties and moving into a project management role. What
advice will you give them?
option1: To keep improving as a business analyst and become the best they can be.
option2: To ensure they can create a Project Charter and Project Management Plan.
option3:
To work on their soft skills, knowledge in ways of working and value
prioritisation.
option4: To work on their contract and procurement skills for hiring and vendors.
explanation:
The new Project Manager Talent Triangle includes Power Skills (soft
skills and communication), Ways of Working skills (the ability to choose
and refine a way of working), and Business Acumen (making good
decisions, prioritizing with cost and benefits).

statement:
You are working as a project manager in an organisation with a new
Project Management Office. They advise all project managers to fulfil
their numerous roles within their sphere of influence, including the
Project, the Industry, and their professional discipline. What is your
responsibility when it comes to the Organisation?
option1:
Proactively interact with other project managers for demands on the same
resources, funding, and alignment of project goals to the organisation.
option2: Communicate within your project consistently and predictably.
option3: Ensure an understanding of changing market niches and standards.
option4:
Educate other professionals in the organisation regarding the value of
project management.
explanation:
This is talking about the Project Manager\'s Sphere of Influence. Being
proactive with other project managers for resources and funding is the
influencing within Organisation. Communicating within your project is
the Project sphere of influence. An understanding of market niches is
the Industry sphere of influence. Educating other professionals is
influencing \"Across Disciplines.\" Process Groups: A Practice Guide
(2022), p53, \"The Project Manager\'s Sphere of Influence\"

statement:
You have just joined an organisation, and are about to be allocated to
an existing project, delivering a new Human Resources system. You have
heard that the project is behind its delivery milestones, and it is over
budget by \$40,000 so far. There are multiple stakeholders with
differing needs. What will you do first?
option1:
Create a project management plan, outlining the processes for each phase
of the project.
option2:
Perform a stakeholder engagement assessment, to see who is sabotaging
the project.
option3:
Start by understanding how the organisation works, including its
politics and power.
option4:
Meet with the project sponsor to understand why the project is off
track.
explanation:
Being new to the organisation and to the project, the best thing you can
do first is to understand the internal Enterprise Environmental Factors
of politics, power, and how to get things done. You are not yet on the
project, so Stakeholders are not your first concern. An existing project
will have a Project plan that you can review. Process Groups: A Practice
Guide (2022), p63, \"Politics, Power and Getting things done\"

statement:
You are working in an organisation where a large change is happening to
multiple departments. You have been asked to manage this change as a
project, and allocate people within your project team with sufficient
leadership skills. What will you NOT look for in potential candidates?
option1:
People who build trust, balance opposing goals and apply persuasion and
conflict resolution.
option2:
People who give feedback constructively and accept feedback
constructively.
option3:
People who can describe the products, goals and objectives of the
project.
option4:
People who ensure the work is done their way, to ensure the fastest
approach.
explanation:
The qualities and skills of a leader include: integrity, being a
visionary, being collaborative and using influence appropriately. They
do not include dictatorships or doing things their own way without
feedback from the team. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p60,
\"Qualities and Skills of a Leader\"

statement:
You have started as a junior project manager on an internal process
improvement project within your existing team. Your program manager asks
that you be aware of and use the various forms of power available to
you. What is the best type of power available to you in this situation?
option1: Informational power.
option2: Situational power.
option3: Positional power.
option4: Punitive or coercive power.
explanation:
