These are the different types of power. As a junior project manager you
have limited ability to discipline others, or provide rewards. As the
change is in your existing team, the best action here is informational
power, as you will have existing expert judgement from within your team.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p63, \"Politics, Power and
Getting things done\"

statement:
You are working on a new waterfall project where the scope and cost have
been planned up front. As the project progresses, you notice several
tasks going off-track and falling behind. Luckily these tasks are not on
the critical path, have several days of float available, and you can
adjust their dates without affecting the overall project. What technique
are you using to keep your project on track?
option1: Resource smoothing.
option2: Resource levelling.
option3: Schedule fast tracking.
option4: Schedule crashing.
explanation:
Resource smoothing is when we use available float to adjust a task,
without affecting the overall schedule. Resource Levelling is moving
tasks further out, in sequence instead of parallel. Schedule Fast
tracking is performing tasks at the same time in order to reduce the
overall time. Schedule crashing is adding cost and resources to try and
reduce the schedule time. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p291,
"Resource Optimisation\"

statement:
About a third of your project team have worked on a similar project in
the past. They have gathered requirements and created a high level
scope. It is time to estimate the size and effort involved, however the
customer believes things are taking too long and would like to see some
progress as soon as possible. What will you do?
option1: Ask the customer to wait so you can plan the project properly.
option2: Estimate the scope using parametric estimating.
option3: Break the high level scope into smaller parts using a WBS.
option4: Estimate the scope using analogous estimating.
explanation:
Analogous estimating uses information regarding resources from a
previous similar project as the basis for estimating a future project.
It is used as quick estimating method and can be used when the project
manager can only identify a few top levels of the WBS. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p246, "Analogous Estimating".

statement:
You have been asked to monitor the changes to a product before
completing your next project phase. You review the control chart. The
data is within the upper and lower limits. You have an upward trend of
four data points, and six points above the average line. What will you
do next?
option1: Proceed with the next phase, as the process is within control.
option2:
Delay the next phase and rework the current release, as the process is
out of control.
option3: Ask the project sponsor to validate the released deliverable.
option4: Proceed with quality testing to verify the created deliverables.
explanation:
The rules for control charts indicate a process is out of control if:
Seven or more points above the centre line, five or more points in one
direction (trend), any point outside the control limits. This process is
within those guidelines. The Certified Six Sigma Green Belt Handbook,
p404, "Control Chart Interpretation"

statement:
You are in the process of planning the resources required for your
project. Work on your project has not started, as your team are not sure
what they should be doing. You decide to create the resource management
plan with your team. What output do you specifically need from this
plan?
option1:
Stakeholder Register, to identify and classify people involved in the
project.
option2:
Project Charter, with the high level roles & responsibilities for the
project.
option3:
Requirement traceability matrix, with who requested and signs off on
each requirement.
option4:
Roles & responsibilities, with Role, Responsibilities, Authority and
Competence.
explanation:
The specific output as part of Planning Resource Management is Roles &
Responsibilities, with their role details, Authority and Competence.
Stakeholder register captures people involved with the project but not
necessarily project team members and their roles. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p228, "Resource Management Plan".

statement:
A quality assurance manager has been hired externally to test the
project and manage defects. She executed the process in the quality
management plan with her testing team, where they found a high number of
low impact defects. You need to see where the biggest impacts are. What
will you do next?
option1: Use a histogram to show the most common defects.
option2: Use a flowchart to show the most common defects.
option3: Use a matrix diagram to show the most common defects.
option4: Use an affinity diagram to show the most common defects.
explanation:
This scenario asks for data representation of the most common item,
meaning a histogram will be best. This is like a bar chart counting the
number of each item, in this case, defects. Process Groups: A Practice
Guide (2022), p274, "Histograms".

statement:
The project you are working on has recently started and the team has
come together from many different parts of the organisation, each with
their own agenda. There are lots of small conflicts within the team and
little work getting done. The program manager asks you to use your
positional power to resolve these conflicts as the team starts to find
its rhythm. What will you do?
option1: Withdraw from the conflicts so the team can work through them.
option2: Force a decision for the conflicts.
option3: Smooth and accommodate all sides in the conflicts.
option4: Problem Solve the conflicts with the team.
explanation:
Using positional power is forcing your viewpoint at the expense of
others, and mostly offers win-lose solutions. This approach mostly works
to resolve conflicts in a quick frame of time. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p258, "Conflict Management"

statement:
During your project working with a third party vendor, your company was
taken over by another, larger company, and the project management office
and its policies were changed. The project customer has already formally
accepted the deliverables, the project has completed delivery and is
ready to be closed. What will you do next?
option1: Refer to the Project Charter for the project exit criteria.
option2: Refer to the scope management plan for deliverables' acceptance.
option3: Review and close outstanding agreements and procurement documentation.
option4:
The project cannot be closed as the project outcomes need to be measured
and accepted.
explanation:
Inputs into "Close Project or Phase" include the accepted deliverables
(already accepted), agreements and procurement documentation. Project
exit criteria is more about when to continue or stop a project (i.e.
meeting benefits or stopping due to cost). Process Groups: A Practice
Guide (2022), p196, \"Close Project or Phase\".

statement:
One of your stakeholders with a very high influence within the company
has raised an urgent change that they want to be implemented within the
next two weeks. You check the change management plan and see that
changes can be approved by the project manager. You raise a change
request and review the impact to the project schedule and cost. What
will you do next?
option1:
Review the configuration management plan to see which documents are
baselined.
option2: Review the schedule earned value reports for your project.
option3: Decide on the changes, approve the changes, and track the changes.
option4:
Review the Requirement Traceability matrix for requirement acceptance
and sign off.
explanation:
Earned value reports on cost or schedule variance are an input to the
change control process. You have already asked for these. As the project
manager can approve change requests on this project, the steps are to
decide, approve and track the changes once the impact has been
determined. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p250, "Change
Control Tools".

statement:
You are in the process of measuring the outcome of one of your
deliverables on the business. The data shows that you are quite far away
from your ideal target value, but the range of results is very close
together. Based on these results, what will you do next?
option1:
The process has high precision but low accuracy. The equipment should be
adjusted.
option2:
The process has high precision but low accuracy. The deliverable process
should be improved.
option3:
The process has high accuracy but low precision. The equipment should be
replaced.
option4:
The process has high accuracy but low precision. Additional features
should be added.
explanation:
This question asks the difference between accuracy and precision:
Accuracy refers to how close a measurement is to the true or accepted
value. Precision refers to how close measurements of the same item are
to each other. In PMBOK examples, if an estimate is not accurate, we
need a large estimate range to capture it. Precision is also described
as how precise the outcome is - two days is more precise than "sometime
this week." (PMBOK Guide) -- Seventh Edition, 2021, p54, "2.4
Estimating"

statement:
In the last few months, your project team have had a large amount of
conflict, finding their role and place within the team. They are
starting to work together and adjust their work habits and behaviours to
support the team. As a leader, what will you do next?
option1:
The team are in the performing stage, work with them to maintain as they
move towards adjourning.
option2:
The team are in the forming stage, work with them to find their roles
and work through the coming conflict.
option3: The team are in the norming stage, work with them to become performing.
option4:
The team are in the storming stage, work with them to normalise their
role in the team.
explanation:
These are the Tuckman stages of team development. A team that has moved
through conflict (storming) and is starting to work together is in the
"Norming" stage of team development. Help them use this and move into
"Performing" so they can truly deliver great outcomes. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p149, "The Tuckman Ladder"

statement:
You have used a project charter and statement of work to create a
project management plan. You present the plan to key stakeholders,
including the project sponsor and you sense a high level of
dissatisfaction by the customer executives. They do not believe the
project will produce the results they had expected. What should you do
next?
option1: Request a written statement from the customer, detailing their concerns.
option2:
Proceed as normal. Your project plan will produce a convincing product
for the customer.
option3:
Arrange a change request, re-plan your project where necessary and go
ahead with the project work.
option4:
Work together with your customer to create a narrative Scope statement
outlining the deliverables.
explanation:
A collaborative and direct approach, combined with the proper outputs of
Defining Scope such as the scope statement including description,
deliverables and acceptance criteria. Process Groups: A Practice Guide
(2022), p221, "Project Scope Statement\"

statement:
You have planned, executed and monitored a project to completion. The
customer has just received the finished product and you would like to
close the project. The customer advises you that they are not happy with
the received product, that your team didn't listen when they raised
quality concerns, and they are raising a claim against your company.
What will you do next?
option1:
Raise a change request for their proposed changes and update your
project plan.
option2:
Call their bluff: take the claim to a dispute, then an appeal if
necessary.
option3:
Review the claims process in the contract and negotiate with them to
settle the claim.
option4:
Review the earned value report for any impact to the schedule and cost
of your project.
explanation:
The claims handling process is outlined in the procurement contract, and
the preferred way to handle claims is to settle the claim through
negotiation. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p252, "Claims
administration"

statement:
You are in the process of creating a project charter for your part of
the organisation, to try and gain resources for an automated process
improvement. The project sponsor is also the functional manager in
charge of the resources for the area. She rejects your project charter,
noting that you have outlined everything you want from her team, but
nothing about what her area will get in return. What will you do next?
option1:
Rework the project charter to include the benefits management plan and
success measures.
option2:
Escalate the charter to the project sponsor's manager, as this project
is important.
option3:
Ask the project sponsor to work Agile, and start delivering a minimum
viable product (MVP).
option4:
Begin work on the Requirements Management Plan, as this will show the
sponsor how their requirements will be added and tracked.
explanation:
The Project Charter will ideally have the project Benefits Management
Plan (including the target benefits, their strategic alignment, and
timeframe for realising them). This may include success measures such as
return on investment (ROI) or Payback Period (PBP). Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p34, "Project Success Measures"

statement:
You are working on a complex, high-value project to replace the
telecommunications system across your organisation. You have created a
Request For Quote for vendors to respond to, including source selection
criteria and a statement of work, and you have selected a seller based
on multiple vendor responses. What will you do next?
option1:
Record the new vendor in your Stakeholder Register, and begin engaging
them to deliver the project.
option2:
Draft an appropriate contract such as Fixed Price Incentive Fee, and
proceed with the implementation.
option3:
Place the approved RFQ in your procurement management plan, and proceed
with delivery of the project.
option4:
Approach organisational senior management for approval to award the
contract.
explanation:
Final approval of complex, high-value, high-risk procurements will
generally require organizational senior management approval prior to
award. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p238, "Selected
Sellers".

statement:
You have been planning your project using a waterfall methodology, and
need to estimate the resources required with your team. Your project
management office recently stopped using third parties or offsite
workers and mandated resources to be onsite only. You review the
activity list based on your project scope and realise that some
essential project resources currently work offsite. What will you do
next?
option1: Discuss available options with the Project Sponsor.
option2:
Perform alternatives analysis on the impact of both onsite and offsite
workers for your project.
option3: Update the resource management plan with the onsite worker mandate.
option4:
Update the resource calendar with the available people, locations and
dates before baselining your project plan.
explanation:
All of these are good options. However, the best option is alternatives
analysis as part of all these things, which assists in providing the
best solution to perform the project activities, within the defined
constraints. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p246,
"Alternatives Analysis".

statement:
You have been working on a project where your organisation wanted only
onsite workers, while you believed the project needed specific skills
that were only available offsite. You worked through the various options
and agreed to use the onsite workers available. As your project
progresses however, it becomes clear that it is not being done to
specification and you really do need the offsite workers. The project is
going off track. What will you do next?
option1:
Update the issue log, and the resource calendar with the resource
requirements.
option2: Negotiate with your Project Sponsor for the onsite workers immediately.
option3: Gather work performance data from your team, and raise a change request.
option4:
Raise a risk in the risk register, and brainstorm risk responses with
your team.
explanation:
This question is referring to the tools, techniques and outputs used
when "Monitoring and controlling resources." These include Work
Performance Data as an input and Change Requests as an output. While we
can negotiate, gathering the evidence first is best. A Resource Calendar
is for the days current resources are available.

statement:
You are working on a very high risk infrastructure project that is
critical to your organisation. The team are experienced in this area
after working on this project for the past 12 months. During a working
group meeting, they mention they are stuck and cannot implement part of
the project. You raise an issue in the issue log and decide to problem
solve with your team. What will you do next?
option1:
Escalate the problem outside your team, as they clearly cannot move
forward.
option2:
Review the lessons learned register for lessons to past similar
problems.
option3:
Define the problem with data, identifying the root cause, and generating
possible solutions.
option4:
Raise a risk to project delivery and note the impact and likelihood of
the risk.
explanation:
This question asks for an approach to solving quality problems. We know
the team are experienced in this area -- going outside the team may
complicate matters with people less familiar. Various problem solving
methods generally include defining the problem with data, finding the
root cause, and generating solutions to choose from and test. Process
Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p287, "Problem Solving\".

statement:
You are creating a high level project charter which has now been
approved. The Project Sponsor expects you to get to work straight away,
but you do not know who to approach or who is available to help you
deliver this project. What will you do next?
option1:
Identify stakeholders with an organisational breakdown chart, and
include this in your project plan.
option2:
Create a RACI or Resource Assignment Matrix so your team know what is
expected of them.
option3:
Review and update the resource calendar with resource working days and
start dates.
option4:
Review the high level stakeholders in the project charter and begin your
project.
explanation:
The next step after creating the project charter is to Identify
Stakeholders, with one way (of many) being an organisational breakdown
chart. There is no point in updating resource calendars or creating a
RACI when you don't know who is involved yet. Process Groups: A Practice
Guide (2022), p274, "Hierarchical charts, OBS", p22, "Process groups."

statement:
You have been assigned to address the fast-moving competition that is
rapidly taking market share from your company. Your executive manager
advises you the company only has around 12 months before jobs will be
lost, but there are currently no projects to address these concerns. As
a project manager, what will you do next?
option1:
Ask the executive manager to come up with project ideas to address the
situation.
option2:
Perform benchmarking against various companies for process improvement
opportunities.
option3:
Create a project charter with high level scope, milestones and cost for
approval.
option4:
Create a project business case, including business need, situation
analysis and solution options.
explanation:
Before a project charter can be created and a project begins, it needs a
Business Case to determine how the project will address the business
goals, including the need, analysis of the situation, and various
options to address them. Benchmarking is also a good answer, but not as
complete as the business case. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022),
p27, "Benefits Management and Business Documents".

statement:
You have just started work in a project that has been in development for
the past six months. You notice it has been through a large amount of
project steps that you are unfamiliar with, such as concept development,
feasibility study, commissioning, milestone review and more. These don't
match the normal phases of design, build and test. What will you do
next?
option1:
Advise the project to reduce and update its phases, for less admin and
faster delivery.
option2:
Advise the project to adhere to the normal project phases of Design,
Build and Test as per the PMBOK Guide.
option3:
Adhere to the project's phases, and familiarise yourself with the
organisation's process.
option4:
Remove phases altogether and work through the waterfall delivery in one
big release.
explanation:
We are referring to Project Phases, but also Enterprise Environmental
Factors. Each organisation can be different, with different politics,
ways of working, governance and approvals. The best immediate course of
action is to adhere to these EEFs and familiarise yourself with the
organisation best practice. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022),
p16, "Project Phase"

statement:
The project management office is looking to you for help in hiring and
coaching new project managers, and they ask you to interview the next
candidate for roles in upcoming projects. In speaking with the
candidate, they do not have any industry experience. What will you do
next?
option1:
Ask them whether they prefer waterfall or agile, to see if they work the
same way as your organisation.
option2:
Ask them whether they would like a different job within the company,
with less focus on industry experience.
option3: Ask them if they can dictate and command a team to get fast results.
option4:
Ask them what their focus on a project would be: the schedule, selected
financial reports, or the issue log.
explanation:
Part of the skills of a qualified project manager are: Ways of working,
Power skills, and Business acumen. The top focuses for leading project
managers were the schedule, financial reports and the issue log. While a
dictatorship can get fast results, it is often at the expense of team
psychological safety and performance over the long term.

statement:
Your project charter has recently been approved, and you are gathering
the customer requirements that will form the basis of your project. The
project customer advises they have been burned by previous project
deliveries in the past, where the project did not deliver what they
really needed. What will you do next?
option1:
Create an initial list of requirements as part of a solid requirements
management plan.
option2:
Call a focus group with customer experts and brainstorm requirements
with them.
option3:
Perform benchmarking between the area process and external
organisations.
option4:
Create a requirements traceability matrix to ensure requirements are
tracked properly.
explanation:
This question focuses on the tools and techniques you will use when
collecting requirements. Brainstorming, Interviews, Focus groups,
questionnaires and benchmarking are all noted. Given the customer has
had difficulties in the past, the best option is direct collaboration:
focus groups and brainstorming. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022),
p83, "Collect Requirements, Tools & Techniques"

statement:
Your deliverables have recently been quality checked by a specialist
testing team, and passed with flying colours. You take them to the
Project Sponsor for approval so you can move to project closure. The
project sponsor accepts all the deliverables except two. One she notes
as a defect, and one as a missed requirement. What will you do next?
option1: Make the changes as soon as possible to avoid further delays.
option2: Raise an issue in the issue log, as this will delay your project.
option3:
Raise a change request for the items, along with the reasons for
non-acceptance of those deliverables.
option4:
Proceed as normal to project closure -- you have delivered them as per
the project needs.
explanation:
For the completed deliverables that have not been formally accepted,
change requests are raised along with the reasons for non-acceptance of
those deliverables. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p169,
"Validate Scope: Outputs, Change Requests"

statement:
You have gathered the customer requirements with your team, and are
finishing the creation of the scope statement. You need to baseline the
project scope to ensure future changes are tracked as necessary. What
will you do next?
option1:
Take the Scope Statement to the project sponsor and steering committee
for approval.
option2:
Ask the project sponsor to approve the Project Charter and project
management plan with the included high level scope.
option3:
Update the configuration management plan with the scope statement as a
baselined document.
option4: Create a Work Breakdown Structure and WBS Dictionary with your team.
explanation:
The Scope Baseline includes the: Scope Statement, WBS, WBS dictionary
and Work packages. As we have already created the Scope Statement, the
next best step is to create these things as inputs into the Scope
Baseline. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p236, "Scope
Baseline".

statement:
You have been planning your project using a waterfall methodology. The
project team have collected the requirements from the customer, who is
eager to begin work as soon as possible. You set a meeting with the
business analysts in your team to create the Scope Statement based on
these requirements. What will you do next?
option1:
Include the Scope description, deliverables, acceptance criteria and
exclusions.
option2:
Include the Scope description, Work Breakdown Structure and WBS
dictionary.
option3:
Include the Scope Management Plan, Statement of Work, and Requirements
Traceability Matrix.
option4:
Include the Project Charter, Project Management Plan, and Accepted
Deliverables.
explanation:
The project scope statement includes: the Scope description,
deliverables, acceptance criteria and exclusions. The scope baseline
include the scope description, WBS and WBS dictionary. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p221, "Project Scope Statement".

statement:
You have been assigned to a project that is behind schedule. Some of
your team approach you and ask what they should be working on. You check
with your team and one of your senior analysts recommends you work
together to create the Work Breakdown Structure Dictionary. What will
you do next?
option1:
Create a scope breakdown with unique identifier, description, and
acceptance criteria.
option2:
Work with the senior analyst to create a Responsibility Assignment
Matrix instead, and coach them on the difference between a RAM and WBS
Dictionary.
option3: Gather the project scope and break it down into smaller work packages.
option4:
Create a scope breakdown with unique identifier, description and
resource requirements.
explanation:
A Responsibility Assignment Matrix is the best approach, showing the
project resources assigned to each work package. It is used to
illustrate the connections between work packages, or activities, and
project team members.

statement:
The project charter has been approved, you have identified the
stakeholders you will be working with and who will be impacted. As the
project progresses, you notice multiple stakeholders who seem to be
actively trying to sabotage your project and efforts. What will you do
next?
option1:
Review the stakeholder engagement assessment matrix and invite feedback
from the people who are resistant.
option2:
Remove the people who seem to be slowing your project from your project
team.
option3:
Escalate the problem stakeholders to their functional managers for
disciplinary action.
option4:
Review the Impact over Influence matrix to determine how to engage with
the stakeholders.
explanation:
Assessing stakeholder engagement and gathering their feedback is the
best answer. Using an Impact over Influence chart to see who holds the
most influence may help you, however this question involves determining
which stakeholders are at risk, and then using communication,
interpersonal and team skills to engage them. Process Groups: A Practice
Guide (2022), p300, "Stakeholder Engagement Assessment Matrix".

statement:
Your project involves nearly a dozen teams who are dispersed around the
country at different sites, with thousands of impacted stakeholders. The
executives in these areas are concerned with the changes the project
will bring, and are starting to push back on your planning efforts. What
will you do next?
option1: Create the project charter, and arrange for sign off by the executives.
option2:
Create the communications management plan to ensure their communication
needs are met.
option3:
Raise a change request for the project, and arrange to co-locate all the
teams.
option4:
Create the requirements traceability matrix, so you can trace the
executives' requirements back to the scope you deliver and keep them
engaged.
explanation:
The project is already in progress so assume the Project Charter has
been approved. The communications management plan contains stakeholder
communication requirements and is the current plan for collecting,
creating, and distributing information in a timely manner. It identifies
the team members, stakeholders, and the work involved in the
communication process. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p206,
"Communications Management Plan"

statement:
A number of risks have been raised on your project. You perform
qualitative analysis to get a subjective risk rating, but your project
customer is not satisfied. They would like you to perform additional
qualitative risk analysis, beyond impact and likelihood. What will you
analyse next?
option1: Simulation, Sensitivity analysis, Decision tree analysis.
option2: Cost of Failure, Cost of Appraisal, Cost of Prevention.
option3: Executive needs, Political needs, Promotion needs.
option4: Urgency, Proximity and Strategic impact.
explanation:
As part of qualitative risk analysis, other risk parameters you might
measure risk by include: Urgency, Proximity, Dormancy, Manageability,
Controllability, Detectability, Strategic Impact and Propinquity.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p247, \"Assessment of Other
Risk Parameters"

statement:
One of the risks on your project is significantly large, and could
seriously impact your company if it ever came about. It has captured the
attention of high ranking executives, who have each proposed a response
and thinks theirs is the best. You decide to solve the argument with a
Decision Tree. What will you do next?
option1:
Rate the risks for their probability and impact to get a risk rating for
each.
option2:
Note each of the options on a vertical bar chart for their impact
sensitivity on cost.
option3:
Note the various response options with their cost impact, and their
probability of occurrence.
option4:
Perform a simulation of each of the responses against multiple criteria
to test their impact.
explanation:
A Decision Tree shows various options with their benefit or cost, and
various (often uncontrollable) outcomes with their probability of
occurrence. This allows you to choose the path with the least cost and
the least probability of impact. Process Groups: A Practice Guide
(2022), p264, "Decision Tree Analysis"

statement:
You have recently become a project manager for a large organisation and
you are not sure what to expect. Your colleague tells you that the
organisation is large enough to have a substantial project management
office, and it is a directing PMO. What will you do next?
option1:
Collaborate with the other project managers in the PMO to find a project
that suits your skillset.
option2: Get started on a business case in your work area to initiate a project.
option3: Await to be assigned to a project by the PMO.
option4:
Review the project management frameworks, templates and tools mandated
for use by the PMO.
explanation:
There are three types of Project Management Offices. Supportive, with
templates and guidelines, Controlling, with adherence to frameworks and
templates, and Directive, with a high degree of control where they
directly managing the projects and assign project managers who report to
the PMO. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p48, \"Project
Management Office\"

statement:
The project customer approaches you as they are worried that the project
is not delivering fast enough. You review the project schedule and cost
so you can see how the project is tracking. The Actual Cost is
\$648,000. The Earned Value is \$601,000. The Planned Value is
\$629,000. What will you tell them?
option1: The project is ahead of schedule and over budget.
option2: The project is ahead of schedule and under budget.
option3: The project is behind schedule and over budget.
option4: The project is behind schedule and under budget.
explanation: This question is about earned value analysis.

statement:
The people and resources involved in your project are currently costing
a large amount of money, and your project sponsor comments about it
frequently. You check how the project is tracking. Planned Value is
\$840,000. Earned Value is \$795,000. Actual Cost is \$755,000. What
will you do next?
option1:
Utilise the project contingency reserves to bring the project schedule
back on track.
option2:
Raise a change request to reduce the project scope and bring the cost
back on track.
option3: Reduce the project resources to bring the project cost back on track.
option4: Add more resources to bring the project schedule back on track.
explanation:
Schedule Variance = EV -- PV = -\$45,000 (behind schedule) Cost Variance
= EV -- AC = \$40,000 (a surplus, therefore under budget) Despite the
Sponsor's comments, the project is behind schedule but under budget, so
we can add more resources and cost to the project to bring the schedule
back on track. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p312, "Variance
Analysis"

statement:
Team members from parts of the organisation have been assigned to your
project at an agreed level of 50% availability. As the project
progresses, you notice they are being pulled back into their normal
roles. You also notice that your project is falling behind schedule.
What will you do next?
option1:
Talk with the project steering committee, and arrange for more resources
to assist your project.
option2:
Talk with functional managers. Negotiate clear and written assignments
with reliable scheduling priorities.
option3:
Raise a risk to project delivery and assign it to the functional
managers to mitigate.
option4:
Raise an issue in the issue log, and raise it at the next project
working group.
explanation:
This question refers to interpersonal and team skills and negotiation.
The best way to approach a problem is directly, clearly and
collaboratively with the functional managers involved. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p258, \"Conflict Management."

statement:
You have been planning a project with the manager of a functional area
who needs to increase their profit by 57% in the next three years. You
are helping him put together a business case. He has come up with 18
different project ideas to reach the goal but will take approximately
seven years. The manager pushes you to find a way to do them in three.
What will you do next?
option1:
Work with the manager to prioritise the ideas based on impact and cost,
and note those ranked ideas in the business case.
option2: Advise the manager that good things take time to deliver.
option3:
Add the appropriate amount of cost for extra resources to your project
plan to get the work done.
option4:
Set a meeting with the functional area experts and ask them to pick the
top ideas for delivery.
explanation:
The project business case includes "Analysis of the situation," which
includes decision criteria by which the various courses of action may be
assessed, output in ways such as required, desired and optional. Process
Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p27, "Benefits Management and Business
Documents".

statement:
Your project charter has been approved and your stakeholder analysis
reveals multiple impacted highly paid executives with the power to
influence your career. As you approach some of these stakeholders, they
raise their concerns about the project, how it is progressing, and what
it involves. What will you do next?
option1: Perform an Impact over Influence analysis on all your stakeholders.
option2:
Create your project management plan and review its detail regularly with
the impacted or influential stakeholders.
option3:
Circulate the signed project charter to anyone who needs convincing that
the project is needed.
option4:
Reduce your communication with most stakeholders -- they can't resist
what they don't know about.
explanation:
Developing the project management plan involves creating a comprehensive
document that defines the basis of all project work and how the work
will be performed. Reviewing this with stakeholders regularly will
assist with their engagement. This step also comes after the Project
Charter and identifying Stakeholders in PMBOK Knowledge Mapping. Process
Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p78, "Develop Project Management Plan"

statement:
You have just created your project management plan and are reviewing it
with your stakeholders. It includes the subsidiary plans of scope
management, schedule, cost, resources and quality. Some of your
stakeholders raise concerns that they cannot see what needs to be
baselined, or how to measure the project performance and changes. What
will you do next?
option1:
Advise your stakeholders the information they need is in the subsidiary
plans.
option2:
Meet as often as needed with your project stakeholders to increase their
engagement and acceptance of your project.
option3:
Show them the project budget timeline with costs projected on a
schedule.
option4:
Create the configuration management plan, change management plan and
performance measurement baseline with your team, and include them in
your project plan.
explanation:
The Configuration Management Plan (what needs to be baselined), Change
Management Plan (how to process changes) and the Performance Measurement
Baseline (an integrated scope-schedule-cost plan to measure against) are
all additional parts of your Project Management Plan. Process Groups: A
Practice Guide (2022), p207, "Configuration Management Plan."

statement:
Part of your project work needs to be outsourced to a supplier. You are
asked to ensure that cost for the outsourced portion of the project does
not exceed \$1.2 million, and if the work comes in under cost and ahead
of schedule, you may provide additional money to the supplier. What sort
of contract will you work with your procurement office to prepare?
option1: Create a Firm Fixed Price contract for the seller.
option2: Create a Fixed Price Incentive Fee contract for the seller.
option3: Create a Cost Plus Fixed Fee contract for the seller.
option4: Create a Cost Plus Award Fee contract for the seller.
explanation:
Fixed Price Incentive Fee contracts include financial incentives tied to
achieving agreed-upon metrics, and a price ceiling is set where all
costs above the price ceiling are the responsibility of the seller. Cost
Plus Award Fee is based on a broad range of subjective factors instead.
Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p330, "Fixed price Incentive
Fee"

statement:
You take the completed scope to your project sponsor for them to accept.
The Project Sponsor advises she cannot accept the deliverables as she
doesn't know the outcomes of testing, and whether the scope met her
requirements or needs further changes. What will you do next?
option1:
Set a meeting with the Project Sponsor directly to address their
concerns.
option2:
Review the Requirements Traceability Matrix and show the Sponsor how
each requirement is met by project scope.
option3:
Review the Quality Report together with your testing leader, and note
which items may need change requests.
option4:
Review the Change Requests for the project, to show the Sponsor whether
any corrective actions needed to be made.
explanation:
The Quality Report includes corrective actions recommendations
(including rework, defect/bugs repair, 100% inspection, and more); and
the summary of findings from the Control Quality process -- perfect to
review to show the sponsor the state of deliverables before they accept
them. This report may also be the basis for change requests. Process
Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p224, "Quality Reports"

statement:
Multiple unforeseen events have happened that have set your project
behind schedule, and in trying to bring it back on track you have spent
a considerable amount of money. Now the project sponsor has threatened
to stop the project unless they can see how much the project will cost
them by the time it is finished. What will you do next?
option1:
Perform Reserve Analysis on the remaining contingency and management
reserves.
option2:
Create a project Estimate at Completion and compare it to the Budget at
Completion.
option3:
Create the To Complete Performance Index with your project team to meet
your project goal.
option4:
Review the Cost Performance Index (CPI) to see how the project cost is
tracking.
explanation:
As the project progresses, the project team develops a forecast for the
estimate at completion (EAC) that may differ from the budget at
completion (BAC) based on the project performance. If it becomes obvious
that the BAC is no longer viable, the project manager should consider
the forecasted EAC. Process Groups: A Practice Guide (2022), p208, "Cost
Forecasts"

statement:
Your project plan includes an estimate of how long the project will take
(via a project schedule), how much it will cost and the resources that
will be required. The Project Sponsor believes that the project can be
done with around 34% fewer resources, which will save them some money
and keep their area within budget. What will you do next?
option1:
Dismiss the Sponsor's comments as uneducated, and proceed with your plan
as it is.
option2:
Reduce the resources in your project plan so the project can meet your
Sponsor's expectations.
option3:
Create a basis of estimates with your project team and review this with
your Sponsor to agree on a way forward.
option4:
Raise a risk to your project delivery in the Risk Register, if the plan
goes ahead with fewer resources.
explanation:
The basis of estimates should provide a clear and complete understanding
of how the estimates are made, including assumptions, constraints, and
ranges. This will help the Project Sponsor understand the reasoning
behind it and agree on the best way forward. Process Groups: A Practice
Guide (2022), p202, "Basis of Estimates\"
